topology: Single node. Two single-node pods (cass-buggy=cassandra:4.1.4, cass-fixed=cassandra:4.1.5) deployed in parallel in namespace repro-19475 on the existing kind-kind cluster. Plain pod template, no cassandra.yaml block (virtual tables are on by default in 4.1, not config-gated). No ring needed.
trigger: Query the system_views.settings virtual table for an array-typed config setting: SELECT name, value FROM system_views.settings WHERE name = 'data_file_directories'. (name is the partition key, no ALLOW FILTERING needed.) On 4.1.4 the value renders as the Java array's default Object.toString() instead of the directory list.
verbatim_signature: data_file_directories | [Ljava.lang.String;@4cb1c088
evidence: BUGGY 4.1.4: `SELECT name, value FROM system_views.settings WHERE name='data_file_directories'` returned value `[Ljava.lang.String;@4cb1c088` (JVM array identity-hash toString; the hex hash varies per run, the stable token is the `[Ljava.lang.String;@` prefix). FIXED 4.1.5 (identical query): returned the real directory list `[/opt/cassandra/data/data]`. Array-specificity confirmed: scalar setting commitlog_directory rendered correctly on BOTH images (`/opt/cassandra/data/commitlog`), so the defect is specific to array-typed settings, matching the Jira title "incorrectly handle array types" and the body exactly. Release versions verified via system.local (4.1.4 and 4.1.5). Full commands + raw cqlsh tables captured in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-19475.md.
control: A/B control with fixed image cassandra:4.1.5 (buggy patch 4 + 1 = 5, <= 4.1 ceiling of 11). Ran the identical query on cass-fixed: data_file_directories returned `[/opt/cassandra/data/data]` (correct directory list) instead of the array toString garbage. Same scalar commitlog_directory value on both. This is a true fixed-version A/B discriminator, not within-version reasoning.
notes: Clean, decisive reproduction matching the Jira body verbatim. The Docker image data path (/opt/cassandra/data/data) differs from the reporter's dev path (/home/fermat/...); irrelevant — the load-bearing fact is array-class toString vs. a real directory string. Client-visible wrong query result. ~3 deploy-and-test within budget (1 deploy cycle, parallel pods). Bug is in the virtual table rendering of String[] config values; fixed across 4.1.5, 5.0, 6.0 lines per fixVersions.
tag_correction: none — classifier hints all correct: topology=1node (single-node bug, confirmed), confidence=H (reproduced with verbatim signature + A/B control), trigger string matched the observed buggy output exactly (`[Ljava.lang.String;@...`).
tooling_findings: None. The provided single-node pod template, image-tag-existence check, Ready/CQL polling, and A/B control flow all worked as documented for this bug. No SREGym tooling issues observed.

trigger: Single-node Cassandra 4.1.4: CREATE TABLE tbl (id int, ts timestamp, primary key (id)); INSERT a pre-Gregorian timestamp value -13767019200000; then SELECT tounixtimestamp(ts), ts, tojson(ts) FROM tbl WHERE id=1; and SELECT JSON * FROM tbl WHERE id=1;. The bare ts column and the JSON-encoded (tojson / SELECT JSON) renderings of the SAME stored long disagree by 10 days.
evidence:
On buggy cassandra:4.1.4 (pod cass-buggy, ns repro-19566, keyspace repro19566), the single row from `SELECT tounixtimestamp(ts), ts, tojson(ts) FROM repro19566.tbl WHERE id=1` rendered:
  system.tounixtimestamp(ts) | ts                              | system.tojson(ts)
             -13767019200000 | 1533-09-28 12:00:00.000000+0000 | "1533-09-18 12:00:00.000Z"
The identical stored long (-13767019200000) renders as 1533-09-28 via the bare `ts` column but 1533-09-18 via `tojson(ts)` — a client-visible 10-day Julian/proleptic-Gregorian offset within ONE row. `SELECT JSON *` is a second witness: {"id": 1, "ts": "1533-09-18 12:00:00.000Z"}. This is captured from my own pod run, not pasted from Jira.
control: A/B control with fixed cassandra:4.1.5 (pod cass-fixed, identical workload, same ns/keyspace). release_version confirmed 4.1.5. `SELECT tounixtimestamp(ts), ts, tojson(ts)` returned: -13767019200000 | 1533-09-28 12:00:00.000000+0000 | "1533-09-28 12:00:00.000Z" and `SELECT JSON *` returned {"id": 1, "ts": "1533-09-28 12:00:00.000Z"}. All three renderings AGREE on 1533-09-28 — the bug is gone on the fixed image. 4.1.5 is a valid fixed control: 4.1.4 patch+1 = 5, within the 4.1 ceiling of 11, and 4.1.5 is listed in fixVersions.
notes: Deterministic single-node CQL JSON-serialization bug; no ring needed. Both pods deployed in parallel to save a cycle. Buggy 4.1.4 took ~130s to accept cqlsh; the initial combined keyspace+table+insert one-liner hit a client request timeout due to post-startup schema propagation, so I split keyspace creation from table/insert with --request-timeout=30 and a short sleep — purely a startup-timing workaround, not a defect. Fix landed in 4.1.5 / 4.0.13 / 5.0-rc1 / 6.0. The fix makes the JSON path emit the proleptic-Gregorian date matching the bare ts rendering.
tag_correction: none — classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger = INSERT -13767019200000 then ts vs toJson(ts)/SELECT JSON dates differ 1533-09-28 vs 1533-09-18) matches the Jira body exactly.
tooling_findings: none

trigger: CREATE TABLE timetest(id int PRIMARY KEY, enddate timestamp, startdate timestamp); then INSERT/SELECT using a CQL timestamp literal that has a SPACE before the timezone offset, e.g. '2022-03-20 12:48:56 +0530'. On 4.0.3 the CQL timestamp parser rejects it with InvalidRequest code=2200 'Unable to parse a date/time'. The same literal with no space ('2022-03-20 12:48:56+0530') is accepted.
verbatim_signature: InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Unable to parse a date/time from '2022-03-20 12:48:56 +0530'"
evidence: Two single-node pods in ns repro-17467: cass=cassandra:4.0.3 (buggy), cass-fixed=cassandra:4.0.4 (fixed control); release_version verified on each. Schema (id int PK, enddate/startdate timestamp) created on both. BUGGY 4.0.3: (T1) INSERT with space-before-tz '2022-03-20 12:48:56 +0530' -> exit 2, InvalidRequest code=2200 'Unable to parse a date/time from ...'. (T2) IDENTICAL INSERT but no space '...56+0530' -> exit 0 (row 2 lands). (T3) SELECT ... enddate='2022-03-20 12:48:56 +0530' ALLOW FILTERING -> same code=2200 parse error. Post-state on buggy: only row 2 present, row 1 (space) absent. FIXED 4.0.4 A/B with the IDENTICAL space-before-tz statements: INSERT -> exit 0, SELECT with space -> returns the row, post-state shows row 1 present. CQL passed via direct argv `cqlsh -e "..."` (no extra sh -c) so the space survived to the server.
control: Two controls, both confirm a client-visible version regression. (1) Within-version on 4.0.3: literals differ only by one space; with-space FAILS (code=2200), no-space SUCCEEDS — isolates the trigger to the space, rules out a malformed-literal/setup artifact. (2) Cross-version A/B: the exact same with-space INSERT and SELECT that throw on cassandra:4.0.3 both succeed on cassandra:4.0.4 (fixVersion; 4 <= 4.0-line ceiling 20). Row lands and is queryable on 4.0.4, absent on 4.0.3.
notes: Clean high-confidence reproduction, no thrash; ~3 test cycles within budget. One incidental hiccup: the first combined CREATE KEYSPACE+TABLE in a single -e statement hit OperationTimedOut right after node startup (schema agreement slow on a fresh node); splitting into separate statements with --request-timeout=60 succeeded immediately and the table was confirmed via DESCRIBE before testing — this did not affect the bug result. The +0530 offset is correctly applied on accepted rows (12:48:56 IST stored as 07:18:56 UTC), confirming the engine handles the tz fine once parsed; the defect is purely in lexing the space before the offset. Jira body also states 3.11 accepts both forms (regression introduced in 4.0 line, fixed in 4.0.4).
tag_correction: none
tooling_findings: None. Used the existing kind-kind cluster and Cassandra pods only; no SREGym tooling exercised beyond kubectl. (Minor environment note, not a tooling defect: schema creation can transiently time out on a just-started node; bumping cqlsh --request-timeout resolves it.)

trigger: On a float column e holding 5.2, SELECT CAST(e AS decimal) returns spurious extra digits (5.199999809265137) because the cast routes float->double->decimal, carrying the double-widening artifacts; CAST(e AS text) correctly returns 5.2.
verbatim_signature: cast(e as decimal) = 5.199999809265137
evidence: Single-node cassandra:4.1.2 pod (cass-412) in ns repro-18647, keyspace repro18647, table tbl(p int PK, e float), INSERT (1, 5.2). BUGGY 4.1.2 raw cqlsh: SELECT CAST(e AS decimal) FROM tbl WHERE p=1 -> 5.199999809265137 (matches Jira body char-for-char); SELECT CAST(e AS text) -> 5.2 (correct contrast, matches body); raw SELECT e -> 5.2; SELECT CAST(e AS double) -> 5.2 (cqlsh display-rounds the double, only the decimal path exposes the full widened value). release_version confirmed 4.1.2.
control: A/B control on cassandra:4.1.3 (fixed; fixVersions lists 4.1.3, ceiling .11) in same namespace (pod cass-413), identical DDL + INSERT (1, 5.2) + identical query. FIXED 4.1.3 raw cqlsh: SELECT CAST(e AS decimal) FROM tbl WHERE p=1 -> 5.2 (clean, no spurious digits); CAST(e AS text) -> 5.2. release_version confirmed 4.1.3. Only the version differs between buggy and control, isolating the regression to the float->decimal cast path.
notes: Deterministic, client-visible wrong query result; no timing/concurrency. Pure CQL/Semantics bug, single node sufficient (hint topology=1node correct). Setup nuance: an initial multi-statement cqlsh -e DDL against the freshly-started node returned OperationTimedOut (slow schema agreement on a just-booted node); re-running each DDL statement separately with cqlsh --request-timeout=60 succeeded. This is generic fresh-node schema-agreement latency, not the bug and not a tooling defect. Both pods deployed together in one namespace to collapse the A/B into a single deploy cycle.
tag_correction: none — hint trigger (CAST float->decimal adds spurious digits via float->double->decimal path) and topology (1node) both match the Jira body exactly.
tooling_findings: none — no SREGym tooling defect observed. (Minor operational note only: DDL against a freshly-started Cassandra node may need cqlsh --request-timeout bumped to avoid OperationTimedOut during schema agreement; this is generic Cassandra startup latency, not a harness bug.)

topology: 1node. Ran two single-node Cassandra pods in parallel in one namespace (repro-15814): cass-buggy=cassandra:3.11.7 and cass-fixed=cassandra:3.11.8. The bug itself is purely single-node CQL/Interpreter (no ring needed); the fixed pod is only the A/B control.
trigger: On 3.11.7, CREATE TABLE with a frozen<list<int>> clustering column WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (version DESC) creates fine, but INSERT of a list literal into that clustering column fails. The identical INSERT succeeds on a table with no clustering order (default ASC), so the trigger is specifically the DESC clustering-order clause on the frozen-list clustering column.
verbatim_signature: InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Invalid list literal for version of type frozen<list<int>>"
evidence: Buggy 3.11.7 (pod cass-buggy), keyspace repro_15814. (1) software_asc table (PRIMARY KEY(name,version), no clustering order): INSERT INTO software_asc(name,version) VALUES('t1',[2,10,30,40,50]) -> exit=0; SELECT returns row [2,10,30,40,50]. (2) software_desc table (same columns) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (version DESC): DESCRIBE confirms the DESC clause on version frozen<list<int>>. INSERT of the IDENTICAL literal [2,10,30,40,50] -> exit=2 with: <stdin>:1:InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Invalid list literal for version of type frozen<list<int>>". This matches the Jira body verbatim. Only variable between (1) and (2) is the CLUSTERING ORDER BY ... DESC clause, isolating the defect to DESC handling of a frozen-list clustering column (not frozen-list inserts in general). Note: first DDL attempt hit cqlsh default ~10s OperationTimedOut on a fresh pod; rerun with cqlsh --request-timeout=60 created schema cleanly (client timeout, not the bug).
control: A/B against fixed image cassandra:3.11.8 (fixVersions list 3.11.8; 8 <= 3.11 ceiling 19). On pod cass-fixed, identical workload: CREATE software_desc WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (version DESC), then INSERT VALUES('t1',[2,10,30,40,50]) -> exit=0 (SUCCEEDS; failed on 3.11.7). A second insert [1,2,3] + SELECT returns rows in valid descending clustering order ([2,10,30,40,50] before [1,2,3]), satisfying the reporter's goal of getting the latest version. Second control leg: within-version on 3.11.7 itself, the ASC table accepted the same literal while the DESC table rejected it, pinning the fault to the DESC clause.
notes: Deterministic CQL/Interpreter validation bug; reproduced on first real attempt (one transient cqlsh client-timeout on cold-start DDL, resolved with --request-timeout=60, unrelated to the bug). Three data points, one variable: 3.11.7 ASC insert OK; 3.11.7 DESC insert FAIL (Invalid list literal); 3.11.8 DESC insert OK. Keyspace named repro_15814 (underscores) since hyphens are illegal in unquoted keyspace names.
tag_correction: none — classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger: frozen<list> DESC clustering + INSERT list literal -> Invalid list literal error) matched the Jira body exactly.
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling defect observed. Operational note only: on a freshly started Cassandra pod, schema DDL via cqlsh can exceed the default ~10s client request timeout and return OperationTimedOut (client-side, distinct from the bug); using `cqlsh --request-timeout=60` for DDL avoids it.

trigger: In cqlsh on a single node: create a table with a uuid column, then INSERT ... JSON with an empty string "" (not null) for that uuid field (Cassandra accepts and stores a zero-length value), then run SELECT JSON of that row. The server throws java.lang.NullPointerException while serializing the empty UUID to JSON; the client sees ServerError: java.lang.NullPointerException.
verbatim_signature: java.lang.NullPointerException: null at org.apache.cassandra.db.marshal.AbstractType.toJSONString(AbstractType.java:156) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.7.jar:3.11.7]
evidence: BUGGY 3.11.7 (pod cass): the heredoc workload's SELECT JSON (line 5) returned client error "<stdin>:5:ServerError: java.lang.NullPointerException" (exit code 2). A plain SELECT confirmed the empty-string another_id was stored as a blank/zero-length value alongside id=1111...1111 and subject=dante. The server log (kubectl logs -n repro-15896 cass) emitted, VERBATIM: "ERROR [Native-Transport-Requests-1] 2026-06-12 02:36:05,274 ErrorMessage.java:384 - Unexpected exception during request / java.lang.NullPointerException: null / at org.apache.cassandra.db.marshal.AbstractType.toJSONString(AbstractType.java:156) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.7.jar:3.11.7] / at org.apache.cassandra.cql3.selection.Selection.rowToJson(Selection.java:343) / at Selection$ResultSetBuilder.getOutputRow(Selection.java:494) / ...build(477) / SelectStatement.process(794) ...". This matches the Jira ground-truth stack exactly (ticket showed 3.11.6 at the same line 156; my run shows 3.11.7). CONTROL 3.11.8 (pod cass-ctl), identical heredoc workload: INSERT still accepted, SELECT JSON returned clean JSON {"id": "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111", "another_id": ""} with exit code 0, and the control server log contained 0 NullPointerException occurrences. A/B confirms the 3.11.8 fix.
control: A/B control run with cassandra:3.11.8 (buggy 3.11.7 + 1 = 8, <= 3.11 ceiling 19). Identical cqlsh workload on pod cass-ctl. Result: the empty-string-UUID INSERT is still accepted, and SELECT JSON returns clean JSON ({"id": "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111", "another_id": ""}) with exit code 0; zero NullPointerExceptions in the 3.11.8 server log. The fix chose the "accept empty string and serialize cleanly" path rather than rejecting at INSERT. This confirms the bug is specific to the buggy 3.11.7 image.
notes: Reproduced entirely via cqlsh (no Java driver needed): the driver's prepared-statement INSERT ... JSON path and the literal INSERT ... JSON '{...}' path share the same parser, so the empty-string "" for a uuid column produces a zero-length ByteBuffer that is accepted at INSERT but NPEs in AbstractType.toJSONString on SELECT JSON. No WHERE clause needed — selecting the row at all triggers JSON serialization of the empty UUID. The classifier hint (1node, H, "INSERT JSON empty-string uuid -> SELECT JSON -> NPE") was exactly correct. Both pods were deployed up front in the same namespace and tested in ~3 exec cycles, well within budget.
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger INSERT...JSON empty-string for a uuid field then SELECT JSON -> server NPE) matched the Jira body's mechanism exactly. The only minor deviation: the ticket's stack shows 3.11.6 at AbstractType.java:156; my buggy 3.11.7 run emitted the identical line number 156.
tooling_findings: None. The provided single-node pod template, the heredoc-via-stdin approach for the JSON quoting, and the standard kubectl wait/exec/logs flow all worked without issue on the existing kind cluster.

topology: 1node (single Cassandra pod). The bug is a pure CQL-parser defect; no ring needed. Hint topology=1node confirmed by the Jira body.
trigger: Issuing a CQL statement that uses a bare (unquoted) capital letter 'P' where the grammar expects an identifier (IDENT), e.g. `CREATE TABLE P (k INT PRIMARY KEY)`. The lexer mis-treats 'P' as the leading token of an ISO-8601 Duration literal (P1Y2M...), so it cannot be consumed as an identifier. Lowercase 'p' is unaffected.
verbatim_signature: SyntaxException: line 1:13 no viable alternative at input 'P' (CREATE TABLE [P]...)
evidence: Single pod cassandra:4.1.1 (release_version=4.1.1, imageID sha256:7772bea6...) in ns repro-17919, isolated keyspace repro17919 (RF=1). REPRODUCER (verbatim from this run): `kubectl exec -n repro-17919 cass -- cqlsh -k repro17919 -e "CREATE TABLE P (k INT PRIMARY KEY)"` -> `<stdin>:1:SyntaxException: line 1:13 no viable alternative at input 'P' (CREATE TABLE [P]...)` ; exit code 2. Matches the Jira body exactly (same line:col 1:13 and identical message). WITHIN-VERSION CONTROL (same 4.1.1 binary): `CREATE TABLE p (k INT PRIMARY KEY)` -> exit 0, table 'p' present in system_schema.tables -> isolates case (P vs p) as the trigger. A/B IMAGE CONTROL: same `CREATE TABLE P` on cassandra:4.1.2 (release_version=4.1.2, imageID sha256:f100c4c5...) -> exit 0, unquoted ident folds to lowercase 'p' and table is created -> fix resolves the defect. The original Accord LET-syntax reproducer in the body was intentionally skipped (Accord/LET not present in 4.1.1); the body's version-agnostic CREATE TABLE reproducer was used.
control: Two controls, both airtight. (1) Within-version, identical 4.1.1 binary: `CREATE TABLE p (k INT PRIMARY KEY)` succeeds (exit 0, table created) while capital-P fails -> the only difference is letter case. (2) A/B fixed image: the identical `CREATE TABLE P (k INT PRIMARY KEY)` statement succeeds on cassandra:4.1.2 (exit 0, table created) -> the fix (released in 4.1.2 per Jira fixVersions) eliminates the parse error.
notes: Bug is a server-side CQL parser error surfaced through cqlsh; high-confidence, deterministic, fires every time. Used `cqlsh -k repro17919` (not a `USE;` prefix) so the failing statement stays at line 1 col 13 to line up with the body. Verbatim signature captured from the actual 4.1.1 pod, not transcribed from Jira. Note on phrasing: I showed P-fails / p-succeeds (matching the body's own evidence bar); I did not exhaustively test other capital letters (e.g. X), which would be gold-plating — so the trigger is best stated as "capital P specifically" since P is the ISO-8601 Duration designator.
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint was fully confirmed by the Jira body on every axis: reproducer (CREATE TABLE P), error string (no viable alternative at input 'P'), topology (1node), and confidence (H). The hint's "(capital P parsed as Duration)" is an accurate root-cause gloss matching the body, not a discrepancy from the actual output.
tooling_findings: None. SREGym tooling was not exercised in this manual reproduction. (The minor grep `-v` quirk in my initial readiness-check command was my own ad-hoc shell logic, not an SREGym tool defect, so it is not logged as a finding.)

trigger: CREATE TABLE with a frozen<map<text,int>> clustering column declared WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (c DESC), then run element selection `SELECT c['testing'] FROM t` (no data, no WHERE needed). The error fires at statement preparation.
verbatim_signature: InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Invalid element selection: c is of type frozen<map<text, int>> is not a collection"
evidence: Two single-node pods deployed in parallel in ns repro-17913: cass-411 (cassandra:4.1.1, confirmed via SHOW VERSION) and cass-412 (cassandra:4.1.2). On 4.1.1: CREATE KEYSPACE repro17913_ks (RF=1) and CREATE TABLE t (k int, c frozen<map<text,int>>, v int, PRIMARY KEY(k,c)) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (c DESC) both succeed; then `SELECT c['testing'] FROM repro17913_ks.t` throws (exit 2): `InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Invalid element selection: c is of type frozen<map<text, int>> is not a collection"` — verbatim match to the Jira body. Slice variant `SELECT c['a'..'z']` also throws `Invalid slice selection: c of type frozen<map<text, int>> is not a collection`, confirming the body's note that WithSliceSelection is also affected. Mechanism isolated: the SAME frozen-map clustering column with DEFAULT (ASC) order (table t_asc, no ReversedType wrapping) returns (0 rows) with no error on the buggy node — proving the fault is the unstripped ReversedType wrapper.
control: A/B fixed-image control on cassandra:4.1.2 (fix landed in 4.1.2 per fixVersions; within 4.1 ceiling of 11). IDENTICAL workload (same keyspace/table DDL + same `SELECT c['testing'] FROM repro17913_ks.t`) returns 0 rows with exit code 0 and NO exception. Exception (4.1.1) vs clean (0 rows) (4.1.2) on the same query is the fix. Control did not trip ALLOW FILTERING, so the no-WHERE minimal reproducer was correct.
notes: Pure CQL-semantics bug in Selectable.WithElementSelection / WithSliceSelection: the collection-type check does not unwrap ReversedType, so a frozen collection clustering column with CLUSTERING ORDER DESC is wrongly rejected as "not a collection". No data, no WHERE clause, single node sufficient — error is thrown at prepare time. Affects both element selection (c['key']) and range/slice selection (c['a'..'z']). Introduced by CASSANDRA-7396 in 4.0; fixed in 4.0.10/4.1.2/5.0. Classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger) was fully accurate.
tag_correction: none — the Jira body matches the classifier hint exactly (frozen collection clustering column with CLUSTERING ORDER DESC + element selection -> InvalidRequestException 'is not a collection', single node, high confidence).
tooling_findings: None. Official Docker Hub images cassandra:4.1.1 and cassandra:4.1.2 pulled cleanly into the kind nodes, and the skill's single-pod template worked without modification. No SREGym tooling involved (manual/hand-crafted reproduction).

trigger: CREATE TABLE t(pk,ck,v PRIMARY KEY(pk,ck)); CREATE INDEX ON t(ck); INSERT (1,2,3); DELETE WHERE pk=1 AND ck=2; UPDATE SET v=3 WHERE pk=1 AND ck=2; then SELECT WHERE ck=2 -> 0 rows (index entry never created) even though the row is live (UPDATE reuses the deleted row's LivenessInfo in CassandraIndex.updateRow). Verbatim first example from the Jira body, run in-memtable (no flush, matching the body).
verbatim_signature: SELECT * FROM repro16868.t WHERE ck=2;  ->  (0 rows)   [SAME row returns (1,2,3) via PK lookup "WHERE pk=1 AND ck=2" on the SAME buggy 4.0.0 node; on fixed 4.0.1 the indexed "WHERE ck=2" lookup returns (1,2,3)]
evidence:
Two single-node pods deployed concurrently in repro-16868: cass-buggy (cassandra:4.0.0) and cass-fixed (cassandra:4.0.1). Identical workload (keyspace repro16868, table t, CREATE INDEX ON t(ck), INSERT(1,2,3), DELETE pk=1/ck=2, UPDATE v=3 pk=1/ck=2) run on both. Discriminator (two SELECTs) to rule out timestamp-shadowing:

BUGGY 4.0.0:
  PK lookup   SELECT * FROM repro16868.t WHERE pk=1 AND ck=2;  ->  (1 rows)  pk=1 ck=2 v=3   [row LIVE]
  Index lookup SELECT * FROM repro16868.t WHERE ck=2;          ->  (0 rows)                  [BUG: index missed write]

FIXED 4.0.1 (A/B control, identical workload):
  PK lookup   SELECT * FROM repro16868.t WHERE pk=1 AND ck=2;  ->  (1 rows)  pk=1 ck=2 v=3
  Index lookup SELECT * FROM repro16868.t WHERE ck=2;          ->  (1 rows)  pk=1 ck=2 v=3   [fix creates index entry]

Because the PK lookup returns the live row on 4.0.0 while the indexed lookup returns (0 rows), the row is proven LIVE but UNINDEXED — exactly the CASSANDRA-16868 mechanism, not a DELETE/UPDATE timestamp collision (which would empty both). Version banners confirmed: buggy=Cassandra 4.0.0, fixed=Cassandra 4.0.1.
control: A/B control with the released fixed image cassandra:4.0.1 (4.0.1 is in fixVersions and <= the 4.0-line ceiling 20). Identical workload on cass-fixed: the indexed lookup SELECT * FROM repro16868.t WHERE ck=2 returns (1 rows) pk=1 ck=2 v=3, i.e. the index entry IS created and the bug does NOT manifest. Same workload, same timing (in-memtable, no flush) as the buggy node, so the only variable is the version — isolating the defect to 4.0.0.
notes: Bug is a silent wrong-result (data-correctness / missing 2i entry), not an exception — no stack trace exists by design; the signature is the (0 rows) indexed result interpreted against the live PK-path result and the 4.0.1 control. Both pods reached Ready and answered CQL; bug fired on the body's FIRST example verbatim, in-memtable, so no flush fallback was needed. Classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger) matched the Jira body precisely. The Jira body also lists two further variants (TTL-expiry-then-UPDATE, and TTL over flushed indexed data) plus the root-cause pointer to CassandraIndex.updateRow not using getPrimaryKeyIndexLiveness; I reproduced the primary deletion-then-update variant, which is sufficient.
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint (1node; CREATE INDEX on clustering/PK col + INSERT + DELETE + UPDATE same row + SELECT by indexed col -> row not found / missing index entry) matches the Jira body exactly. Single node is sufficient (pure local 2i index logic, RF=1).
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling defects observed. The cqlsh "SyntaxException: no viable alternative at input ';'" and "Cannot infer type for term '...'" errors in the first combined run were self-inflicted by my own label-marker statements (blank lines and bare string literals in the SELECT clause) inside a multi-statement -e script; they did not affect the actual reproducer statements. I re-ran the discriminator as separate clean exec calls and got pristine output. Not a tooling bug.

topology: Two single-node Cassandra pods in namespace repro-17918 on the existing kind-kind cluster: cass-buggy (cassandra:4.1.1, release_version confirmed 4.1.1) and cass-ctrl (cassandra:4.1.2, release_version confirmed 4.1.2). Keyspace repro17918 (SimpleStrategy RF=1), UDT repro17918.t with fields "token" and "desc".
trigger: CREATE TYPE repro17918.t ("token" text, "desc" text) with reserved-keyword field names, then DESCRIBE TYPE repro17918.t. On 4.1.1 the field names are emitted unquoted; feeding that output back as a CREATE TYPE fails to re-import.
verbatim_signature: DESCRIBE TYPE on 4.1.1 emits "    token text," (unquoted reserved keyword); fixed 4.1.2 emits "    \"token\" text,". Downstream impact when re-importing the 4.1.1 output: SyntaxException: line 2:4 no viable alternative at input 'token'.
evidence:
Reproducer (identical on both pods): CREATE KEYSPACE repro17918 ...RF=1; CREATE TYPE repro17918.t ("token" text, "desc" text); DESCRIBE TYPE repro17918.t.

BUGGY 4.1.1 DESCRIBE TYPE output (unquoted reserved keywords):
  CREATE TYPE repro17918.t (
      token text,
      desc text
  );

CONTROL 4.1.2 DESCRIBE TYPE output (correctly quoted):
  CREATE TYPE repro17918.t (
      "token" text,
      "desc" text
  );

Round-trip re-import (proves Jira-stated impact "schema cannot be imported"): feeding the BUGGY 4.1.1 unquoted output back as CREATE TYPE repro17918.t2 fails verbatim:
  <stdin>:1:SyntaxException: line 2:4 no viable alternative at input 'token' (CREATE TYPE repro17918.t2 (    [token]...)
  command terminated with exit code 2
Feeding the CONTROL 4.1.2 quoted output back succeeds (exit code 0) and the type is created.

Full log with raw commands/outputs at /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-17918.md.
control: A/B control with the fixed image cassandra:4.1.2 (buggy 4.1.1 + 1; <= 4.1.11 ceiling). Identical workload: DESCRIBE TYPE on 4.1.2 emits the reserved-keyword field names QUOTED ("token" text / "desc" text), and re-importing that output as a new CREATE TYPE succeeds (exit 0). DESCRIBE is a server-side CQL statement in 4.0+ (DescribeStatement / SchemaElement.toCqlString, per the Jira), so the 4.1.1->4.1.2 output difference is a genuine server-side fix, not a cqlsh client-rendering artifact.
notes: Clean reproduction with both the direct wrong-output signature and the downstream re-import failure captured verbatim, plus a valid fixed-image A/B control. The bug is client-visible (wrong DESCRIBE output that is not re-importable), so disposition is reproduced, not not-observable. Both pods shared namespace repro-17918 and were deployed in parallel; no Pending/resource starvation observed. Pre-existing namespaces (cass-*, k8ssandra-operator, cert-manager, repro-smoke, other parallel-session repro-*) were not touched.
tag_correction: none — classifier hints matched the Jira body exactly: topology=1node is correct (DESCRIBE is a coordinator-local CQL operation), and the trigger (CREATE TYPE with reserved-keyword fields "token"/"desc" + DESCRIBE TYPE -> unquoted output that fails on re-import) matches the body's reproducer verbatim.
tooling_findings: none — RECORD-ONLY, no SREGym/repo/Cassandra files edited. Minor (expected per procedure): the single-node pod template has no readinessProbe, so kubectl wait condition=Ready fired ~100s before the native transport accepted CQL on the 4.1.1 pod; the task's separate cqlsh polling step compensates, so this is not a defect.

topology: Single Cassandra pod per version in kind (context kind-kind), namespace repro-16898: cass-buggy=cassandra:4.0.1 and cass-fixed=cassandra:4.0.2. Identical config on both (MV enabled pre-start). Keyspace repro16898ks, SimpleStrategy RF=1. Schema-definition logic is node-local, so 1-node is the correct topology.
trigger: Create a base table with a clustering column ordered DESC (WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ck DESC)), then CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW on it WITHOUT specifying WITH CLUSTERING ORDER, keeping ck as a clustering column in the MV (PRIMARY KEY (pk, v, ck)). On 4.0.1 the MV's inherited clustering column ck defaults to ASC instead of inheriting the base table's DESC order.
verbatim_signature: DESCRIBE MATERIALIZED VIEW repro16898ks.mv on 4.0.1 prints: WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (v ASC, ck ASC)  -- and system_schema.columns shows row: ck | clustering | 1 | asc  (base table ck is DESC, so ck should be DESC in the MV)
evidence: Base table on both versions: system_schema.columns shows ck | clustering | 0 | desc. BUGGY 4.0.1: MV mv -> ck | clustering | 1 | asc (DID NOT inherit base DESC); DESCRIBE: WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (v ASC, ck ASC). CREATE succeeded with only the benign "Materialized views are experimental" warning. The misbehaviour is on the inherited clustering column ck; v stays ASC because v was a regular column in the base (no order to inherit), which holds the control variable stable. Raw outputs captured in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-16898.md. Exact release_version confirmed 4.0.1 (buggy) and 4.0.2 (fixed) via system.local. Both observable via operator-facing DESCRIBE and via system_schema.columns ground truth.
control: A/B with the fixed image cassandra:4.0.2 (fixVersions=4.0.2; 4.0.2 <= 4.0 ceiling .20), IDENTICAL CQL and IDENTICAL config (enable_materialized_views: true set the same way on both). Result on 4.0.2: MV mv -> ck | clustering | 1 | desc (correctly inherits base DESC); DESCRIBE: WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (v ASC, ck DESC). Same CREATE, correct inheritance -> the only variable that flips ck from asc to desc is the Cassandra version, confirming the 4.0 regression and its 4.0.2 fix.
notes: Clean reproduction with a direct A/B against the fixed image. Discriminator: for the inherited clustering column ck (base = DESC), the MV's clustering_order is asc on 4.0.1 (buggy) vs desc on 4.0.2 (fixed). This is the primary, operator-visible symptom of the bug (clustering order in the created MV), exposed via both DESCRIBE MATERIALIZED VIEW and system_schema.columns; clustering order also governs physical row sort so downstream MV query ordering would be wrong too. Budget: ~2 deploy cycles (first deploy had MV disabled; redeployed with config gate opened).
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger about CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW with no CLUSTERING ORDER over a DESC base defaulting to ASC) matched the Jira body and the observed behaviour exactly.
tooling_findings: Cassandra 4.0.x ships enable_materialized_views: false by default (renamed materialized_views_enabled in 4.1+). Any automated SREGym pipeline that deploys 4.0.x and runs an MV reproducer will hit "Materialized views are disabled. Enable in cassandra.yaml to use." unless it sets that flag pre-start (e.g. sed the cassandra.yaml before docker-entrypoint). This is a Cassandra default, not an SREGym defect, but it is a real gotcha for the entire MV bug class on 4.0. RECORD-ONLY: not fixed.

topology: 1node (single Cassandra pod in kind, namespace repro-17623)
trigger: Prepared/bound-parameter INSERT of an UNSORTED frozen<map<text,text>> as a clustering key, then SELECT the map projection c['key']. DDL (verbatim from body): CREATE TABLE ks17623.t (k text, c frozen<map<text,text>>, PRIMARY KEY (k, c)). Bound parameter: OrderedDict([('z','second_value'),('a','first_value')]) (z before a). Then SELECT k, c['a'] FROM t WHERE k='key'. Must be a PREPARED statement bound parameter (path Maps.Value#fromSerialized) — a CQL literal is sorted by the parser and does NOT reproduce.
verbatim_signature: c['a'] = None
evidence: A/B run with IDENTICAL script+wire bytes on cassandra:4.0.4 (cass404) vs cassandra:4.0.5 (cass405). The driver serialized the OrderedDict unsorted on BOTH pods — wire bytes identical: 00000002 000000017a 0000000c 7365636f6e645f76616c7565 0000000161 0000000b 66697273745f76616c7565 ('z'=hex 7a before 'a'=hex 61), proving the unsorted trigger was staged identically. BUGGY 4.0.4: SELECT_FULL returned {'z':'second_value','a':'first_value'} (unsorted); c['a'] = None (BUG — body says it should be 'first_value'); c['z'] = 'second_value'. CONTROL 4.0.5: SELECT_FULL returned {'a':'first_value','z':'second_value'} (sorted by fix); c['a'] = 'first_value' (correct). Physical on-disk proof via nodetool flush + /opt/cassandra/tools/bin/sstabledump: BUGGY 4.0.4 stored clustering UNSORTED: "clustering" : [ {"z": "second_value", "a": "first_value"} ]; CONTROL 4.0.5 stored sorted: "clustering" : [ {"a": "first_value", "z": "second_value"} ]. This covers both symptoms in the body: SELECT projection returns NULL, and invalid (unsorted) data persisted to disk.
control: cassandra:4.0.5 (buggy patch 4 + 1 = 5 <= 4.0-line ceiling 20; 4.0.5 is in fixVersions). Ran the IDENTICAL reproducer script (same bundled Python driver, same OrderedDict, same prepared INSERT + same SELECT) on pod cass405. Result: did NOT misbehave — c['a'] = 'first_value' and on-disk clustering correctly sorted. The fix sorts the map in Maps.Value#fromSerialized.
notes: Refinement of the hint (not a contradiction): the bug fires ONLY via a prepared/bound-parameter map, not a CQL literal — literals go through Maps.Literal/DelayedValue.bind which builds a sorted TreeMap, while the bound path Maps.Value#fromSerialized was missing the sort. Used the Cassandra-pod-bundled Python driver (cassandra-driver-internal-only-3.25.0.zip) connecting to 127.0.0.1 inside each pod via kubectl exec — symmetric, no port-forward. One transient cqlsh OperationTimedOut on first DDL on 4.0.4 (schema-agreement), succeeded on retry with --request-timeout=30. The body also mentions a second branch (full-map value in WHERE clause -> 0 rows); I cleanly reproduced the c['a']->NULL branch only, which is sufficient. Reproducer script saved at /tmp/repro17623.py.
tag_correction: none — hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger="INSERT unsorted frozen<map> via bound parameter + SELECT c['key'] -> NULL") matched reality. The literal-vs-bound nuance refined but did not contradict the hint.
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling defect. Image-level note only: sstabledump is not on PATH in the cassandra:4.0.x image; it lives at /opt/cassandra/tools/bin/sstabledump (must be invoked by full path).

trigger: On cassandra:4.0.3 with enable_materialized_views=true: CREATE TABLE with default_time_to_live=60, CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW (no TTL), then DESCRIBE MATERIALIZED VIEW. The emitted CQL wrongly includes "AND default_time_to_live = 0", which MVs forbid; replaying that output to re-create the view fails.
verbatim_signature: AND default_time_to_live = 0
evidence: DESCRIBE MATERIALIZED VIEW repro17266_ks.test_view on buggy 4.0.3 emits a CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW block containing line 15 "    AND default_time_to_live = 0" (grep confirmed PRESENT). Replaying that exact DESCRIBE output after DROP fails: "/tmp/buggy_replay.cql:24:InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message=\"Cannot set default_time_to_live for a materialized view. Data in a materialized view always expire at the same time than the corresponding data in the parent table.\"" (REPLAY RC=2). This matches the Jira body verbatim. The defect is in server-side DESCRIBE output generation (TableParams.appendCqlTo / TableMetadata.appendTableOptions); the replay error is the shared validation guardrail confirming the emitted CQL is invalid.
control: A/B control with cassandra:4.0.4 (fixed; 4.0.3 patch+1, <= ceiling 20), identical workload and config in same namespace (pod cass-fixed). On 4.0.4 the same DESCRIBE MATERIALIZED VIEW output OMITS default_time_to_live entirely (grep -> no match). Round-trip on 4.0.4's own output: DROP (RC=0), replay captured CREATE (REPLAY RC=0, only the experimental-MV warning), and the view was recreated (system_schema.views shows test_view, 1 row). The discriminator is presence vs absence of the default_time_to_live line in DESCRIBE output; the shared "Cannot set default_time_to_live..." validation exists in both versions, so the round-trip is done per-version on each version's own output. Buggy fails, fixed succeeds.
notes: Bug is purely server-side CQL generation in DESCRIBE; single node, no ring/gossip needed. Reached the path by enabling MVs (disabled by default in 4.0). Keyspace renamed to repro17266_ks for isolation; reproducer otherwise identical to Jira body. First MV DDL on a fresh 4.0 node caused a cqlsh OperationTimedOut even though the schema applied (verified via system_schema.tables/views and idempotent retry with --request-timeout). Jira summary also mentions DESCRIBE KEYSPACE; only the MV variant was tested but it is the same code path so KEYSPACE emits the same bad line (namespace already torn down, not re-tested). Confidence H.
tag_correction: none — hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger: CREATE MV no TTL + DESCRIBE MV emits default_time_to_live=0 -> re-CREATE fails) matched the Jira body exactly.
tooling_findings: In cassandra:4.0.x, materialized views are gated off by default (cassandra.yaml: enable_materialized_views: false); CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW otherwise fails with "Materialized views are disabled. Enable in cassandra.yaml to use." Any MV-dependent reproducer must set enable_materialized_views: true (done here by appending to cassandra.yaml in the pod command before docker-entrypoint.sh). Also, the first MV-related DDL on a freshly-started 4.0 node can exceed the default cqlsh client timeout (OperationTimedOut) although the DDL applies server-side; use --request-timeout and/or verify via system_schema. No repo/tooling/Cassandra files were modified (record-only).

topology: 1node (single Cassandra pod in kind, namespace repro-19637). CQL/Semantics bug; no ring needed.
trigger: LWT IF-conditions on a collection column handle null/empty inconsistently. (a) On a NULL column, same condition UPDATE...IF l < [1,2]: frozen list returns [applied]=False, non-frozen returns [applied]=True. (b) On a non-frozen list column, IF l >= null throws InvalidRequest while IF l >= [] (empty list, stored as null) returns [applied]=True.
verbatim_signature: 4.1.5 non-frozen list, same column: `UPDATE ... IF l >= null` -> InvalidRequest code=2200 message="Invalid comparison with null for operator ">="" ; `UPDATE ... IF l >= []` -> [applied]=True. (On 4.1.6 the `>= []` case throws InvalidRequest "Invalid comparison with an empty list for operator >=".)
evidence:
Single pod cassandra:4.1.5 (cqlsh: Cassandra 4.1.5, CQL 3.4.6) in repro-19637, keyspace repro19637. Tables tf(k int PK, l frozen<list<int>>) and tn(k int PK, l list<int>), each with a row k=0 having l NULL; tn also k=1 NULL.
CASE (a) HEADLINE, same condition `IF l < [1,2]` on NULL column:
  FROZEN tf k=0  -> [applied]=False, l=null
  NON-FROZEN tn k=0 -> [applied]=True
  (matches Jira body's frozen [false,null] vs non-frozen [true].)
CASE (b) NON-FROZEN tn k=1 (l NULL):
  IF l >= null -> InvalidRequest code=2200 "Invalid comparison with null for operator >=" (exit 2)
  IF l >= []   -> [applied]=True
A/B CONTROL on cassandra:4.1.6 (FIXED), identical workload, verified-clean null rows via SELECT:
  (a) FROZEN IF l < [1,2] -> False/null (unchanged); NON-FROZEN IF l < [1,2] -> False/null (FIXED, now matches frozen).
  (b) IF l >= null -> InvalidRequest (unchanged); IF l >= [] -> InvalidRequest code=2200 "Invalid comparison with an empty list for operator >=" (FIXED: empty list now rejected like null).
Both documented inconsistencies reproduce verbatim on 4.1.5 and are resolved on 4.1.6, matching the fix description (treat empty multi-cell collection as null; reject null/empty for operators other than =/!=).
control: A/B with fixed image cassandra:4.1.6 (fixVersions include 4.1.6; 4.1 ceiling=11 so image exists). Identical CQL run on cass-416. Buggy->fixed deltas: non-frozen `IF l < [1,2]` on null changed True -> False (now consistent with frozen); non-frozen `IF l >= []` changed [applied]=True -> InvalidRequest "Invalid comparison with an empty list for operator >=" (now rejected like `>= null`). The unchanged cases (frozen `< [1,2]`, `>= null`) confirm only the buggy paths changed. Clean, apples-to-apples discrimination.
notes: Disposition rests on cases (a) and (b), which match the Jira body verbatim and have clean A/B controls. Case (c) (INSERT empty list, then DELETE...IF l = []) did NOT reproduce the body's shorthand `{false,null}` — on 4.1.5 it returned [applied]=True (a stored empty list reads back as null so `= []` applied). I am NOT relying on (c); it is recorded only for completeness. The frozen-column `IF l < [1,2]` returning False on a null column is itself arguable, but the Jira's core complaint is the frozen-vs-non-frozen inconsistency, which is exactly what the fix changes. Two pods (cass-415 buggy, cass-416 fixed) deployed in parallel in repro-19637 to save wall-clock.
tag_correction: none — the classifier hints were accurate: topology=1node correct (single pod sufficed), confidence=H justified (reproduced verbatim), and the trigger hint (UPDATE IF l < [1,2] frozen [false,null] vs non-frozen [true]; IF colA >= [] true while >= null errors) matched the Jira body exactly.
tooling_findings: Not an SREGym tooling issue, but an environmental note: CREATE/DROP TABLE DDL intermittently returned `OperationTimedOut` (Client request timeout, 127.0.0.1:9042) on the memory-constrained pod (MAX_HEAP_SIZE=1024M, limit 2560Mi). The timed-out ops were no-ops in context; I re-verified table/row state via SELECT before trusting any control output, so results are sound. Also: multi-statement `cqlsh -e "...;"` blobs emit a cosmetic trailing `SyntaxException: no viable alternative at input ';'` for the empty final statement — all real statements still execute. Record-only; nothing fixed.

trigger: Single Cassandra 3.11.11 pod (RF=1, UDFs enabled via cassandra.yaml). Create a table, a UDF with a mixed-case quoted name "Double", and a materialized view whose WHERE clause references it: WHERE k < ks."Double"(2). The MV's stored WHERE clause is persisted/regenerated WITHOUT the quotes (as ks.Double(2)). An INSERT into the base table triggers MV maintenance, which re-parses that WHERE clause; the unquoted Double is lowercased to ks.double, which does not exist, so the local mutation fails. Fires on the FIRST insert after MV creation AND after an in-place node restart (the body's documented scenario; restart preserved via emptyDir at /var/lib/cassandra + kill 1).
verbatim_signature: org.apache.cassandra.exceptions.InvalidRequestException: Unknown function repro16836.double called
evidence: Buggy cassandra:3.11.11: DESCRIBE KEYSPACE shows the function created as repro16836."Double" (quoted) but the MV persisted WHERE as "WHERE k < repro16836.Double(2)" (UNQUOTED) — the root cause. INSERT INTO repro16836.t(k,v) VALUES(3,1) returns to cqlsh: WriteFailure: Error from server: code=1500 [Replica(s) failed to execute write] message="Operation failed - received 0 responses and 1 failures" info={'failures':1,'received_responses':0,'required_responses':1,'consistency':'ONE'}. Server system.log (captured both pre-restart AND post-restart on the fresh restartCount=1 container) shows: InvalidRequestException: Unknown function repro16836.double called, with the exact frame chain from the Jira body: FunctionCall$Raw.prepare(FunctionCall.java:139) -> SingleColumnRelation.toTerm(:122) -> SingleColumnRelation.newSliceRestriction(:209) -> Relation.toRestriction(:146) -> StatementRestrictions.<init>(:182) -> SelectStatement$RawStatement.prepare(:969) -> View.getSelectStatement(:184) -> View.getReadQuery(:199) -> TableViews.updatedViews(:361) -> ViewManager.updatesAffectView(:83) -> Keyspace.applyInternal(:495). Failure occurs both on the first insert after MV creation (restartCount=0) and after an in-place restart (restartCount=1, on-disk schema reloaded from emptyDir).
control: A/B control on cassandra:3.11.12 (a listed fixVersion; 3.11.11+1 <= ceiling 19), pod cassctl in the same namespace, IDENTICAL .cql workload. Discriminating check: DESCRIBE KEYSPACE on 3.11.12 emits the MV WHERE WITH quotes preserved — "WHERE k < repro16836.\"Double\"(2) AND k IS NOT NULL AND v IS NOT NULL" — versus the buggy 3.11.11 which emits it unquoted (repro16836.Double(2)). On the control both INSERTs (1,1) and (3,1) succeed (exit 0, no WriteFailure); SELECT * FROM t returns 2 rows and SELECT * FROM mv returns 2 rows, proving MV maintenance works. The function and MV genuinely exist on the control (DESCRIBE confirms), so the insert success is meaningful and not a disabled-UDF no-op. The quoted-vs-unquoted DESCRIBE diff between 3.11.11 and 3.11.12 is the single strongest artifact.
notes: Bug is exactly as described: incorrect quoting of a UDF in a materialized view's WHERE clause. The MV's stored read query loses the case-sensitive quoting of the function name, so when the view read query is (re)parsed during MV maintenance, the lowercased name (double) is not found and the write fails locally. Two practical deployment gotchas (not bugs): (1) UDFs require enable_user_defined_functions: true in cassandra.yaml (off by default in 3.11) or CREATE FUNCTION fails outright; (2) the on-disk schema must survive the restart, so I mounted an emptyDir at /var/lib/cassandra and restarted in place with `kill 1` rather than deleting the pod. Reproduced on both the first-insert path and the documented post-restart path. Image cold-pull of cassandra:3.11.11 onto the kind node was the slowest step (~3-4 min).
tag_correction: Topology hint 1node is CORRECT (error is "Failed to apply mutation locally"; RF is irrelevant — body's RF=3 reduced to RF=1 with no change in behavior). Trigger hint partially refined: the hint/body say a "node restart" is required, but the restart is NOT strictly necessary — the WriteFailure/Unknown-function error also fires on the FIRST insert immediately after MV creation (same prepare-time mechanism). The restart still reproduces, matching the body. So the documented restart is sufficient but the bug surfaces earlier than documented. Confidence H confirmed.
tooling_findings: None. The provided single-node pod template needed two well-known additions to reach this bug (UDF enablement flag in cassandra.yaml via a command override, and an emptyDir at /var/lib/cassandra to persist schema across an in-place restart); these are documented in the skill's config-gated / restart guidance and are not tooling defects. The advisor flagged a subtle pitfall worth noting for future restart-based reproductions: `kill 1` resets /var/log/cassandra (the emptyDir only covers /var/lib/cassandra), so the post-restart server log must be re-grepped after the restart to capture the post-restart exception instance rather than the pre-restart one — handled here.

topology: 1node (single Cassandra pod cassandra:5.0.1 in kind, namespace repro-19889)
trigger: CREATE TABLE with ck frozen<list<int>> as clustering key AND WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ck DESC), then CREATE INDEX ON tbl(FULL(ck)). The DESC ordering wraps ck in ReverseType, which the FULL()-index validation fails to unwrap, so a valid index on a frozen collection is wrongly rejected.
verbatim_signature: <stdin>:1:InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="full() indexes can only be created on frozen collections"
evidence:
Single-node deterministic schema-validation bug, reproduced on cassandra:5.0.1 in kind ns repro-19889, keyspace repro19889_ks.

BUGGY 5.0.1: CREATE TABLE repro19889_ks.tbl (pk int, ck frozen<list<int>>, value int, PRIMARY KEY(pk, ck)) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ck DESC) -> ok. Then CREATE INDEX ON repro19889_ks.tbl(FULL(ck)) -> FAILS:
  <stdin>:1:InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="full() indexes can only be created on frozen collections"  (cqlsh exit code 2).
Note: cqlsh surfaces the client-side form of the exception (code=2200 InvalidRequest), not the Jira's server-side Java stack frames (AlterSchemaStatement.ire / CreateIndexStatement.validateIndexTarget) — same InvalidRequestException, different wire representation; the message string is identical.

STRENGTHENER on the SAME buggy node: identical table WITHOUT CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ck DESC) -> CREATE INDEX ON tbl2(FULL(ck)) SUCCEEDS (RC=0, index tbl2_ck_idx kind=COMPOSITES options={'target':'full(ck)'}). This isolates ReverseType (the DESC ordering) as the trigger, matching the Jira root cause.

A/B CONTROL fixed 5.0.2 (cass-fixed pod), IDENTICAL workload incl. CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ck DESC): CREATE INDEX ON tbl(FULL(ck)) SUCCEEDS (RC=0, index tbl_ck_idx created). buggy-patch+1 = 5.0.2 is both <= ceiling 8 and the exact fixVersion where the fix landed, so 5.0.1-rejects / 5.0.2-accepts straddles the fix boundary.

Versions confirmed via SHOW VERSION: cass = Cassandra 5.0.1, cass-fixed = Cassandra 5.0.2.
control: A/B with fixed image cassandra:5.0.2 (fixVersions list 5.0.2; <= 5.0 ceiling of 8). Ran the IDENTICAL DDL (table with CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ck DESC) + CREATE INDEX FULL(ck)) on the 5.0.2 pod: it SUCCEEDED (RC=0, index tbl_ck_idx created), whereas 5.0.1 rejects it. Additional within-node control: on the buggy 5.0.1 pod, the same FULL(ck) index on the same frozen<list<int>> column but WITHOUT DESC ordering succeeds, isolating ReverseType as the trigger.
notes: Bug is fully client-visible and deterministic — no data, no ring, no timing needed. Classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger) matches the Jira body exactly. Both buggy (5.0.1) and control (5.0.2) images had to be pulled from Docker Hub (not cached in kind nodes); deployed both pods in parallel so pulls ran concurrently. Both reached Ready and cqlsh-answering. Reproduced on first deploy-and-test cycle.
tag_correction: none — classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger = frozen<list<int>> reversed clustering key + CREATE INDEX FULL(ck) -> InvalidRequestException, valid index rejected) matches the Jira body precisely.
tooling_findings: none — no SREGym tooling issues encountered. Pod templates and procedure from the skill worked as written.

topology: 1node (single Cassandra pod in kind, namespace repro-19749)
trigger: On an auth-enabled node (PasswordAuthenticator + CassandraAuthorizer + CassandraRoleManager), run `ALTER ROLE IF EXISTS <missing_role> WITH SUPERUSER = true;` (or the equivalent `ALTER USER IF EXISTS <missing_user> SUPERUSER;`) for a role that does not exist, then SELECT it from system_auth.roles.
verbatim_signature: repro19749_missing |      null |         True |      null |        null
evidence: Buggy 4.1.5: baseline SELECT for role 'repro19749_missing' in system_auth.roles returned 0 rows. After `ALTER ROLE IF EXISTS repro19749_missing WITH SUPERUSER = true;` (accepted with no error), the SELECT returned 1 row: `repro19749_missing | null | True | null | null`. The ALTER USER form (`ALTER USER IF EXISTS repro19749_missinguser SUPERUSER;`) likewise produced a phantom row `repro19749_missinguser | null | True | null`. A statement that must be a no-op under IF EXISTS instead CREATED the role (as a superuser). Auth was verified genuinely enforced beforehand: config grep showed `authenticator: PasswordAuthenticator` / `authorizer: CassandraAuthorizer`; bare cqlsh was rejected with `AuthenticationFailed('Remote end requires authentication')` and a wrong password with `[Bad credentials]`, so the system_auth.roles results are trustworthy. Matches the Jira body almost character-for-character.
control: A/B control on fixed image cassandra:4.1.6 (fixVersions list 4.1.6; 4.1 ceiling=11) with IDENTICAL workload and identical auth config. Baseline 0 rows; after the same `ALTER ROLE IF EXISTS repro19749_missing WITH SUPERUSER = true;` the SELECT returned 0 rows (correct no-op). The ALTER USER IF EXISTS form also returned 0 rows. Sanity: the non-IF-EXISTS form `ALTER ROLE repro19749_noexist WITH SUPERUSER = true;` on the fixed node correctly errored `InvalidRequest ... code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Role repro19749_noexist doesn't exist"`, confirming the role truly does not exist. Identical inputs, opposite outputs (1 row buggy vs 0 rows fixed).
notes: Single-node reproduction; bug lives in the global system_auth.roles table so isolation is the dedicated namespace/pod rather than a user keyspace (no user keyspace created). On 4.1.x cassandra.yaml uses the FLAT string form for authenticator/authorizer (the map form in the Jira description is 5.0+ syntax); auth enabled via sed in the pod command before docker-entrypoint. Both pods (cass-buggy 4.1.5, cass-fixed 4.1.6) deployed in parallel in repro-19749 to get the A/B in one cycle. The created role has can_login=null and no salted_hash, so it cannot directly log in; the undisputed defect is that an IF-EXISTS-guarded ALTER created a non-existent role at all (component: Legacy/CQL).
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger "ALTER USER IF EXISTS missing_user SUPERUSER -> creates the role (superuser)") matches the Jira body exactly. The only addition is that the body requires the auth stack (PasswordAuthenticator/CassandraAuthorizer/CassandraRoleManager) enabled, which the hint did not mention but the body specifies.
tooling_findings: RECORD ONLY (not fixed): The SREGym single-node pod template's readiness/wait line uses bare `cqlsh -e "SELECT now() FROM system.local"`, which fails under PasswordAuthenticator. Auth-gated bugs require `cqlsh -u cassandra -p cassandra` plus a retry loop, because the default superuser is created ~10-90s after the CQL port binds (superuser_setup_delay). Handled in-session here. The template also does not provide a hook to flip cassandra.yaml to PasswordAuthenticator/CassandraAuthorizer; I used sed in the pod command, which worked.

topology: Single Cassandra pod per version in namespace repro-19880 on the existing kind cluster (context kind-kind). Two single-node pods deployed in parallel for the A/B control: cass-500 = cassandra:5.0.0 (buggy), cass-501 = cassandra:5.0.1 (fixed). No ring/gossip needed — pure CQL transport path. Keyspace repro19880ks, table t(id int PRIMARY KEY, m map<text,text>).
trigger: Prepare an INSERT into a table with a map<text,text> column, bind the row with the map column left UNSET (cassandra.query.UNSET_VALUE), then session.execute(bound, trace=True). Tracing is enabled per-request via the driver flag. On the server, ExecuteMessage.traceQuery formats the bound values for the trace string and calls CQL3Type$Collection.toCQLLiteral -> CollectionSerializer.readCollectionSize, which reads a 4-byte element count out of the empty UNSET sentinel buffer -> IndexOutOfBoundsException. Used the cqlsh-bundled Python driver inside the pod (no pip), with sys.path built exactly as bin/cqlsh.py does (<zip>/cassandra-driver-3.29.0 plus pure_sasl/wcwidth/geomet zips).
verbatim_signature: at org.apache.cassandra.transport.messages.ExecuteMessage.traceQuery(ExecuteMessage.java:227) -> at org.apache.cassandra.cql3.CQL3Type$Collection.toCQLLiteral(CQL3Type.java:221) -> at org.apache.cassandra.serializers.CollectionSerializer.readCollectionSize(CollectionSerializer.java:74), raising java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException: null
evidence: BUGGY cass-500 (cassandra:5.0.0): identical script -> client got NoHostAvailable with "<Error from server: code=0000 [Server error] message=\"java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException\">". Server /opt/cassandra/logs/system.log VERBATIM stack matches the Jira report's frame chain exactly: java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException: null at Buffer.checkIndex / HeapByteBuffer.getInt / ByteBufferUtil.toInt(476) / ByteBufferAccessor.toInt(208,42) / CollectionSerializer.readCollectionSize(74) / CQL3Type$Collection.toCQLLiteral(221) / ExecuteMessage.traceQuery(227) / ExecuteMessage.execute(159) / ... / Dispatcher$RequestProcessor.run(307) / SEPWorker.run(143). CONTROL cass-501 (cassandra:5.0.1, the fix): SAME script -> "RESULT: execution SUCCEEDED (no exception)" and "TRACE OK, request_type=Execute CQL3 prepared query, duration=0:00:00.006904"; server log grep -c IndexOutOfBoundsException = 0, no ERROR lines. Deterministic, fired on first execute.
control: A/B fixed-image control run. 5.0 line ceiling is 8 so cassandra:5.0.1 (buggy patch+1) exists and was used. Identical reproducer script run inside cass-501 (5.0.1) succeeded: the UNSET-map bound statement executed with trace=True with no exception, the query trace was fetched cleanly (request_type "Execute CQL3 prepared query"), and zero IndexOutOfBoundsException / ERROR lines appeared in its server log. Confirms the bug is specific to 5.0.0 and resolved in 5.0.1.
notes: Clean deterministic single-node CQL transport-path bug. Two refinements vs my first attempt: (1) cqlsh cannot express UNSET (it is a protocol sentinel only reachable via a prepared-statement bind), so I used the bundled Python driver imported from the on-disk zips the way bin/cqlsh.py constructs sys.path (no pip, no network). (2) First DDL at the driver's default 10s timeout hit OperationTimedOut on a freshly started node; raising execute timeout to 30s fixed it (not bug-related). Line numbers in the buggy stack (readCollectionSize:74, toCQLLiteral:221, traceQuery:227) differ from the Jira paste (147/222/223) only because the report's paste came from a slightly different build; the discriminating frame chain is identical. Full log at /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-19880.md.
tag_correction: Topology and trigger hints were correct (1node, prepared-statement UNSET map + tracing -> IndexOutOfBoundsException). One clarification worth recording: "tracing enabled" is a PER-REQUEST driver flag (session.execute(bound, trace=True)), NOT a server config or setraceprobability — cassandra.yaml was untouched. Confidence H was justified.
tooling_findings: The cassandra:5.0.x image has no `unzip` and no system-installed cassandra-driver. Reproducing UNSET (unreachable from cqlsh) requires the bundled driver; importing it directly from the on-disk zips placed on sys.path (the bin/cqlsh.py approach: <zip>/cassandra-driver-<ver> plus pure_sasl/wcwidth/geomet zips) works without network or pip. Worth baking this helper into the skill for any UNSET / prepared-statement-binding Cassandra reproducer. No SREGym repo files were edited.

trigger: CREATE TABLE (pk int, val vector<float,2>, PK(pk)) + CREATE CUSTOM INDEX USING StorageAttachedIndex; INSERT pk0=[1,2], pk1=[1,3]; nodetool flush (old vectors -> SSTable A); INSERT pk0=[1,4] (overwrite to worse position); then SELECT pk FROM t ORDER BY val ann of [1.0,2.0] LIMIT 1. Stale exact-match [1,2] for pk0 remains in SSTable A's SAI index and is returned without re-scoring against pk0's current value.
verbatim_signature: SELECT pk FROM t ORDER BY val ann of [1.0, 2.0] LIMIT 1 -> pk=0 on 5.0.6 (WRONG; correct=pk=1 since pk1=[1,3] dist 1 < pk0=[1,4] dist 2; cassandra:5.0.7 returns pk=1)
evidence: Single-node cass-buggy=5.0.6, cass-fixed=5.0.7 in ns repro-20086, keyspace ks_clean20086, SAI index on val vector<float,2>. After INSERT pk0=[1,2],pk1=[1,3]; flush; INSERT pk0=[1,4] (current stored values: pk0=[1,4], pk1=[1,3]). BUGGY 5.0.6: `SELECT pk ... ORDER BY val ann of [1.0,2.0] LIMIT 1` returns pk=0 in BOTH pre-flush (State A) and post-flush (State B). Correct nearest to [1,2] is pk1 (dist 1) over pk0 (dist 2); Jira dtest asserts row(1). FIXED 5.0.7 with IDENTICAL workload+data returns pk=1 in both states. LIMIT 1 is the sole discriminating query (LIMIT 2 returns (1,0) on both versions, as the Jira test also expects for fixed code, so it does not discriminate and was not claimed as evidence).
control: A/B fixed-image control run. cassandra:5.0.7 (fixVersions=5.0.7,6.0; 7<=5.0 ceiling 8) ran the byte-identical CQL+flush sequence in the same namespace. Result: ORDER BY val ann of [1.0,2.0] LIMIT 1 -> pk=1 (CORRECT) in both pre-flush and post-flush states, versus pk=0 (WRONG) on 5.0.6. Same data, same query, deterministic with 2 vectors (trivial HNSW graph), so the differential is not ANN approximation jitter but the fixed defect.
notes: Mechanism exactly as Jira test testUpdateVectorToWorseAndBetterPositions block 1 predicts: SAI vector search pulls top-k in index score order and materializes/returns rows without ensuring the materialized vector's score matches the current value, so an overwritten vector's stale exact-match score in a prior SSTable index ranks it above the true nearest neighbor. Only block 1 of the test was needed for the signature; later blocks exercise updatedKeys dedup (more moving parts, not required). Reproduced directly via plain CQL — SAI is GA in 5.0, no config gate, no in-JVM dtest harness needed (hint confidence H was correct).
tag_correction: none — classifier hint accurate (topology=1node confirmed; trigger matched body exactly). Minor refinement: the discriminating signature is the LIMIT 1 case (returns pk=0 instead of pk=1); the LIMIT 2 result looks superficially ordered (1,0) on both versions and is NOT evidence.
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling was exercised in this manual reproduction (plain kubectl + cqlsh + nodetool). Unrelated to SREGym: cqlsh multi-statement `-e` with a leading newline (heredoc) emits `SyntaxException: line 1:0 no viable alternative at input ';'` for the empty first statement yet still executes the remaining statements, producing confusing partial output on first attempt; re-ran with one statement per `cqlsh -e` for clean evidence.

trigger: CREATE FUNCTION with quoted name containing a [class-like bracket] (e.g. "admin_created_udf[org.apache.cassandra.db.marshal.LongType]") then GRANT EXECUTE and LIST EXECUTE OF user. Body expects the resource to show the wrong input type (long instead of int), or a ConfigurationException "Unable to find abstract-type class" when the bracket content is not a valid class. Requires PasswordAuthenticator + CassandraAuthorizer + user_defined_functions_enabled=true.
verbatim_signature: InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Function name 'admin_created_udf[org.apache.cassandra.db.marshal.LongType]' is invalid"
evidence: The candidate buggy image cassandra:4.1.0 ALREADY CONTAINS the CASSANDRA-17848 fix, so the body's reproducer cannot fire. Confirmed three independent ways. (A) In-image /opt/cassandra/CHANGES.txt lists "* Fix incorrect resource name in LIST PERMISSION output (CASSANDRA-17848)" on line 6, between the "4.1.0" header (line 1) and "4.1-rc1" (line 10) => the fix shipped in 4.1.0 GA (Jira fixVersions says 4.1.1, which diverges from the actual GA cut). (B) Source at git tag cassandra-4.1.0 already defines the fix's new validation in FunctionName.java: DISALLOWED_CHARACTERS = {'/','[',']'} and isNameValid(), called by CreateFunctionStatement which throws InvalidRequestException. (C) Empirical on the running pod: release_version=4.1.0; the exact Jira CREATE FUNCTION command is rejected with code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Function name 'admin_created_udf[org.apache.cassandra.db.marshal.LongType]' is invalid"; the invalid-class variant "weird[not.a.real.Class]" is rejected the same way; even minimal "f[x]" is rejected. Because the only path to the buggy LIST-PERMISSION resource-name parser is a bracket-named UDF, and the fix's validation blocks creating one, the buggy code path is unreachable on this image.
control: Within-image control on the SAME buggy image: a normal (non-bracket) UDF behaves correctly. CREATE FUNCTION repro17848.normalfn(input int)... succeeds (DESCRIBE FUNCTIONS shows "normalfn(int)"); GRANT EXECUTE ON FUNCTION repro17848.normalfn(int) TO u1; LIST EXECUTE OF u1 returns the correct resource "<function repro17848.normalfn(int)>" with permission EXECUTE. So the LIST EXECUTE machinery and auth/authz/UDF gating all work; only the bracket-named path (which is blocked) could mis-parse. A/B on cassandra:4.1.1 was not run because it is redundant: 4.1.1 carries the identical DISALLOWED_CHARACTERS validation, so it would reject the CREATE FUNCTION exactly as 4.1.0 does; the in-image CHANGES.txt + cassandra-4.1.0 source tag already establish 4.1.0 == fixed.
notes: The candidate's premise is wrong: 4.1.0 is the FIRST FIXED release on the 4.1 line, not a buggy one. The CASSANDRA-17848 patch (commit 473656c) does two things: (1) adds name validation rejecting '/','[',']' in FunctionName.isNameValid(), wired into CreateFunctionStatement/CreateAggregateStatement, and (2) fixes FunctionResource resource-name parsing (lastIndexOf('[') and split(name,"/",3)). The validation half is what shadows the reproducer. The actual buggy range is 4.1-rc1/4.1-beta/4.1-alpha and 4.0.x < 4.0.8 (and pre-3.0.29/3.11.15 on the 3.x lines). To genuinely reproduce, a pre-fix image such as 4.0.7 or 4.1-beta1 would be required, where CREATE FUNCTION with a bracket name succeeds and LIST EXECUTE then shows the wrong type or throws ConfigurationException. The verbatim_signature recorded here is the validation-rejection error (the fix firing), NOT the bug; per the evidence bar this is why the disposition is not-reproducible rather than reproduced.
tag_correction: topology hint (1node) is correct; confidence=H is misplaced. Trigger hint matches the Jira body, but the body's reproducer does NOT fire on the given buggy version 4.1.0 because 4.1.0 already ships the fix (CHANGES.txt lists CASSANDRA-17848 under the 4.1.0 header; Jira fixVersions=4.1.1 is the divergent field). Correct buggy versions would be 4.1-rc1/beta/alpha or 4.0.x<4.0.8.
tooling_findings: The version-resolution step that selected cassandra:4.1.0 as the "buggy" image for CASSANDRA-17848 is unreliable here: it appears to have used the Jira fixVersions (4.1.1) minus one patch to pick 4.1.0, but the fix actually shipped in 4.1.0 GA itself (confirmed by the image's own /opt/cassandra/CHANGES.txt placing CASSANDRA-17848 under the 4.1.0 header, above 4.1-rc1). For tickets whose Jira fixVersion is the first GA of a new minor line (X.Y.1 listed but fix actually merged before the X.Y.0 GA cut), "fixVersion patch - 1" can land on an already-fixed image. A more robust check would grep the candidate image's CHANGES.txt for the ticket id before declaring it buggy. RECORD ONLY — not fixed.

trigger: Enable audit logging (audit_logging_options.enabled=true, default BinAuditLogger) and place a zero-byte .cq4 file (e.g. 20220928-12.cq4) in the audit_logs_dir, then start the node. Startup aborts during AuditLogManager static init when chronicle-queue's SingleChronicleQueue.cleanupStoreFilesWithNoData tries to lock/resize the empty file.
verbatim_signature:
Caused by: java.nio.channels.OverlappingFileLockException: null
	at java.base/sun.nio.ch.FileLockTable.checkList(Unknown Source)
	at net.openhft.chronicle.bytes.MappedFile.resizeRafIfTooSmall(MappedFile.java:369)
	at net.openhft.chronicle.queue.impl.single.SingleChronicleQueue.cleanupStoreFilesWithNoData(SingleChronicleQueue.java:821)
	at org.apache.cassandra.audit.BinAuditLogger.<init>(BinAuditLogger.java:55)
evidence: On cassandra:4.0.6 single pod in ns repro-17933, with audit_logging_options{enabled=true, audit_logs_dir=/var/lib/cassandra/audit} and a planted zero-byte file /var/lib/cassandra/audit/20220928-12.cq4, the node failed startup. kubectl logs showed verbatim: "ERROR [main] ... CassandraDaemon.java:911 - Exception encountered during startup" -> "java.lang.ExceptionInInitializerError" -> "Caused by: org.apache.cassandra.exceptions.ConfigurationException: Unable to create instance of IAuditLogger." (at AuditLogManager.<clinit>(AuditLogManager.java:60)) -> "Caused by: java.lang.reflect.InvocationTargetException" -> "Caused by: java.nio.channels.OverlappingFileLockException" with the full chronicle-queue frame chain (FileChannel.lock -> MappedFile.resizeRafIfTooSmall:369 -> SingleChronicleQueue.cleanupStoreFilesWithNoData:821 -> StoreAppender.<init> -> BinLog.<init> -> BinAuditLogger.<init>:55). Pod ended phase=Failed, container exitCode=3; node never reached CQL. This is a frame-for-frame match to the Jira description stack trace.
control: A/B control on the FIXED image cassandra:4.0.7 (buggy patch 6 + 1 = 7, <= 4.0 ceiling 20) with the IDENTICAL workload (same audit-logging-enabled config, same zero-byte 20220928-12.cq4 planted before start; only the image changed). Result: OverlappingFileLockException count = 0; log showed "Starting listening for CQL clients on /0.0.0.0:9042" and "Startup complete" and "state jump to NORMAL"; cqlsh "SELECT now() FROM system.local" returned a valid timeuuid (eddc2610-660a-11f1-b38b-df708973d594); pod phase=Running. The fix deletes the empty file: after successful startup the audit dir contained only metadata.cq4t (the planted 0-byte 20220928-12.cq4 was gone). Confirms the crash is specific to 4.0.6 and fixed in 4.0.7.
notes: Config-gated single-node bug. Reproducer was deployed by overriding the container command to (1) patch cassandra.yaml enabling audit logging with an explicit audit_logs_dir, (2) mkdir the audit dir and create a zero-byte 20220928-12.cq4 via ': > file', then (3) exec docker-entrypoint.sh cassandra -f. The empty .cq4 must exist before Cassandra starts (the bug fires at AuditLogManager static init, which happens during CassandraDaemon.setup after the storage/role bootstrap, ~30-60s in). Buggy log /tmp/repro-17933-buggy-full.log, control log /tmp/repro-17933-fix-full.log, evidence /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-17933.md. Manifests: /tmp/repro-17933-buggy.yaml, /tmp/repro-17933-fix.yaml.
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint (1node, confidence H, "zero-byte .cq4 in audit log dir with audit logging enabled + start node -> OverlappingFileLockException at startup") matched the Jira body exactly and reproduced as described.
tooling_findings: None. No SREGym tooling was exercised in manual mode beyond kubectl against the existing kind cluster; no issues observed.

topology: Single-node Cassandra pod in the existing kind cluster (context kind-kind), namespace repro-16902. Config-gated: cassandra.yaml patched at startup via sed before docker-entrypoint to enable authenticator: PasswordAuthenticator and authorizer: CassandraAuthorizer (the default AllowAllAuthorizer would mask the bug). Pod node-pinned to kind-worker2 (had the 4.0.1 image) to avoid ImagePullBackOff.
trigger: As superuser: CREATE ROLE parent WITH PASSWORD='x' AND LOGIN=true; GRANT CREATE ON ALL ROLES TO parent. Then in a SEPARATE authenticated cqlsh session as parent (-u parent -p x), which substitutes for the body's interactive LOGIN parent: CREATE ROLE child WITH PASSWORD='x' AND LOGIN=true; LIST ALL PERMISSIONS OF 'child'. The non-superuser parent that just created child is denied viewing child's permissions because the default DESCRIBE grant on the created role is missing in 4.0.1.
verbatim_signature: <stdin>:1:Unauthorized: Error from server: code=2100 [Unauthorized] message="You are not authorized to view child's permissions"
evidence: On buggy 4.0.1, session B (as parent) running `CREATE ROLE child...; LIST ALL PERMISSIONS OF 'child';` returned exit code 2 with the server error: <stdin>:1:Unauthorized: Error from server: code=2100 [Unauthorized] message="You are not authorized to view child's permissions". Confirmed the child role WAS successfully created (LIST ROLES as superuser shows cassandra/child/parent, 3 rows) so the failure is specifically the LIST authorization, not a CREATE failure. Auth config verified applied (grep of cassandra.yaml shows PasswordAuthenticator + CassandraAuthorizer). Version banner confirmed Cassandra 4.0.1. Full commands and raw outputs in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-16902.md.
control: A/B control on fixed image cassandra:4.0.2 (buggy-patch+1, <= 4.0 ceiling of 20), node-pinned to kind-worker3, identical auth config and identical two-session workload. Session B (as parent) running the SAME `CREATE ROLE child...; LIST ALL PERMISSIONS OF 'child';` returned RC=0 with a proper result table (header `role | resource | permissions`, `(0 rows)`) and NO Unauthorized error. Same workload/auth/topology/cluster, only the image version differs. The (0 rows) is expected (child has no explicit permission grants yet); the point proven is the query is now AUTHORIZED on 4.0.2 vs. the code=2100 Unauthorized on 4.0.1, confirming the fix auto-grants DESCRIBE to the role creator.
notes: Bug is config-gated (requires auth enabled: PasswordAuthenticator + CassandraAuthorizer); with the default AllowAllAuthorizer the LIST would silently succeed and the bug would not be observable. The body's mid-session `LOGIN parent` was realized as a separate authenticated cqlsh invocation (`-u parent -p x`) because cqlsh LOGIN is interactive-only; the error correctly originates from the parent (non-superuser) session, not the superuser. Image availability across kind nodes was uneven (4.0.1 on worker/worker2/worker3; 4.0.2 only on worker3; 4.0.3 on worker/worker3), hence node-pinning; 4.0.3 would have been an equally valid post-fix control.
tag_correction: none
tooling_findings: None. Standard kubectl/cqlsh against the existing kind cluster worked as expected. No SREGym tooling defects observed during this manual reproduction.

trigger: On cassandra:4.1.4, run nodetool import on a FLAT source directory whose parent dir names do NOT match <keyspace>/<table>. Steps: create keyspace repro19401ks + table t, insert 5 rows, nodetool flush; copy the full SSTable component set to a flat dir /tmp/staging (chown to uid 999 so the cassandra daemon can write); TRUNCATE t; run `nodetool import --copy-data repro19401ks t /tmp/staging`. The import silently does nothing (nodetool exits 0, no stdout) and the table stays empty.
verbatim_signature: INFO  [RMI TCP Connection(6)-127.0.0.1] 2026-06-12 03:00:08,251 SSTableImporter.java:173 - No new SSTables were found for repro19401ks/t
evidence: Single-node A/B with two independent controls, all matching the Jira body line-for-line. BUGGY (4.1.4, flat path /tmp/staging): server log `SSTableImporter.java:72 Loading new SSTables for repro19401ks/t: Options{srcPaths='[/tmp/staging]',...}` followed by `SSTableImporter.java:173 - No new SSTables were found for repro19401ks/t`; SELECT count(*) = 0 (nothing imported). WITHIN-VERSION CONTROL (4.1.4, nested path /tmp/nested/repro19401ks/t, IDENTICAL SSTables): log `SSTableImporter.java:177 - Loading new SSTables and building secondary indexes...` then `SSTableImporter.java:190 - Done loading load new SSTables for repro19401ks/t`; count = 5. FIXED-IMAGE A/B (4.1.5, SAME flat path /tmp/staging that failed on 4.1.4): log `:177 Loading new SSTables and building secondary indexes` then `:190 Done loading`; count = 5. So in the flat case the only variable that flips fail->pass is the version; in the buggy 4.1.4 pod the only variable is the directory name. Discriminator is directory NAMING (parent dirs must equal <keyspace>/<table>), not flat-vs-nested per se. Versions confirmed via `SELECT release_version FROM system.local` (4.1.4 buggy pod, 4.1.5 fixed pod).
control: Two controls, both crisp. (1) Within-version (cassandra:4.1.4): the IDENTICAL SSTables import successfully (count 5, 'Done loading') when placed in a dir named /tmp/nested/repro19401ks/t, proving the SSTables are valid and isolating the failure to import path/dir-name handling. (2) Fixed-image A/B (cassandra:4.1.5, within ceiling 4.1->11, fixVersions includes 4.1.5): the SAME flat-path import (/tmp/staging) that produced 'No new SSTables were found' + count 0 on 4.1.4 instead logs 'Loading new SSTables... Done loading' and yields count 5 on 4.1.5. The fix is confirmed and the bug pinned to 4.1.4.
notes: The classifier hint matched the Jira ground truth in every respect (topology=1node, flat-path nodetool import -> 'No new SSTables were found'). The bug is client/operator-visible as silent data-not-imported: nodetool import prints NO stdout and exits 0 even when it imports nothing — the only signal is the server-side INFO log line SSTableImporter.java:173. Used --copy-data to match the Jira call exactly and to leave staging intact for the control re-run. Fresh nb-format SSTables reproduce (Jira mentions old me-format snapshots, but the path-inference mechanism is format-independent, as expected). Both pods (buggy cass + fixed cass-fixed) were placed in the single namespace repro-19401 so isolation held; no pre-existing namespace (cass-*, repro-smoke, operators) was touched.
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger 'nodetool import --copy-data ks tbl /flat_path -> No new SSTables were found') matched the Jira body exactly.
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling defect. Operational caveat for any automated reproducer of this issue: `nodetool import --copy-data` requires the SOURCE directory to be writable by the cassandra server uid (999).
