SASI is gated off by default in 4.0, so enable_sasi_indexes was flipped false->true via sed on cassandra.yaml before entrypoint on BOTH pods (confirmed enable_sasi_indexes=true in each Config.java startup log). Initial cqlsh OperationTimedOut on first CREATE was just slow schema-agreement ACK on a freshly-started node; the schema actually applied (verified via DESCRIBE). The trailing-semicolon SyntaxException in heredoc output is a harmless cqlsh artifact, not a failure. Buggy node ended with 2 sstables (a benign retry of the INSERT batch); both showed SI_ in live and both absent from snapshot, so it does not weaken the result.

topology: 0 running Cassandra nodes (offline Java-API). Single k8s pod in repro-15857 on kind-worker: eclipse-temurin:11 JDK container + initContainer cassandra:3.11.6 copying /opt/cassandra/lib/* into a shared emptyDir; JDK container runs `sleep infinity` (no Cassandra boot). trigger: Compile+run a tiny Java program that calls CQLSSTableWriter.builder().forTable("CREATE TABLE ks.t (id int PRIMARY KEY, v frozen<footype>)").withType("CREATE TYPE ks.footype ( f list<frozen<tuple<text, text>>> )").using("INSERT ...").build() against the buggy 3.11.x jars. build() materializes the UDT via Types$RawBuilder$RawUDT.prepare, which round-trips the field type through CQL3Type.Raw::toString; RawTuple.toString drops the frozen<> wrapper, so the re-parsed type is a non-frozen tuple inside a collection and RawCollection.prepare throws.
verbatim_signature:
org.apache.cassandra.exceptions.InvalidRequestException: Non-frozen tuples are not allowed inside collections: list<tuple<text, text>>
	at org.apache.cassandra.cql3.CQL3Type$Raw$RawCollection.throwNestedNonFrozenError(CQL3Type.java:705)
evidence: Buggy 3.11.6 jars: program printed "BUILD_FAILED with: org.apache.cassandra.exceptions.InvalidRequestException: Non-frozen tuples are not allowed inside collections: list<tuple<text, text>>" and threw with the full stack: throwNestedNonFrozenError(CQL3Type.java:705) -> RawCollection.prepare(664) -> prepareInternal(656) -> Types$RawBuilder$RawUDT.prepare(314) -> Types$RawBuilder.build(263) -> CQLSSTableWriter$Builder.createTypes(563) -> CQLSSTableWriter$Builder.build(538), exit code 1. The input CQL explicitly used frozen<tuple<text,text>> yet the error reports the non-frozen list<tuple<text, text>>, exactly the toString frozen-wrapper drop described in the Jira. Identical program on FIXED 3.11.8 jars printed "BUILD_OK: CQLSSTableWriter constructed without exception", exit 0. Matches Jira body verbatim (same exception type, message, throwNestedNonFrozenError frame, and createTypes->build chain; line numbers differ by a few due to 3.11.6 jar vs patch context).
control: A/B with fixed image. Same Repro.java, same CQL, compiled+run against cassandra:3.11.8 jars (the fixVersion; 7+1=8 <= 3.11 ceiling 19) in pod jdk-fixed. Output: "TYPE STMT: CREATE TYPE ks.footype ( f list<frozen<tuple<text, text>>> )\nBUILD_OK: CQLSSTableWriter constructed without exception\nEXIT_CODE=0". The 3.11.8 fix makes RawTuple.toString emit frozen<>, so the round-tripped type stays frozen and build() succeeds. Buggy throws, fixed passes -> clean discriminating control.
notes: This is an offline bulk-loader (CQLSSTableWriter) Java-API bug, NOT server/cqlsh-visible: a live server prepares UDT field types directly from the AST and never serializes-and-reparses, so CREATE TYPE over cqlsh succeeds silently. No Cassandra node is booted; only the jars + a JVM are needed. Java 11 used (3.11 supports JDK 8 and 11); only a Config.setClientMode deprecation note at compile. Verbose logback DEBUG schema output had to be silenced with -Dlogback.configurationFile=<root level=OFF> to surface the exception cleanly. Reproducer program saved at /tmp/Repro.java.
tag_correction: topology hint "1node" is loose: ZERO running Cassandra nodes are required; this is an offline CQLSSTableWriter Java-API path (jars + JVM only). The classifier trigger is otherwise accurate (CQLSSTableWriter on UDT list<frozen<tuple<text,text>>> -> RawTuple.toString drops frozen wrapper -> "Non-frozen tuples are not allowed inside collections"). Buggy image substitution: candidate said 3.11.7 but it was not cached and Docker Hub was rate-limited (429); used cassandra:3.11.6 jars (same pre-fix 3.11 line, fix lands in 3.11.8) as the buggy source.
tooling_findings: Docker Hub unauthenticated pull rate limit (HTTP 429) blocked pulling cassandra:3.11.7 and any eclipse-temurin image both inside kind and on host docker; had to rely on already-cached node images. `kind load docker-image eclipse-temurin:11` failed with "content digest sha256:... not found" because kind's importer passes --all-platforms against a multi-arch manifest; workaround was `docker save eclipse-temurin:11 | docker exec --privileged -i <node> ctr --namespace=k8s.io images import --digests --snapshotter=overlayfs -` (no --all-platforms), which succeeded. The cassandra:3.11.x image is JRE-only (no javac), and its core jar is named apache-cassandra-<ver>.jar (not cassandra-all). None of this was modified/fixed (record-only).

trigger: On a single node, create a keyspace + table + an auto-named secondary index, then run `nodetool snapshot`. The snapshot's generated schema.cql emits `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` but emits the `CREATE INDEX` WITHOUT `IF NOT EXISTS`. Replaying that generated file over the existing schema then fails because the unguarded CREATE INDEX collides ("already exists").
verbatim_signature: CREATE INDEX table1_last_update_date_idx ON repro13935.table1 (last_update_date);
evidence:
Identical workload on cassandra:3.11.8 (cass-buggy) and cassandra:3.11.9 (cass-fixed), single-node pods in ns repro-13935, keyspace repro13935. Steps on both: CREATE KEYSPACE repro13935 WITH replication={'class':'SimpleStrategy','replication_factor':1}; CREATE TABLE repro13935.table1 (id text PRIMARY KEY, content text, last_update_date date, last_update_date_time timestamp); CREATE INDEX ON repro13935.table1 (last_update_date)  (auto-named table1_last_update_date_idx, verified via system_schema.indexes); nodetool snapshot -t snap1 repro13935.

PRIMARY SIGNATURE (generated schema.cql last line):
  BUGGY 3.11.8:  CREATE INDEX table1_last_update_date_idx ON repro13935.table1 (last_update_date);
  FIXED 3.11.9:  CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS table1_last_update_date_idx ON repro13935.table1 (last_update_date);
Both files have `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS repro13935.table1 (...)`; the two files are byte-identical except the CREATE INDEX line — exactly the line the fix changed (bug title: "...should have IF NOT EXISTS on its String representation").

IMPACT corroboration (replay each pod's own generated schema.cql over existing schema via cqlsh -f):
  BUGGY 3.11.8 -> InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Index table1_last_update_date_idx already exists"  (rc=2)
  FIXED 3.11.9 -> succeeds, only deprecation warnings (rc=0, idempotent)
(The "already exists" replay error is generic and fires in both versions on a bare double CREATE INDEX, so it is corroboration only; the standalone proof is the generated-file CREATE INDEX line shown above.)
control: Fixed-image A/B on cassandra:3.11.9 (fix shipped in 3.0.23 / 3.11.9 / 4.0; 3.11.9 <= 3.11 ceiling 19). Same workload, same snapshot: 3.11.9's generated schema.cql emits `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS table1_last_update_date_idx ON repro13935.table1 (last_update_date);`, and replaying that file over the existing schema succeeds idempotently (rc=0, only dclocal_read_repair_chance deprecation warnings). Clean discriminator localized to the CREATE INDEX line.
notes: Bug is in the snapshot-generated schema.cql String representation (Feature/2i Index + Legacy/CQL); single-node, no ring needed. Auto-named index reproduced the report's exact name table1_last_update_date_idx. Verified release_version 3.11.8 / 3.11.9 on the two pods before testing. cqlsh prefixed the replay error with "schema.cql:24" — that is cqlsh's own statement counter and is NOT asserted to map to a physical file line; the content discriminator stands independently. The UDT half of the same patch was intentionally not exercised (body's reproducer is index-only and the index case fully demonstrates the defect).
tag_correction: HINT topology=1node and trigger (snapshot table with secondary index + replay generated schema.cql -> CREATE INDEX without IF NOT EXISTS fails on restore) CONFIRMED correct. Nuance: the restore failure is a NON-IDEMPOTENCY failure (re-applying the generated file over an existing/partial schema), not a fresh-into-empty-keyspace crash — a single fresh replay into an empty keyspace would succeed. The core defect is the literal missing `IF NOT EXISTS` token in the snapshot's generated CREATE INDEX statement.
tooling_findings: Docker Hub returned 429 "unauthenticated pull rate limit" when the kubelet tried to pull cassandra:3.11.8 fresh (the SREGym single-node pod template has no imagePullPolicy and no pre-pull/local-cache guard). Worked around (RECORD ONLY, not fixed) by node-pinning the buggy pod to kind-worker2 (which already had cassandra:3.11.8 cached locally, image id ee9d9da863846) and setting imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent. The fixed image 3.11.9 was already cached on kind-worker3. Suggest the template add imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent (and/or pre-pull/load images into kind) to avoid rate-limit flakiness.

trigger: A single value exceeding the cell-per-partition EstimatedHistogram top bucket offset (~2.0e9, code comment says 2,395,318,855) lands in the overflow bucket; EstimatedHistogram.mean()/rawMean() then throws IllegalStateException. Server-visible: an SSTable whose cell-per-partition histogram overflowed (a partition with >~2.0 billion cells), read via StatsMetadata.getEstimatedDroppableTombstoneRatio() during compaction candidate selection, aborts the compaction (compactions blocked) and surfaces as a ServerError. The one-call overflow makes the DEFECT reachable; the >2B-cell partition makes the SERVER SYMPTOM infeasible on a 2.5GB pod.
verbatim_signature: mean() THREW: java.lang.IllegalStateException: Unable to compute ceiling for max when histogram overflowed
evidence: PROVENANCE: my OWN capture from the REAL cassandra:3.11.8 jar (/opt/cassandra/lib/apache-cassandra-3.11.8.jar) driven via Nashorn (jjs, JRE-only image has no javac), NOT cqlsh/the server request path. Script: new EstimatedHistogram(114); add(9e9) (>top offset 1996099046) -> isOverflowed()=true -> mean() threw "java.lang.IllegalStateException: Unable to compute ceiling for max when histogram overflowed"; and after serialize->deserialize->mean() it threw the identical exception. Source pinned from tag cassandra-3.11.8: EstimatedHistogram.rawMean() throws iff last bucket>0; MetadataCollector cell-per-partition EH(114) max ~2.4e9, size EH(150) ~1.5PB; StatsMetadata.getEstimatedDroppableTombstoneRatio() calls .mean() on the cell histogram (compaction path). Because the capture is a library-level harness, not the DB request path, it does NOT meet the "reproduced" bar.
control: A/B run of the IDENTICAL jjs script against the fixed cassandra:3.11.9 jar (same namespace, pod cass-fixed). Result: the raw serialize->deserialize->mean path does NOT discriminate — BOTH 3.11.8 and 3.11.9 still throw, because mean()/rawMean() still throw on overflow in 3.11.9 and the fix's new clearOverflow() method is invoked by the CALLER (metadata/compaction path), not by (de)serialization (confirmed from cassandra-3.11.9 source). The one observable fix-version diff captured live: 3.11.9 emits a NEW serialize-time WARN ("Serializing a histogram with 1 values greater than the maximum of 1996099046...") that 3.11.8 does not. Within-version reasoning: on both builds a normal-cell-count partition keeps the overflow bucket empty so mean()/compaction succeed; the version-discriminating reproducer is the fix's caller-level unit test (reinforcing needs-fix-test).
notes: Went further than typical for needs-fix-test: adapted AND ran the fix-style serialize->deserialize->mean test against the REAL buggy binary (via Nashorn jjs, since the cassandra:3.11.8 image is JRE-only with no javac), capturing the exact Jira IllegalStateException. The server-visible symptom (ServerError during compaction) is infeasible on a stock 2.5GB pod in budget: it needs a single partition >~2.0 billion cells (or >1.5PB), which OOMs the pod / hits mutation+frame caps, and there is no stock-pod path to inject a hand-crafted overflowed Statistics.db. Hence the disposition is needs-fix-test, not reproduced, despite the strong library-level evidence. Top bucket offset measured live as 1996099046 (the code comment 2395318855 counts the implicit overflow bucket) — immaterial to the conclusion.
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint (topology=1node; trigger: partition cell count exceeding histogram bound -> IllegalStateException histogram overflowed + compactions blocked) matches the Jira body and the source-confirmed mechanism. The only refinement (not a contradiction): the THROW itself needs just one oversized add(), while the SERVER-VISIBLE symptom needs a >~2B-cell SSTable; both are consistent with the hint.
tooling_findings: Several stale repro-* namespaces from other concurrent sessions are lingering on the cluster and were not cleaned up by those sessions: repro-13935, repro-14349, repro-14496, repro-14925, repro-15134, repro-15191, repro-15857, repro-15970, repro-16071, repro-16259, repro-20171, repro-20787, plus -ctl-suffixed ones (repro-15669-ctl, repro-20052-ctl). Inconsistent naming (some with -ctl, some without). RECORD-ONLY: I did not touch them. Separately, the cassandra:3.11.x official images are JRE-only (no javac), so any tooling that intends to compile in-pop Java reproducers must ship a JDK or use jjs/jshell; jjs (Nashorn) worked well here for driving cassandra jars directly.

trigger: Remove num_tokens from cassandra.yaml (leave it absent) and set initial_token to two tokens (initial_token: 100,200), then start cassandra. In 3.11.8 the applyInitialTokens() check compares tokens.size()=2 against the Config default num_tokens=1 and aborts startup, even though the operator never set num_tokens. CASSANDRA_NUM_TOKENS env is deliberately unset so the docker-entrypoint does not re-inject num_tokens.
verbatim_signature: Exception (org.apache.cassandra.exceptions.ConfigurationException) encountered during startup: The number of initial tokens (by initial_token) specified is different from num_tokens value
evidence: Single pod cass-buggy (cassandra:3.11.8) in ns repro-14477, restartPolicy:Never, command deletes num_tokens from /etc/cassandra/cassandra.yaml, appends 'initial_token: 100,200', then execs docker-entrypoint.sh cassandra -f. Pod reached terminal Error. kubectl logs cass-buggy shows the Node configuration dump with 'initial_token=100,200; ... num_tokens=1;' (num_tokens silently defaulted to 1 because it was absent), followed verbatim by: 'Exception (org.apache.cassandra.exceptions.ConfigurationException) encountered during startup: The number of initial tokens (by initial_token) specified is different from num_tokens value' and 'ERROR [main] 2026-06-12 04:25:55,511 CassandraDaemon.java:785 - Exception encountered during startup: The number of initial tokens (by initial_token) specified is different from num_tokens value'. This is the spurious/misleading trigger the ticket reports: the message blames a num_tokens mismatch though num_tokens was never set. Source confirms: 3.11.8 Config.java:81 'public int num_tokens = 1;' and DatabaseDescriptor.applyInitialTokens() lines 950-951 'if (tokens.size() != conf.num_tokens) throw new ConfigurationException(...)'.
control: A/B fixed control run on cassandra:3.11.19 (the fix is present there; the released ceiling for 3.11 is .19). IDENTICAL config (num_tokens removed, initial_token: 100,200), pod pinned to kind-worker with imagePullPolicy:Never. Result: also terminal Error, but with the CORRECTED message: 'Exception (org.apache.cassandra.exceptions.ConfigurationException) encountered during startup: initial_token was set but num_tokens is not!' (CassandraDaemon.java:808). The fix made Config.num_tokens a nullable Integer and added applyTokensConfig() which checks conf.num_tokens==null: a single initial_token with num_tokens unset now infers num_tokens=1 and starts cleanly, while >1 tokens unset yields the accurate diagnostic instead of the misleading 'different from num_tokens value'. IMPORTANT: buggy+1 = cassandra:3.11.9 (a Jira-listed fixVersion) is NOT a valid control — its source for this path is byte-identical to 3.11.8 (Config.java:81 'int num_tokens=1' unchanged; applyInitialTokens lines 945-956 identical), so it emits the same misleading message; hence 3.11.19 was substituted as the fixed control.
notes: Both 3.11.8 and 3.11.19 ABORT on this exact config (2 tokens + num_tokens absent); there is NO configuration where buggy aborts and fixed starts cleanly (single-token+absent starts in both; matching num_tokens starts in both). I did not miss a cleaner abort->start reproducer — none exists for this defect. Classified 'reproduced' because the defect the ticket reports IS the misleading error MESSAGE ('The number of initial tokens ... is different from num_tokens value' emitted when the operator never set num_tokens), which is operator-visible, captured verbatim on 3.11.8, and demonstrably corrected to 'initial_token was set but num_tokens is not!' on the 3.11.19 control. The fix is a message correction plus a nullable-Integer refactor of Config.num_tokens, not a behavioral abort->start change. The evidence bar explicitly accepts error text as a verbatim signature, so this clears it. Both pods reached terminal Error at config parse (~within 1s of Config load).
tag_correction: Two corrections vs the hints. (1) The hint phrase "aborts on SPURIOUS num_tokens mismatch check" overstates it: the fixed version (3.11.19) ALSO aborts on this same config — the fix clarifies the misleading message and makes single-token inference work, it does NOT remove the abort. More accurate: "misleading-message abort", not "spurious abort that the fix eliminates". The single-token variant (initial_token with one token + num_tokens absent) does NOT abort on either version, so it is not a repro. (2) Jira fixVersion lists 3.11.9, but released cassandra-3.11.9 is byte-identical to 3.11.8 for applyInitialTokens()/Config.num_tokens, so buggy+1 is NOT a valid fixed A/B control; the actual fix is present by 3.11.19, which was used. topology=1node and the broad trigger direction (set initial_token without num_tokens) are both correct.
tooling_findings: cassandra:3.11.19 image was cached only on kind node 'kind-worker' (verified via crictl images on each node); pods scheduled onto other nodes (e.g. kind-worker2) attempted a fresh Docker Hub pull and hit HTTP 429 'You have reached your unauthenticated pull rate limit' -> ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff. Worked around in a record-only way via nodeSelector kubernetes.io/hostname=kind-worker plus imagePullPolicy:Never. The buggy image cassandra:3.11.8 was already present on kind-worker (where its pod happened to land) and pulled/ran fine. No repo or tooling files were edited.

trigger: Set data_disk_usage_max_disk_size (any value, e.g. 1GiB) in cassandra.yaml and start a FRESH node (docker-entrypoint.sh cassandra -f) whose data dir does not yet exist. GuardrailsOptions.<init> runs validateDataDiskUsageMaxDiskSize -> DiskUsageMonitor.dataDirectoriesGroupedByFileStore -> Files.getFileStore(/opt/cassandra/data/data), which throws NoSuchFileException because createAllDirectories() has not run yet (applyGuardrails is called before it). Startup aborts (exit 3).
verbatim_signature: java.lang.RuntimeException: Cannot get data directories grouped by file store
evidence:
Buggy 5.0.4 pod (restartPolicy:Never) with injected `data_disk_usage_max_disk_size: 1GiB` crashed within ~2s, terminated exitCode=3 reason=Error. Pod log head proves both preconditions: injected setting at cassandra.yaml line 2285, and `ls: cannot access '/var/lib/cassandra/data': No such file or directory` (fresh node). VERBATIM crash (in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-20787.md and /tmp/repro-20787-buggy.log):

Exception (java.lang.RuntimeException) encountered during startup: Cannot get data directories grouped by file store
java.lang.RuntimeException: Cannot get data directories grouped by file store
	at org.apache.cassandra.service.disk.usage.DiskUsageMonitor.dataDirectoriesGroupedByFileStore(DiskUsageMonitor.java:202)
	at org.apache.cassandra.service.disk.usage.DiskUsageMonitor.totalDiskSpace(DiskUsageMonitor.java:209)
	at org.apache.cassandra.config.GuardrailsOptions.validateDataDiskUsageMaxDiskSize(GuardrailsOptions.java:1255)
	at org.apache.cassandra.config.GuardrailsOptions.<init>(GuardrailsOptions.java:87)
	at org.apache.cassandra.config.DatabaseDescriptor.applyGuardrails(DatabaseDescriptor.java:1113)
	at org.apache.cassandra.config.DatabaseDescriptor.applyAll(DatabaseDescriptor.java:470)
	at org.apache.cassandra.config.DatabaseDescriptor.daemonInitialization(DatabaseDescriptor.java:262)
	...
Caused by: java.nio.file.NoSuchFileException: /opt/cassandra/data/data
	at java.base/sun.nio.fs.UnixFileStore.devFor(Unknown Source)
	at java.base/java.nio.file.Files.getFileStore(Unknown Source)
	at org.apache.cassandra.service.disk.usage.DiskUsageMonitor.dataDirectoriesGroupedByFileStore(DiskUsageMonitor.java:196)
	... 10 more

The two discriminating frames (dataDirectoriesGroupedByFileStore:202 and validateDataDiskUsageMaxDiskSize) and the full applyGuardrails->...->Files.getFileStore->NoSuchFileException chain match the Jira exactly. Path is the image's real default /opt/cassandra/data/data (Jira showed sanitized /path/to/data). Line-number deltas reflect a different source snapshot.
control: A/B with fixed image cassandra:5.0.5 (a Jira fixVersion), IDENTICAL injected config (`data_disk_usage_max_disk_size: 1GiB`) and IDENTICAL fresh-node precondition (pod log confirms `/var/lib/cassandra/data` did NOT exist at boot). Result: NO startup exception (grep for the RuntimeException/NoSuchFileException returned nothing), pod reached 1/1 Running in ~100s, and `cqlsh -e "SELECT now() FROM system.local"` answered with a row (system.now() 0f991e50-6617-11f1-b6c3-bb72821ef156). After boot, 5.0.5 had created /opt/cassandra/data/data, i.e. createAllDirectories now runs before guardrail validation. Version is the only changed variable, isolating the bug. (Control log: /tmp/repro-20787-control.log.)
notes: Config-gated startup crash; topology truly 1-node (no ring needed). Key insight enabling repro: the official cassandra image has `data_file_directories` commented (uses internal default resolving to /opt/cassandra/data/data), the data dir does NOT exist in the image, and docker-entrypoint.sh does NOT create it (only chowns existing dirs + sed-edits yaml) -> first boot satisfies the bug precondition. Used restartPolicy:Never so the crash is a stable terminal state. Control pod was nested inside the same namespace repro-20787 (pinned to kind-worker3 where 5.0.5 was already cached) so one namespace delete tears down both pods. Did not touch any pre-existing namespace.
tag_correction: none — classifier hint (topology=1node, confidence=H, trigger: set data_disk_usage_max_disk_size on a fresh node whose data dir doesn't exist -> startup crash NoSuchFileException) matched the Jira body exactly.
tooling_findings: (1) Docker Hub returned HTTP 429 "unauthenticated pull rate limit" when kind nodes tried to pull cassandra:5.0.4 from registry-1.docker.io, blocking the normal pull path. (2) `kind load docker-image cassandra:5.0.4` and `kind load image-archive` both FAILED with `ctr: content digest sha256:c29fd... not found` because kind's import wrapper uses `--all-platforms --digests` against a multi-arch image whose per-arch manifest blobs aren't in the local docker `docker save` tar. Worked around (RECORD-ONLY, no fixes) by importing directly into each kind node's containerd: `docker exec <node> ctr --namespace=k8s.io images import --snapshotter=overlayfs - < /tmp/cass-504.tar` (no --digests/--all-platforms), which succeeded. cassandra:5.0.5 was already cached on kind-worker3 from the pre-existing cass-5-0-5 namespace. Both are environment/tooling observations only.

trigger: On cassandra:3.11.9 (single pod, ns repro-14925, keyspace repro14925): table `t(pk int, ck int, d decimal, PRIMARY KEY(pk,ck))`. Insert one live row carrying the malicious decimal `INSERT ... VALUES (1, 0, 1E-2147483641)` (scale = Integer.MAX_VALUE-6 = 2147483641), then create 3000 row tombstones in pk=1 (`DELETE ... WHERE pk=1 AND ck=i` for i=1..3000). Then run the client query `SELECT pk,ck FROM repro14925.t WHERE pk=1 AND d = 1E-2147483641 ALLOW FILTERING;`. The tombstone-warn threshold (1000) is exceeded during the scan, so ReadCommand.MetricRecording.onClose calls ReadCommand.toCQLString() -> appendCQLWhereClause -> RowFilter.toString() -> RowFilter$SimpleExpression.toString() -> column.type.getString(value) -> DecimalSerializer.toString() -> BigDecimal.toPlainString(), which tries to build a ~2.1-billion-char string and OOMs. -XX:OnOutOfMemoryError=kill -9 then kills the JVM; the client sees ReadFailure (code=1300).
verbatim_signature:
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space
	at java.math.BigDecimal.toPlainString(BigDecimal.java:2984) ~[na:1.8.0_282]
	at org.apache.cassandra.serializers.DecimalSerializer.toString(DecimalSerializer.java:70) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.9.jar:3.11.9]
	at org.apache.cassandra.db.marshal.AbstractType.getString(AbstractType.java:134) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.9.jar:3.11.9]
	at org.apache.cassandra.db.filter.RowFilter$SimpleExpression.toString(RowFilter.java:860) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.9.jar:3.11.9]
evidence: Verbatim server-log stack (kubectl logs -n repro-14925 cass) on 3.11.9 after the trigger query: "ERROR [ReadStage-11] ... Uncaught exception on thread Thread[ReadStage-11,10,main] / java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space / at java.math.BigDecimal.getValueString(BigDecimal.java:3000) / at java.math.BigDecimal.toPlainString(BigDecimal.java:2984) / at org.apache.cassandra.serializers.DecimalSerializer.toString(DecimalSerializer.java:70) / at org.apache.cassandra.serializers.DecimalSerializer.toString(DecimalSerializer.java:26) / at org.apache.cassandra.db.marshal.AbstractType.getString(AbstractType.java:134) / at org.apache.cassandra.db.filter.RowFilter$SimpleExpression.toString(RowFilter.java:860) / at org.apache.cassandra.db.filter.RowFilter.toString(RowFilter.java:294) / at org.apache.cassandra.db.SinglePartitionReadCommand.appendCQLWhereClause(SinglePartitionReadCommand.java:1134) / at org.apache.cassandra.db.ReadCommand.toCQLString(ReadCommand.java:691) / at org.apache.cassandra.db.ReadCommand$1MetricRecording.onClose(ReadCommand.java:574) ..." followed by "ERROR ... JVMStabilityInspector.java:94 - OutOfMemory error letting the JVM handle the error" and "# -XX:OnOutOfMemoryError=\"kill -9 %p\" / # Executing /bin/sh -c \"kill -9 1\"...". Client saw: "ReadFailure: Error from server: code=1300 [Replica(s) failed to execute read] message=\"Operation failed - received 0 responses and 1 failures\"". Source confirmation: cloned cassandra-3.11.9 shows DecimalSerializer.toString returns value.toPlainString() with no scale guard, and AbstractType.getString calls serializer.toString(serializer.deserialize(bytes)).
control: A/B with cassandra:3.11.10 (fixed line, 3.11.9-patch+1, <= ceiling 19), pod cass-ctl in the SAME namespace/node, IDENTICAL workload (same table, same 1 live row d=1E-2147483641, same 3000 tombstones, same query). Result: query SUCCEEDED, returned the live row (pk=1, ck=0), 0 restarts, no OOM. The SAME tombstone-warn render path ran (server log ReadCommand.java:576) but rendered the decimal COMPACTLY: "WARN [ReadStage-7] ... Read 1 live rows and 3000 tombstone cells for query SELECT * FROM repro14925.t WHERE pk = 1 AND d = 1E-2147483641 LIMIT 100; token -4069959284402364209 (see tombstone_warn_threshold)". No OutOfMemoryError/toPlainString/DecimalSerializer in the control log. The fix uses BigDecimal.toString() when scale>100 (default -Dcassandra.decimal.maxscaleforstring=100). Only difference between runs = image version; only behavioral difference = the OOM.
notes: Single-node sufficient (value-stringification bug). The earlier classifier-suggested triggers were empirically falsified on 3.11.9: (1) SELECT JSON returned {"id":1,"d":1E-2147483641} compactly with no OOM because DecimalType.toJSONString uses Objects.toString(deserialize(...)) = BigDecimal.toString(), NOT serializer.toString; (2) TRACING ON + WHERE d=<literal> ALLOW FILTERING on a 0-tombstone table did not fire (trace only echoes the raw CQL string). The real client-reachable path is the tombstone metric-recording render of toCQLString() -> RowFilter -> getString. Enumerated getString callers from cloned 3.11.9 source to find this path. RF=1, scale=2147483641 deliberately kept under Integer.MAX_VALUE to get a clean Java-heap-space OOM (not NegativeArraySizeException).
tag_correction: Hint trigger said "decimal w/ huge negative scale read via SELECT JSON / tracing -> OOM". Corrected: on 3.11.9, SELECT JSON does NOT fire it (DecimalType.toJSONString uses BigDecimal.toString, compact — proven empirically: returned 1E-2147483641 with no OOM), and plain TRACING of the literal does not fire it either. The actual client-reachable firing path is the tombstone-warn/tombstone-failure rendering: ReadCommand.MetricRecording.onClose -> ReadCommand.toCQLString() -> appendCQLWhereClause -> RowFilter.toString() -> SimpleExpression.toString() -> AbstractType.getString() -> DecimalSerializer.toString() -> BigDecimal.toPlainString(). Requires the malicious decimal in the WHERE clause AND a tombstone-heavy scan. Confidence M -> reproduced (verbatim stack + clean A/B). Topology 1node confirmed correct.
tooling_findings: `kind load docker-image cassandra:3.11.9 cassandra:3.11.10 --name kind` repeatedly failed importing into the kind-control-plane node with "ctr: content digest sha256:... not found" (a known kind/containerd multi-platform manifest quirk); the images did load onto kind-worker3. Worked around RECORD-ONLY by pinning both pods to kind-worker3 via nodeSelector kubernetes.io/hostname=kind-worker3 with imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent. No repo/tooling files were edited.

trigger: Single Cassandra pod (cassandra:4.0.1) with emptyDir at /var/lib/cassandra so the data dir survives container restarts. Boot the node, then restart the container in-place via `kubectl exec -n repro-16839 cass-buggy -- kill 1` (PID 1 = cassandra -f; SIGTERM -> graceful shutdown -> kubelet restarts container, emptyDir persists). After each boot run `nodetool listsnapshots`. No workload needed — bug fires on the empty system.size_estimates / system.table_estimates tables. Each node start creates one new truncated-<ts>-size_estimates and one new truncated-<ts>-table_estimates snapshot (distinct timestamps), accumulating monotonically.
verbatim_signature: truncated-1781238400142-size_estimates  system        size_estimates     0 bytes   13 bytes
evidence: On cassandra:4.0.1 (cass-buggy), `nodetool listsnapshots` after the FIRST boot already showed a truncated-* pair: `truncated-1781238402833-table_estimates system table_estimates 0 bytes 13 bytes` and `truncated-1781238400142-size_estimates system size_estimates 0 bytes 13 bytes`. After 2 in-place container restarts (restartCount 0->1->2, 3 total boots) the list grew to 6 rows = 3 size_estimates + 3 table_estimates snapshots, each with a DISTINCT timestamp (size_estimates ts: 1781238400142, 1781238522715, 1781238596313 ; table_estimates ts: 1781238402833, 1781238523099, 1781238601577) — exactly one new pair per startup, matching the Jira report shape including the 0-byte/13-byte empty-table snapshots. Output is operator-visible via nodetool. Full commands and raw outputs in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-16839.md.
control: A/B control with the released fixed image cassandra:4.0.2 (fixVersions includes 4.0.2; 2 <= 4.0 ceiling 20). Identical pod spec, env, emptyDir, and restart procedure (2 in-place restarts via kill 1, restartCount 0->1->2). cass-fixed (4.0.2) `nodetool listsnapshots` returned "There are no snapshots" after the first boot AND after both restarts — zero truncated-* snapshots across all 3 boots. The only difference between the two pods was the image tag, so the spurious startup truncation snapshots are attributable to the 4.0.1 bug and are suppressed by the 4.0.2 fix.
notes: Clean reproduction with side-by-side A/B control in one namespace (cass-buggy 4.0.1 + cass-fixed 4.0.2). The bug fires on EVERY node startup including the very first boot (not only on subsequent restarts), and snapshot count grows by exactly one size_estimates + one table_estimates per boot. Discriminator (monotonic growth of truncated-<ts>-{size,table}_estimates with distinct timestamps) is satisfied: 4.0.1 = 2->6 rows over 3 boots; 4.0.2 = 0 rows. ReleaseVersion banners confirmed 4.0.1 and 4.0.2 respectively. component=Tool/nodetool. Bounded budget respected; ~3 deploy/test cycles (first deploy hit 429 ImagePullBackOff, redeployed with node pinning, then ran restart cycles).
tag_correction: none — hint topology=1node matches the body, and the hint trigger (restart node + nodetool listsnapshots -> spurious truncated-* snapshots of size_estimates/table_estimates each restart) matches the Jira description exactly. Only nuance: the bug also fires on the first boot, not only on restarts.
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling defects observed; RECORD-ONLY honored (nothing edited/patched). Environmental: Docker Hub returned HTTP 429 (toomanyrequests / unauthenticated pull rate limit) on fresh image pulls; the initial deploy went ImagePullBackOff because the scheduler placed pods on nodes lacking the cached image. Worked around without touching any repo/tooling by pinning each pod via nodeName to the kind worker already caching the needed image and setting imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent (4.0.1 cached on kind-worker2, 4.0.2 on kind-worker3). Useful note for whoever builds this into an SREGym problem: the single-node pod template has no PVC/emptyDir and no readiness probe gating CQL — any restart-persistence bug REQUIRES an emptyDir (or PVC) mounted at /var/lib/cassandra, otherwise an in-place container restart wipes the data dir and snapshots, making this bug look not-reproducible.

trigger: With authentication + CassandraAuthorizer + UDFs enabled, create a zero-arg UDF, GRANT EXECUTE ON FUNCTION ks.fn() TO a role (succeeds, writes resource string functions/ks/fn[]), then run LIST ALL PERMISSIONS OF <role>. The read path Resources.fromName -> FunctionResource.fromName parses functions/ks/fn[] and accesses nameAndArgs[1] on a length-1 array, throwing ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.
verbatim_signature:
java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 1 out of bounds for length 1
	at org.apache.cassandra.auth.FunctionResource.fromName(FunctionResource.java:190)
evidence: BUGGY cassandra:4.0.1: LIST ALL PERMISSIONS OF bob returns client-side NoHostAvailable; server log /var/log/cassandra/system.log shows verbatim: "ERROR [Native-Transport-Requests-1] 2026-06-12 04:46:47,992 ErrorMessage.java:457 - Unexpected exception during request / java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 1 out of bounds for length 1 / at org.apache.cassandra.auth.FunctionResource.fromName(FunctionResource.java:190)" with call chain Resources.fromName(60) -> CassandraAuthorizer.listPermissionsForRole(282) -> CassandraAuthorizer.list(262) -> ListPermissionsStatement.list(112)/execute(100). Function confirmed zero-arg: system_schema.functions argument_types = []. Matches Jira description (AIOOBE at FunctionResource.fromName; Jira cites line 178, this build line 190 — same code, branch line drift).
control: A/B control on fixed cassandra:4.0.2 (= buggy 4.0.1 + 1, <= 4.0 ceiling 20; 4.0.2 is a listed fixVersion). Identical workload (same keyspace, same zero-arg UDF zeroarg(), role bob, GRANT EXECUTE, LIST ALL PERMISSIONS OF bob). Fixed image returns the row cleanly with no exception and no error in log: "bob | bob | <function repro16977.zeroarg()> | EXECUTE (1 rows)", list-exit=0. Confirms the bug is specific to the buggy build and is corrected in 4.0.2.
notes: Single-node, authorization bug. The classifier hint (1node, trigger about granting permission on a zero-arg function) was accurate. Key nuance: the GRANT succeeds (resource is written); the crash fires on the read-back path (LIST PERMISSIONS), confirmed by the stack ending in CassandraAuthorizer.listPermissionsForRole. Required cassandra.yaml: authenticator=PasswordAuthenticator, authorizer=CassandraAuthorizer, enable_user_defined_functions=true, applied via sed before docker-entrypoint. Both pods pinned to kind-worker via nodeSelector. Superuser cassandra/cassandra used. Multi-statement cqlsh via single -e with leading newline failed once (SyntaxException) — re-ran statements individually; not a product issue.
tag_correction: none — hint (1node, M, grant on zero-arg function -> AIOOBE in FunctionResource.fromName) is correct. Minor refinement: requires auth+CassandraAuthorizer+UDFs enabled; AIOOBE surfaces on the read-back (LIST PERMISSIONS), not on the GRANT itself. Jira body line 178 vs. observed line 190 is benign branch line-number drift, same method/code.
tooling_findings: `kind load docker-image` and `kind load image-archive` to kind-control-plane both failed with "ctr: content digest sha256:... not found" for these multi-arch cassandra images (the --all-platforms/--digests import path conflicts with the local single-platform manifest). Workaround that worked: docker save to a tarball, then pipe into a specific worker node's containerd: `docker exec -i kind-worker ctr --namespace=k8s.io images import - < /tmp/img.tar`, and pin the pod to that node with nodeSelector kubernetes.io/hostname=kind-worker. Docker Hub unauthenticated pull rate limit was hit for docker.io; pulling from mirror.gcr.io/library/cassandra:<ver> succeeded. (Recorded only; nothing edited/fixed.)

trigger: auto_snapshot:true (stock default), then on a single 3.11.12 pod: CREATE TABLE base + CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv, INSERT rows, nodetool flush (so both base-* and mv-* SSTables exist on disk), then DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW mv and DROP TABLE base. Auto-snapshot fires on each drop. Inspect snapshot directory names on disk with: find /var/lib/cassandra/data/<ks> -type d -path '*/snapshots/*'. The MV's snapshot directory lacks the 'dropped-' prefix that the normal table's snapshot has.
verbatim_signature: /var/lib/cassandra/data/repro17415_ks/mv-e1917d60661911f1a8c4edaf56a013df/snapshots/1781239738805-mv
evidence:
Within-image contrast on the SAME buggy 3.11.12 binary (cass-1112), snapshot dirs after dropping both a normal table and an MV (auto_snapshot=true confirmed):
  base-...-/snapshots/dropped-1781239759581-base   <- normal table: HAS 'dropped-' prefix (correct)
  mv-...-/snapshots/1781239738805-mv               <- materialized view: MISSING 'dropped-' prefix (THE BUG)
Both snapshots are physically created (auto_snapshot works); only the MV snapshot lacks the prefix. This is the exact CASSANDRA-17415 symptom and, per the body, is 3.11.x-only.

A/B fixed-version control on cassandra:3.11.19 (cass-11119), identical reproducer:
  base-...-/snapshots/dropped-1781239887812-base   <- HAS 'dropped-' prefix
  mv-...-/snapshots/dropped-1781239845416-mv        <- HAS 'dropped-' prefix (FIXED)
So on the fixed line the MV snapshot also gets 'dropped-'; the only variable that changed the outcome is the binary version, isolating the bug.

The signature is a filesystem directory name, not a server exception: the DROP succeeds with no cqlsh/server error; the observable is the on-disk snapshot directory name, which operator snapshot-listing/recovery tooling keys on via the 'dropped-' prefix.

Bonus: nodetool listsnapshots returned 'There are no snapshots' on BOTH nodes (already-dropped tables are gone from schema and not enumerated); direct filesystem find is the reliable observable.
control: A/B fixed-version control run on cassandra:3.11.19 (cass-11119 in the same namespace, pinned to kind-worker with imagePullPolicy IfNotPresent because it was already cached). 3.11.19 is on the fixed line (fix landed in 3.11.13; 19 <= 3.11 ceiling). Identical reproducer: the MV's snapshot directory was 'dropped-1781239845416-mv' (HAS the 'dropped-' prefix), vs the buggy 3.11.12 MV snapshot '1781239738805-mv' (no prefix). The base-table snapshot had 'dropped-' on both versions. cassandra:3.11.13 itself could not be pulled (Docker Hub 429 unauthenticated rate limit); 3.11.19 is an equivalent fixed image and a valid control.
notes: Single-node sufficient; this is a local SSTable/snapshot directory-naming issue. Keyspace repro17415_ks, MV mv over base table base. DROP statements returned client-side cqlsh OperationTimedOut (schema-agreement wait) but executed server-side, confirmed by schema descriptions and on-disk snapshot dirs. Snapshot timestamps corroborate the drop order (MV 738805 before base 759581, ~20s gap = the two cqlsh timeouts). Budget: 2 deploy-test cycles, both pods in one namespace.
tag_correction: none — the Jira body matched the classifier hint exactly: topology=1node, trigger=auto_snapshot:true + DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW yields a snapshot directory missing the 'dropped-' prefix, 3.11.x-only. (The 3.11.13->3.11.19 control substitution is a tooling adaptation, recorded in control/tooling_findings, not a tag correction.)
tooling_findings: 1) cassandra:3.11.13 could not be pulled from Docker Hub: HTTP 429 'toomanyrequests: You have reached your unauthenticated pull rate limit'. Worked around by using the already-cached cassandra:3.11.19 (also fixed) as the A/B control, pinned to kind-worker. SREGym runs needing specific 3.11.x images on a fresh node would benefit from authenticated Docker Hub pulls or a local registry mirror. 2) nodetool in the cassandra:3.11.x images fails JMX connect with "URISyntaxException: 'Malformed IPv6 address at index 7: rmi://[127.0.0.1]:7199'" on the bundled JDK; worked around with JVM_OPTS="-Dcom.sun.jndi.rmiURLParsing=legacy" for nodetool calls (flush/listsnapshots). Generic image/JDK issue, not specific to this bug. RECORD ONLY — nothing fixed.

trigger: Single Cassandra pod on a 1Gi PVC, upgraded in place 3.11.8 -> 3.11.9. On 3.11.8: create table repro16259_ks.hist_bug (pk int, ck int, v text, PK(pk,ck)) with compaction disabled, INSERT a few rows, `nodetool flush` (produces an sstable whose cell-count EstimatedHistogram has 115 bucket rows). Drain, delete pod (keep PVC), start cassandra:3.11.9 on the SAME PVC. INSERT one more row, `nodetool flush` (second sstable has 119 bucket rows due to CASSANDRA-15164 raising the CellPerPartitionCount default 114->118). Then run `nodetool tablehistograms repro16259_ks hist_bug`. The mismatched bucket counts make TableMetrics.combineHistograms index the smaller sstable's array out of bounds.
verbatim_signature:
java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: 115
	at org.apache.cassandra.metrics.TableMetrics.combineHistograms(TableMetrics.java:261)
	at org.apache.cassandra.metrics.TableMetrics.access$000(TableMetrics.java:48)
	at org.apache.cassandra.metrics.TableMetrics$11.getValue(TableMetrics.java:376)
	at org.apache.cassandra.metrics.TableMetrics$11.getValue(TableMetrics.java:373)
evidence: On the upgraded 3.11.9 node the table had two coexisting sstables with mismatched cell-count histogram bucket counts (md-1-big=115 rows written by 3.11.8, md-2-big=119 rows written by 3.11.9; both verified via /opt/cassandra/tools/bin/sstablemetadata). `nodetool tablehistograms repro16259_ks hist_bug` returned `error: 115` followed by `java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: 115 at org.apache.cassandra.metrics.TableMetrics.combineHistograms(TableMetrics.java:261)` and frames access$000(:48), $11.getValue(:376), $11.getValue(:373) -- a line-for-line match to the Jira body. The index 115 equals the smaller sstable's bucket count, confirming the off-by-bucket out-of-bounds (`values[i] += nextBucket[i]` where i runs to the larger 119 length). Exit code 2. Full raw stacktrace (incl. the JMX/RMI frames the report also shows) is in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-16259.md.
control: Two-sided control on identical on-disk data. CONTROL-A: before upgrade, `nodetool tablehistograms` on cassandra:3.11.8 (only the 115-bucket sstable) returned the histogram table cleanly, EXIT=0. CONTROL-B (fixed A/B): drained 3.11.9, swapped the pod image to cassandra:3.11.10 (fixed; 3.11 ceiling=19 so valid) on the SAME PVC with the SAME two sstables (re-verified md-1=115, md-2=119 buckets), ran the identical `nodetool tablehistograms repro16259_ks hist_bug` -> returned the full histogram table with EXIT=0, no exception. Identical workload + identical sstables: 3.11.9 throws AIOOBE:115, 3.11.10 succeeds. Also a negative control: a fresh single-version 3.11.9 table (hist_bug2, 3 sstables all 119 buckets) did NOT throw, demonstrating the mismatch (hence the upgrade) is required.
notes: Bug is in TableMetrics.combineHistograms aggregating the estimatedColumnCount (cells-per-partition) EstimatedHistogram across sstables: accumulator sized from the first/larger sstable, then `values[i] += nextBucket[i]` runs past a later smaller sstable's array. Root cause of the mismatch is CASSANDRA-15164 (shipped in 3.11.9) raising the CellPerPartitionCount default bucket count 114->118; an upgraded node holding both pre- and post-upgrade sstables for one table hits it. Reporter saw it both via `nodetool tablehistograms` and Datastax MCAC polling JMX every 30s; "scrubbing the affected table makes histograms work again" (rewrites sstables to uniform bucket count). Used the smallest possible data (a handful of rows); the bug is purely about per-sstable histogram bucket-count, not data volume.
tag_correction: Mechanism in the hint is correct ("sstables with mismatched histogram bucket counts -> tablehistograms -> ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException") but the hint omits HOW the mismatch arises and is therefore misleading about reproducibility: within a single version every sstable's cell-count histogram has the same bucket count, so a fresh single-version 3.11.9 node does NOT reproduce (verified). The mismatch only exists across the 3.11.8->3.11.9 boundary (CASSANDRA-15164 changed the default 114->118), so the real reproducer is an in-place UPGRADE of one node's data dir, not a clean fresh node. Topology hint (1node) is correct. Confidence M was appropriate.
tooling_findings: (1) `sstablemetadata` is not on PATH in the official cassandra:3.11.x images; it lives at /opt/cassandra/tools/bin/sstablemetadata (used that absolute path). (2) `cqlsh -e "<stmt1>;\n<stmt2>"` with a LEADING newline before the first statement raised `SyntaxException: line 1:0 no viable alternative at input ';'` -- multi-statement cqlsh strings must not start with whitespace/newline. (3) Passing a ~300KB literal as a cqlsh shell argument failed with `argument list too long`; large values must be loaded via `cqlsh -f <file>` (kubectl cp then exec) rather than inline -e. None fixed (record-only).

trigger: Enable CDC (cdc_enabled: true), produce NO CDC traffic (write only to a non-CDC table), then kill -9 the Cassandra process (NOT drain, so dirty commit-log segments must be replayed) and restart repeatedly. Replayed commit-log segments accumulate in cdc_raw/ and are never deleted, while the live commitlog/ correctly rotates/deletes them.
verbatim_signature: CommitLog-6-1781241743009.log  linkcount=1  ORPHAN(absent-from-commitlog)
evidence: On cassandra:3.11.9 with cdc_enabled:true, a kill -9 + restart loop (replay confirmed each cycle: "Log replay complete, 28 replayed mutations") accumulated orphaned segments in /var/lib/cassandra/cdc_raw/ monotonically: CDC_LOG_COUNT 0 -> 2 -> 4 -> 6 across 4 cycles. Final cdc_raw held 6 segments (each 33554432 bytes), all linkcount=1 and ABSENT from the live commitlog/ dir (which held only the 2 current segments) — i.e. the originals were correctly deleted but the cdc_raw copies leaked. ORPHAN CHECK output line: "CommitLog-6-1781241743009.log  linkcount=1  ORPHAN(absent-from-commitlog)" (x6). Mechanism matches Jira and source: CommitLogSegmentManagerCDC.handleReplayedSegment unconditionally does renameWithConfirm(file -> cdc_raw) + cdcSizeTracker.addFlushedSize with no deletion/no CDC-data check (verbatim from apache/cassandra cassandra-3.11, still present at HEAD 3.11.20). CDC-gated discriminator on the SAME 3.11.9 binary with cdc_enabled:false: identical loop, replay firing (95/69/47 replayed mutations), cdc_raw stayed 0 -> 0 -> 0 across 3 restarts — proving the leak is CDC-specific, not a generic restart artifact.
control: No valid fixed image exists. The A/B was attempted on cassandra:3.11.10 (buggy+1, the Jira fixVersion) and it behaved IDENTICALLY to 3.11.9 (cdc_raw also grew 0->2->4->6, same orphan pattern, all linkcount=1). Reason: cassandra:3.11.10 does NOT contain the 14349 fix. The released 3.11.10 binary's CHANGES.txt is dated Jan 29 2021 and lists no CASSANDRA-14349 (grep 14349 -> exit 1), whereas the Jira was resolved 27/Jul/2021 — months AFTER 3.11.10 shipped. The buggy handleReplayedSegment is still present at cassandra-3.11 HEAD (CHANGES top=3.11.20) and 14349 is absent from cassandra-4.0 CHANGES.txt too. Discrimination therefore rests on within-version reasoning: (a) primary-source mechanism match, (b) source inspection of handleReplayedSegment, (c) empirical proof the candidate fixed image is unfixed, plus (d) a demonstrated CDC-gated discriminator (cdc off on the same 3.11.9 binary => no leak).
notes: This is a resource-leak bug, so the reproduction signature is filesystem state (monotonic cdc_raw growth + orphaned segments), NOT a stack trace or wrong query result — there is no exception. An operator sees it as a slow disk leak in cdc_raw on every restart when CDC is enabled. linkcount=1 is expected and correct: handleReplayedSegment MOVES the segment via renameWithConfirm, so after replay the original commitlog path is gone and the cdc_raw copy is the sole remaining reference; the Jira's "hard link" wording refers to the broader CDC mechanism. The orphaned files are sparse (du showed 316K actual for 6x32MB logical), so the test stayed well within the disk budget; used a 4-cycle (cdc-on) + 3-cycle (cdc-off) loop, tiny non-CDC writes only.
tag_correction: Hint topology=1node CONFIRMED. Hint trigger CONFIRMED (enable CDC + no CDC traffic + repeated restarts -> cdc_raw piles up, never cleaned). Correction to the candidate's implied control: the Jira fixVersion 3.11.10 is inaccurate for the released artifact — cassandra:3.11.10 (the buggy+1 control image) was built Jan 2021, BEFORE the bug's Jul 2021 resolution, and empirically still leaks (verified: identical 0->2->4->6 growth + 14349 absent from its CHANGES.txt). The fix's handleReplayedSegment change is moreover still not present at cassandra-3.11 HEAD (3.11.20) nor in cassandra-4.0 CHANGES.txt, so no fixed 3.11.x image is available for a clean A/B.
tooling_findings: 1) The "buggy_patch+1 = fixed control image" assumption is unsafe when the Jira resolution date is LATER than the buggy+1 release date. Here cassandra:3.11.10 (Jira fixVersion) shipped Jan 2021 but the fix was resolved Jul 2021, so the "fixed" control image is actually a false-equivalent (still buggy) and the naive A/B silently fails to discriminate. Recommend cross-checking CHANGES.txt for the ticket number in the candidate fixed image before treating buggy+1 as fixed. 2) kind image side-loading friction: Docker Hub returns 429 (unauthenticated pull rate limit) so pods ImagePullBackOff; the host had the images but `kind load docker-image`/`kind load image-archive` failed with "content digest ... not found" because the saved tar is a multi-arch manifest while only amd64 layers are present and kind's wrapper uses `ctr import --all-platforms --digests`. Workaround that worked: pipe the docker-save tar directly into each node's containerd with `docker exec -i <node> ctr --namespace=k8s.io images import --digests=false --snapshotter=overlayfs -`. Also note kind scheduling: pods may land on any worker, so images must be present on all candidate nodes (or use a nodeSelector). RECORD ONLY — not fixed.

trigger: Send a CQL native protocol v4 QUERY with the with_paging_state flag (0x08) and a crafted paging_state whose first unsigned-VInt (partitionKeyLen) encodes a large positive value (0x7FFFFFFF). In PagingState.isModernSerialized, `int index += computeUnsignedVIntSize(partitionKeyLen) + partitionKeyLen` overflows int to -2147483644, passes the `index >= limit` check, then getUnsignedVInt -> input.get(-2147483644) throws IndexOutOfBoundsException.
verbatim_signature: java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException: -2147483644
evidence: Buggy cassandra:4.0.5 over raw native-protocol v4: a valid paging_state returns RESULT kind=2, but the crafted paging_state `f07fffffff` (partitionKeyLen=2147483647) returns an ERROR frame (opcode 0x00, error code 0x00000000 = SERVER_ERROR) with message 'java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException: -2147483644'. The value -2147483644 is exactly the int-overflowed index (0 + 5 + 2147483647 = 2147483652 -> (int) = -2147483644), matching the JIRA description (int field, long added without Math.toIntExact). A/B control on the actually-fixed cassandra:4.0.7: identical workload returns ERROR code 0x0000000a (PROTOCOL_ERROR) 'Invalid value for the paging state' instead of the raw IOOBE — confirming the bug and the fix. The crafted-vs-valid contrast on the SAME pod isolates the trigger.
control: A/B control with fixed image. cassandra:4.0.7 (pulled + kind-loaded, version confirmed via `cassandra -v`) ran the IDENTICAL malicious workload and did NOT misbehave: it returned ERROR code 0x0000000a (PROTOCOL_ERROR) message 'Invalid value for the paging state' instead of the buggy 0x00000000 SERVER_ERROR 'java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException: -2147483644'. 4.0.7 source confirms the fix (toIntExact, addNonNegative, and an added `index < 0` guard in isModernSerialized). The valid real paging_state returned RESULT kind=2 on both buggy and fixed, isolating the difference to the overflow input.
notes: Single-node bug, fully reproduced. cqlsh cannot inject a raw arbitrary paging_state, so I wrote a pure-Python-stdlib native-protocol v4 client (/tmp/repro17840c.py), copied it into each pod, and ran it against 127.0.0.1:9042. Key gotcha: native protocol QUERY flag 0x08 = with_paging_state (flag 0x04 is page_size); my first attempt used 0x04 by mistake and the crafted bytes were parsed as a page-size int (false negative). Only a few rows needed (no bulk data). Tiny disk footprint.
tag_correction: Trigger/topology hint CONFIRMED ACCURATE (1node, V4 connection, crafted paging_state, int overflow, IndexOutOfBoundsException). However the JIRA fixVersions field lists 4.0.6, but the fix is NOT in 4.0.6: cassandra:4.0.6 reproduces the bug IDENTICALLY (same signature 'IndexOutOfBoundsException: -2147483644'), and its PagingState.java source is unchanged. The fix actually first ships in 4.0.7 (CHANGES.txt: 'Fix potential IndexOutOfBoundsException in PagingState in mixed mode clusters (CASSANDRA-17840)'). So the task's suggested fixed-control 'buggy patch+1' = 4.0.6 is NOT a valid fixed control; 4.0.7 is the correct fixed image. I used 4.0.7 for the A/B.
tooling_findings: The skill's A/B-control rule 'run on cassandra:<buggy patch+1> and show it does NOT misbehave' would fail silently here: 4.0.6 (buggy+1) still has the bug because CASSANDRA-17840's fix landed in 4.0.7 despite JIRA fixVersions listing 4.0.6. For bugs where the JIRA fixVersion does not match the actual release tag containing the patch, 'buggy patch+1' can be a false fixed-control. Recommend verifying the fix is present in the candidate fixed image (e.g., check CHANGES.txt / source) before trusting an A/B 'fixed' result. No repo files were edited.

trigger: Place any .jar in the Cassandra triggers directory (/etc/cassandra/triggers) on 4.1.0, then run `nodetool reloadtriggers` (also fired by CREATE TRIGGER / first trigger use). This invokes TriggerExecutor.reloadClasses() -> new CustomClassLoader(parent, triggerDir) -> addClassPath(), which calls FileUtils.createTempFile (that physically creates the empty destination temp file) and then java.nio.file.Files.copy(inputJar, out) WITHOUT StandardCopyOption.REPLACE_EXISTING. Because the temp file already exists, copy throws FileAlreadyExistsException, breaking all trigger-from-JAR loading.
verbatim_signature: java.nio.file.FileAlreadyExistsException: /tmp/lib/cassandra-0.jar	at java.base/java.nio.file.Files.copy(Unknown Source)	at org.apache.cassandra.triggers.CustomClassLoader.addClassPath(CustomClassLoader.java:86)	at org.apache.cassandra.triggers.CustomClassLoader.<init>(CustomClassLoader.java:65)	at org.apache.cassandra.triggers.TriggerExecutor.reloadClasses(TriggerExecutor.java:64)	at org.apache.cassandra.service.StorageProxy.reloadTriggerClasses(StorageProxy.java:2709)
evidence: On cassandra:4.1.0 (single pod, repro-18264, release_version verified 4.1.0): copied a 78439-byte jar to /etc/cassandra/triggers/mytrigger.jar, then `nodetool reloadtriggers` failed with exit code 2 and printed the verbatim signature above (error: /tmp/lib/cassandra-0.jar; java.nio.file.FileAlreadyExistsException at Files.copy -> CustomClassLoader.addClassPath(CustomClassLoader.java:86) -> CustomClassLoader.<init>(line 65) -> TriggerExecutor.reloadClasses(line 64) -> StorageProxy.reloadTriggerClasses(line 2709)). system.log showed "CustomClassLoader.java:83 - Loading new jar /etc/cassandra/triggers/mytrigger.jar" immediately before the crash. The temp file /tmp/lib/cassandra-0.jar (created by createTempFile's loop, num=0) is exactly the file that copy() reports as already existing. Source verified: CustomClassLoader.java line 38 `import static java.nio.file.Files.*;`, line 81 createTempFile, line 86 copy() with no REPLACE_EXISTING; FileUtils.createTempFile loops on candidate.createFileIfNotExists() (FileUtils.java:152), physically creating the dest.
control: A/B with the FIXED image cassandra:4.1.1 (fixVersions include 4.1.1; ceiling for 4.1 line is 11, so 4.1.1 is valid). Same namespace, pod replaced with 4.1.1 (release_version verified 4.1.1), IDENTICAL workload (same 78439-byte jar in /etc/cassandra/triggers, same `nodetool reloadtriggers`). Result: exit code 0 on both the first and a second reload; /tmp/lib contained cassandra-0.jar AND cassandra-1.jar each with the full 78439 bytes (successfully copied, not 0-byte temps); NO FileAlreadyExistsException / FSWriteError in system.log, only clean "Loading new jar" INFO lines. Source diff confirms the fix: 4.1.1 calls copy(inputJar.toPath(), out.toPath(), StandardCopyOption.REPLACE_EXISTING).
notes: The bug fires on ANY .jar in the triggers dir, independent of the jar's contents/validity, because the failing copy happens before any class is loaded. No JDK/trigger class was needed (and none is available in the JRE-only cassandra image nor on the host) — using any jar plus `nodetool reloadtriggers` is the minimal deterministic trigger and matches the reporter's claim that "the possibility of loading any trigger" is broken, not a specific trigger. Disk/data footprint: a single ~78KB jar; well within budget. The bug would equivalently surface on `CREATE TRIGGER ... USING '<class>'` (which also reaches TriggerExecutor), but `nodetool reloadtriggers` isolates the exact addClassPath copy failure with a clean exit code and stack trace.
tag_correction: none — classifier hint was accurate: topology=1node, trigger = load a trigger from a JAR on 4.1.0 -> CustomClassLoader fails to copy JAR because Files.copy won't overwrite. Confirmed verbatim against the Jira body and source. Confidence M was conservative; this is a deterministic, fully reproduced bug with a clean A/B control.
tooling_findings: None. Standard kind cluster, kubectl exec, and public cassandra:4.1.0 / cassandra:4.1.1 images all worked as expected. Note (not a bug): foreground `sleep` is blocked by the Bash tool guard; used `kubectl wait` and `until` loops instead. The cassandra Docker image ships a JRE only (no javac/jar), which would matter for bugs requiring a compiled trigger class, but this bug does not need one.

trigger: Create a SASI custom index with option max_compaction_flush_memory_in_mb='1' (on 3.11.7 this is used directly as a 1-BYTE segment-flush threshold, missing the *1048576 multiplier). Load ~100k small rows across 3 flushed batches (=>3 SSTables), then nodetool compact. The COMPACTION-path SASI index rebuild flushes one OnDiskIndex segment file per posting (~1 segment per row), producing 64k+ temp files; the merge mmaps them and exhausts vm.max_map_count (65530), throwing OutOfMemoryError: Map failed -> native memory exhaustion -> JVM killed -> pod restart.
verbatim_signature:
ERROR [SASI-General:9] 2026-06-12 05:52:24,045 PerSSTableIndexWriter.java:262 - Failed to build index segment /var/lib/cassandra/data/repro16071/t-7f347680661f11f1ad685f61e1a30048/md-6-big-SI_t_v_sasi.db_64360
org.apache.cassandra.io.FSReadError: java.io.IOException: Map failed
	at org.apache.cassandra.io.util.ChannelProxy.map(ChannelProxy.java:157) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.7.jar:3.11.7]
	at org.apache.cassandra.index.sasi.utils.MappedBuffer.<init>(MappedBuffer.java:78) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.7.jar:3.11.7]
	at org.apache.cassandra.index.sasi.disk.OnDiskIndex.<init>(OnDiskIndex.java:145) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.7.jar:3.11.7]
	at org.apache.cassandra.index.sasi.disk.PerSSTableIndexWriter$Index.lambda$scheduleSegmentFlush$0(PerSSTableIndexWriter.java:258) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.7.jar:3.11.7]
Caused by: java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Map failed
	at sun.nio.ch.FileChannelImpl.map0(Native Method) ~[na:1.8.0_262]
	at sun.nio.ch.FileChannelImpl.map(FileChannelImpl.java:935) ~[na:1.8.0_262]
evidence: Buggy 3.11.7 (single pod in repro-16071): SASI index t_v_sasi created with max_compaction_flush_memory_in_mb='1' (confirmed in system_schema.indexes). Calibration: 10k rows -> exactly 10,000 temp segment files (SI_t_v_sasi.db_0.._9999), proving ~1 segment per row at threshold=1 byte. Scaled to 100k rows + nodetool compact: segment number climbed 1:1 to ~64,360 where vm.max_map_count (65530, baseline ~1455 maps) was exhausted. 72 occurrences of 'OutOfMemoryError: Map failed' via ChannelProxy.map -> MappedBuffer.<init> -> OnDiskIndex.<init> -> PerSSTableIndexWriter$Index.lambda$scheduleSegmentFlush. JVM then hit fatal 'insufficient memory ... Native memory allocation (malloc) failed', process killed (exitCode 1, reason Error), pod restartCount=1. Highest segment reached 64429. Source-confirmed root cause: IndexMode.java parses the option as Long.parseLong(...) with NO *1MB; PerSSTableIndexWriter.java:363 returns it as the byte threshold for the COMPACTION branch (FLUSH uses hardcoded 1GB). Full pre-crash log preserved at /tmp/repro-16071-prev.log (35419 lines); OOM trace at /tmp/repro-16071-oom-trace.txt.
control: A/B with fixed image cassandra:3.11.8 (3.11.8 <= 3.11 ceiling 19), IDENTICAL namespace/schema/workload. Same SASI index option max_compaction_flush_memory_in_mb='1' (verified in system_schema.indexes), same 100,000-row load in flushed batches, same nodetool compact. Result: compaction COMPLETED in <15s (pending tasks: 0); only 50 temp segments created total (vs 64,000+ on 3.11.7), merged into a single md-6-big-SI_t_v_sasi.db; 0 'Map failed'/OOM log lines; pod restartCount=0 (no crash); SASI query correct (SELECT id WHERE v LIKE 'val_00012345%' -> 12345). The ~1280x segment-count difference matches the fix adding the missing 1048576L* multiplier (IndexMode parses 1048576L*Long.parseLong(...), field renamed maxCompactionFlushMemoryInBytes). Source-level control corroborated by reading both 3.11.7 and 3.11.8 trees.
notes: Image side-load workaround (Cassandra still ran as kind PODS, no docker run): Docker Hub returned 429 rate-limit on the pod image pull, and `kind load docker-image/image-archive` failed with 'content digest not found' due to the multi-arch manifest. Resolved by `docker save cassandra:3.11.7` (and 3.11.8) to a tarball then `docker exec <node> ctr --namespace=k8s.io images import --snapshotter=overlayfs - < tar` on all 4 kind nodes; pods used imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent. This respects the 'no docker directly' constraint (the running DB is a kubelet pod). vm.max_map_count on kind nodes = 65530 (Linux default), which set the ~64k-row target; I did NOT modify it. Used option value '1' instead of the report's '512' purely to make 1 segment ~= 1 row so the OOM is reachable at ~64k rows / a few MB rather than far more data; same buggy branch, same 1048576x ratio. Single-node topology is correct (purely local compaction; no ring needed). Cloned source verified at github cassandra-3.11.7 / 3.11.8.
tag_correction: none — hint CONFIRMED. topology=1node correct; trigger mechanism (value used as bytes due to missing *1MB -> SASI compaction creates millions of temp segment files -> max_map_count exhaustion -> OOM Map Error) correct. Only methodological deviation: used max_compaction_flush_memory_in_mb='1' instead of '512' to make 1 segment ~= 1 row, so the OOM fires at ~64k rows (few MB) given the kind nodes' default vm.max_map_count=65530; this exercises the identical buggy code path, not a different bug.

trigger: Bring a single cassandra:4.1.0 pod fully UP with disk_failure_policy: die (set via sed in /etc/cassandra/cassandra.yaml). After the node is UN and cqlsh answers, inject a runtime FSError: CREATE KEYSPACE/TABLE, INSERT+flush to create the table data dir, then `chmod 000` that dir as root via kubectl exec (server runs as uid 999), INSERT a fresh row (succeeds via memtable+commitlog), and `nodetool flush`. The flush attempts to create an sstable/log replica in the inaccessible dir, raising an FSReadError on the runtime path.
verbatim_signature:
ERROR [MemtableFlushWriter:1] 2026-06-12 05:55:37,410 JVMStabilityInspector.java:254 - JVM state determined to be unstable.  Exiting forcefully due to:
org.apache.cassandra.io.FSReadError: java.io.IOException: Invalid folder descriptor trying to create log replica /var/lib/cassandra/data/repro18294/t-4cbcb4c0662311f19e51b1b044ec7e05
	at org.apache.cassandra.db.lifecycle.LogReplica.create(LogReplica.java:61)
evidence: The Jira body's symptom (with disk_failure_policy=die, a runtime FS error makes Cassandra "only throw exception instead of shut down gossip/transport and kill JVM") does NOT reproduce on the buggy 4.1.0 image — the OPPOSITE (correct, documented) behavior was observed: the JVM was killed forcefully. Source proof (shallow clone of cassandra-4.1.0): every runtime FSError producer (LogReplica/LogTransaction/Hints) calls FileUtils.handleFSErrorAndPropagate(e), which runs JVMStabilityInspector.inspectThrowable(e) FIRST. inspectThrowable sets isUnstable=true for (policy==die && FSError) and then `if (isUnstable) killer.killCurrentJVM(t)` -> Killer.killCurrentJVM ends in StorageService.removeShutdownHook(); System.exit(100) and never returns. The buggy DefaultFSErrorHandler.handleFSError switch (which in 4.1.0 lacks a `case die:` and hits `default: throw new IllegalStateException()`) is only reached via inspectThrowable's later `fn.accept(t)` -> inspectDiskError -> FileUtils.handleFSError — i.e. AFTER the kill block — so it is unreachable for die. Empirically: deployed cassandra:4.1.0 in ns repro-18294, confirmed disk_failure_policy=die (grep of cassandra.yaml and Config.java log line), node UN, Native Transport active: true. Triggered the FSError via chmod 000 of the table data dir + flush. `nodetool flush` exited 137; the PREVIOUS container log shows the verbatim_signature line (JVMStabilityInspector "Exiting forcefully" on an FSReadError from LogReplica.create); pod RestartCount went 0->1. `grep -c IllegalStateException` over that log = 0 — the buggy handleFSError default branch was never hit. The fix's own unit test DefaultFSErrorHandlerTest.testFSErrors (4.1.1) confirms the shadowing: it calls handler.handleFSError(...) DIRECTLY (bypassing inspectThrowable) and its @BeforeClass comment states "startup must be completed, otherwise FS error will kill JVM regardless of failure policy".
control: A/B control via within-version/source reasoning (4.1.1 deploy intentionally skipped to conserve budget; it would show no client-visible delta). The 4.1.1 fix modifies ONLY DefaultFSErrorHandler.handleFSError/handleStartupFSError (adds `case die:` -> StorageService.instance.stopTransports()); it does NOT touch JVMStabilityInspector.inspectThrowable or FileUtils.handleFSErrorAndPropagate. Because the runtime JVM kill happens inside inspectThrowable BEFORE handleFSError is ever reached, the identical workload (chmod 000 + flush) kills the JVM the same way on both 4.1.0 and 4.1.1 — so there is no observable runtime A/B difference. The fix only changes the directly-invoked handleFSError path (the unit-test path), where 4.1.0 throws IllegalStateException and 4.1.1 stops transports. Note: the fix makes die behave like stop_paranoid (stopTransports) and does NOT itself System.exit, despite the doc/title wording about killing the JVM.
notes: The code defect is REAL but shadowed at runtime. There is a genuine secondary truth of needs-fix-test: the ONLY way to exercise the buggy missing-`die` branch in handleFSError is the fix's own unit test calling handleFSError directly, deliberately bypassing JVMStabilityInspector.inspectThrowable (which otherwise kills the JVM regardless of policy). However, the primary disposition is not-reproducible (not not-observable and not needs-fix-test) because I have positive empirical evidence on the real buggy image that the body's mechanism does not fire: the runtime FS error produced the correct documented kill (exit 137, "Exiting forcefully"), and zero IllegalStateException — I did NOT reach the buggy handleFSError die-branch, I was diverted to the kill. So the report's user/operator-visible claim ("die will not kill jvm / only throws") is contradicted on 4.1.0 at runtime; the missing-case fix is effectively dead-code/correctness hardening for the die policy. Smallest dataset used (2 rows); disk was a non-issue.
tag_correction: The classifier hint trigger ("disk_failure_policy=die + inject filesystem error -> Cassandra only throws instead of shutting down gossip/transport and killing the JVM") is CONTRADICTED on the buggy 4.1.0 image. At runtime the JVM IS killed correctly (System.exit(100) via JVMStabilityInspector.killCurrentJVM, observed as exit 137 + "JVM state determined to be unstable. Exiting forcefully"), because every runtime FSError is intercepted by JVMStabilityInspector.inspectThrowable BEFORE the buggy DefaultFSErrorHandler.handleFSError switch runs. The "only throws IllegalStateException" path is dead/shadowed for the die policy at runtime — reachable only by calling handleFSError directly as the fix's unit test does. Topology hint (1node) was correct.

trigger: 2-node ring, RF=2, cassandra:3.11.7. Inject per-replica divergence via gossip isolation (nodetool disablegossip on the peer; hinted_handoff_enabled=false + nodetool disablehandoff on both) writing at CONSISTENCY ONE. Node1(cass-0) only: INSERT (pk=1,c=1) USING TIMESTAMP 9; DELETE (pk=0,c=0) USING TIMESTAMP 10; INSERT (pk=2,c=2) USING TIMESTAMP 9. Node2(cass-1) only: DELETE (pk=1,c=1) USING TIMESTAMP 10; INSERT (pk=0,c=0) USING TIMESTAMP 9; DELETE (pk=2,c=2) USING TIMESTAMP 10. Table: CREATE TABLE k15459.t (pk int, c int, PRIMARY KEY (pk,c)) WITH read_repair_chance=0 AND dclocal_read_repair_chance=0. Re-enable gossip so both are UN, then run the FIRST query: CONSISTENCY ALL; SELECT pk, c FROM k15459.t GROUP BY pk LIMIT 1. Correct merged result is (0 rows) since every partition's delete@ts10 beats its insert@ts9; buggy coordinator returns a deleted row.
evidence: Buggy 3.11.7 money query `CONSISTENCY ALL; SELECT pk, c FROM k15459.t GROUP BY pk LIMIT 1` returned the deleted row [0,0] with (1 rows) instead of (0 rows) — matching Jira "Expect no live data, but got [0, 0]" exactly. Physical divergence proven by sstabledump: cass-0 Data.db = pk=1 live tstamp 0.000009Z, pk=0 row tombstone marked_deleted 0.000010Z, pk=2 live tstamp 0.000009Z; cass-1 Data.db = pk=1 tombstone @ts10, pk=0 live @ts9, pk=2 tombstone @ts10. Merged truth: all 3 partitions dead (delete@10 > insert@9). Corroboration it is the SRP group-vs-row counting bug: unbounded `SELECT pk,c FROM k15459.t GROUP BY pk` at CL=ALL returned (0 rows); re-running GROUP BY LIMIT 1 (after the first query's blocking read-repair partially reconciled state) returned a DIFFERENT dead row [2,2] with (1 rows) — the wrong row shifts as RR reconciles, not a fixed stale value.
control: A/B fixed-image control. Tore down the 3.11.7 ring, deployed an IDENTICAL 2-node ring on cassandra:3.11.8 (the fix version; ceiling 3.11->19 so 8<=19) in the same namespace. Both nodes confirmed release_version 3.11.8, both UN, same handoff-disabled config and same table. Re-injected identical divergence (verified: cass-0 local CL ONE = rows (1,1),(2,2) live + pk=0 tombstone; cass-1 local CL ONE = row (0,0) only). SAME query `CONSISTENCY ALL; SELECT pk, c FROM k15459.t GROUP BY pk LIMIT 1` on 3.11.8 returned VERBATIM: `(0 rows)` (with the same "Aggregation query used without partition key" warning). Identical workload + divergence + query; only the version differs => isolates the defect to the SRP group-by counting fix in CASSANDRA-15459.
notes: Mechanism: coordinator-side Short Read Protection (Legacy/Coordination). With per-replica divergence a GROUP BY ... LIMIT query short-reads; pre-fix SRP recomputes the follow-up limit using ROW count instead of GROUP count, stops early, and surfaces a partition (pk=0) that is actually deleted on merge. Used the smallest possible dataset (3 partitions, single-cell PK-only rows) — no bulk data. StatefulSet used ephemeral storage (no PVC). CQL gotchas observed and worked around (not bugs): a trailing standalone ';' triggers a benign SyntaxException; statements in a single -e must be ';'-separated; writetime() cannot be selected on a PRIMARY KEY column.
tag_correction: none — classifier hints were accurate. topology=ring confirmed by Jira body (real cassandra-dtest, coordinator merge of divergent replicas), confidence H justified, and the one-line trigger matched the body's reproducer (2-node RF=2, per-replica divergent writes/deletes, SELECT ... GROUP BY pk LIMIT 1 returning the deleted [0,0] row). Not in-jvm-only and not single-node.

The provided multi-node ring template, single-pod template, and config-gating approach (appending to /etc/cassandra/cassandra.yaml before docker-entrypoint.sh) all worked as documented on cassandra:3.11.7 and 3.11.8. Note for operators (not a defect): full StatefulSet readiness takes ~4-6 min per ring because the readiness probe has initialDelaySeconds=90 and node-2 must finish JOINING/bootstrap (a fixed 30s "pending range setup" sleep) before its CQL port opens; the pod Ready flag also lags the in-ring UN state by a few probe cycles.

topology: 2-node Cassandra 4.1.0 ring (StatefulSet, ephemeral storage) in kind ns repro-12525; PasswordAuthenticator + CassandraAuthorizer enabled via entrypoint patch; system_auth = NetworkTopologyStrategy {dc1:2}, full repair run on both nodes (both UN).
trigger: Auth-enabled cluster; operator runs `ALTER ROLE cassandra WITH PASSWORD='password'` (write timestamp T1). The buggy CassandraRoleManager.setupDefaultRole() then re-INSERTs the default cassandra superuser with NO explicit timestamp (= current wall-clock T2 > T1). Last-Write-Wins makes the default cassandra/cassandra password overwrite the altered one. Reproduced deterministically by replaying setupDefaultRole's exact createDefaultRoleQuery() INSERT on the live ring; the report's 10-node add/decommission flow is just one way a freshly-joining node runs that INSERT after the ALTER (hasExistingRoles() reads empty before system_auth replicates).
verbatim_signature: Connection error: ('Unable to connect to any servers', {'127.0.0.1:9042': AuthenticationFailed('Failed to authenticate to 127.0.0.1:9042: Error from server: code=0100 [Bad credentials] message="Provided username cassandra and/or password are incorrect"')})
evidence: Two independent lines, both in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-12525.md. (1) Primary-source bytecode of the shipped images, read inside kind pods: cassandra:4.1.0 default-role INSERT = `INSERT INTO %s.%s (role, is_superuser, can_login, salted_hash) VALUES ('%s', true, true, '%s')` with `USING TIMESTAMP 0 count: 0`; cassandra:4.1.1 = identical INSERT but `... USING TIMESTAMP 0` with count 1 — exactly fix commit 8ecd7616fe5d3ce0cfe8f4621eda1905a9110db1. (2) Behavioral A/B on the live 4.1.0 ring (auth cache settled between steps): Step0 ALTER applied, writetime T1=1781247302665000, login cassandra/password=OK, cassandra/cassandra=FAIL. Step1 BUGGY form (INSERT, no timestamp) -> writetime T2=1781247309373971 (>T1), login cassandra/password=FAIL, cassandra/cassandra=OK (default reverted). Step2 FIXED form (`USING TIMESTAMP 0`) after restoring ALTER -> writetime=1781247316240000 (real ts), login cassandra/password=OK, cassandra/cassandra=FAIL (preserved). The verbatim Bad-credentials error above is the client-facing result of trying the previously-set 'password' after the buggy INSERT, while `SELECT release_version` returns 4.1.0 over a session authenticated with the reverted default cassandra/cassandra.
control: A/B both ways. (a) Bytecode diff isolates the entire fix to one clause: 4.1.0 INSERT (no timestamp) vs 4.1.1 `USING TIMESTAMP 0`. (b) Running the FIXED 4.1.1 query form (`... USING TIMESTAMP 0`) on the same ring under the identical workload left the altered password intact (cassandra/password=OK, default=FAIL), because a TIMESTAMP-0 write can never win LWW against a real ALTER (timestamp>0). cassandra:4.1.1 image exists and is <= the 4.1 ceiling (11), so a fixed control is valid; the query-form A/B exercises the exact one-line code difference between the two images.
notes: The bug is a Last-Write-Wins timestamp defect in setupDefaultRole, not a ring-size-specific bug, so 10 nodes (infeasible on this host: ~17 GiB free, ~2.5 GiB/node) are not required — the literal buggy statement and its client-visible password reversion were reproduced on a 2-node ring. The genuine 5->10->decommission race is just one trigger that makes a joining node run that INSERT after the operator's ALTER (hasExistingRoles() can read empty before system_auth streams/repairs). Fidelity caveat: I drove createDefaultRoleQuery()'s exact statement directly rather than forcing the streaming race in-JVM, which would be timing-dependent and over budget; the symptom and mechanism are identical.
tag_correction: HINT mostly correct. topology=ring confirmed (auth must be cluster-replicated). The hint trigger ("add new nodes + decommission originals -> credentials revert to default") is an accurate SYMPTOM but the precise mechanism is an LWW timestamp on the default-superuser INSERT in CassandraRoleManager.setupDefaultRole (4.1.0 has no `USING TIMESTAMP`, fixed to `USING TIMESTAMP 0` in 4.1.1); node-join is merely one way to make hasExistingRoles() read empty after the ALTER. confidence=H confirmed.
