control: A/B control on FIXED cassandra:3.11.12 (fix landed in 3.11.11; 3.11.12 = buggy-patch+2, within ceiling 19). Ran the IDENTICAL sequence in the same namespace WITH allocate_tokens_for_keyspace: k, with the precondition verified identical (gossipinfo: seed 10.244.1.132 NEW schema d5f43817-..., removed cassB 10.244.3.108 lingering STATUS:LEFT with OLD schema e84b6a60-...). RESULT: cassC(3.11.12) JOINED CLEANLY - it passed the same schema wait ('INFO StorageService.java:1568 - JOINING: schema complete, ready to bootstrap' then 'JOINING: getting bootstrap token' -> 'JOINING: Finish joining ring' -> 'Starting listening for CQL clients on /0.0.0.0:9042'), nodetool status showed 2 UN nodes (cassC=10.244.3.110 at 50% ownership), pod stayed Running, and grep -c "Didn't receive schemas" over its log = 0. Clean buggy-vs-fixed contrast confirming the fix repairs the waitForSchema/outstandingVersions logic.
notes: Verified the buggy crash call site is joinTokenRing:987 -> waitForSchema:947 (the general join-time schema wait), NOT the Jira's allocateTokens:206 site - the captured stack has NO BootStrapper.allocateTokens frame. waitForSchema is at :947 in both the Jira and my run (same source), but the call site differs (987 < 1073). I ran a DISCRIMINATING TEST (3rd deploy cycle): identical setup but cassC launched WITHOUT allocate_tokens_for_keyspace (config confirmed allocate_tokens_for_keyspace=null) - it crashed with the IDENTICAL signature and call site (WARN outstanding {e84b6a60-...=[/10.244.3.112]}, joinTokenRing:987 -> waitForSchema:947). So on this Docker Hub 3.11.10 build allocate_tokens_for_keyspace is NOT the necessary trigger; the necessary trigger is a removed node lingering in gossip with a schema diverging from current (keyspace created after the node left) + any new node bootstrapping. The allocate path the Jira describes is a second call site of the same defect. This makes the bug easier to trigger than the Jira states and the A/B contrast cleaner. Only ONE removed node is mechanically required (single outstanding version, = Jira's single /127.0.0.3). 3 deploy cycles used: (1) buggy WITH allocate -> crash, (2) fixed 3.11.12 WITH allocate -> clean join, (3) buggy WITHOUT allocate -> crash. All within budget.
tag_correction: topology=ring is CORRECT. Trigger hint ('decommission/remove 2 nodes + allocate_tokens_for_keyspace + re-add -> startup fails on schema-agreement timeout') is the Jira reporter's specific path but is NOT the necessary trigger on the Docker Hub cassandra:3.11.10 image: a discriminating test showed cassC crashes with the identical schema-agreement-timeout signature even with allocate_tokens_for_keyspace UNSET, because the abort fires at the general join-time schema wait (joinTokenRing:987 -> waitForSchema:947) rather than the allocateTokens path (allocateTokens:206). The real necessary trigger is just: a decommissioned/removed node still lingering in gossip (STATUS:LEFT) whose stale schema diverges from the cluster's current schema (created a keyspace AFTER the node left), then any node bootstraps. Also only ONE removed-and-stays-removed node is needed (not two); ccm removed two only because it re-added node2 into its old slot, whereas I used a fresh-identity bootstrapper.
tooling_findings: 1. 'kind load docker-image cassandra:3.11.10 cassandra:3.11.12' FAILED with: ERROR failed to load image ... ctr 'content digest sha256:... not found' (the multi-platform manifest + 'ctr images import --all-platforms --digests' path chokes on these images). Partial loads left images on only some nodes. Worked around (no repo edits) with: docker save IMG -o /tmp/x.tar ; docker exec -i <kind-node> ctr --namespace=k8s.io images import - < /tmp/x.tar, per worker node. 2. nodetool on the cassandra:3.11.12 image (JDK Temurin 1.8.0_332) fails EVERY invocation with: nodetool: Failed to connect to '127.0.0.1:7199' - URISyntaxException: 'Malformed IPv6 address at index 7: rmi://[127.0.0.1]:7199'. This is the JDK 8u331+ RMI URL-parsing regression (it brackets even an IPv4 address). The cassandra:3.11.10 image (JDK 1.8.0_292) is unaffected. The Cassandra daemon/bootstrap is fine on both images; only the nodetool RMI CLIENT breaks. Worked around with: export JVM_OPTS="-Dcom.sun.jndi.rmiURLParsing=legacy" before nodetool. Unrelated to CASSANDRA-16577. This may affect any SREGym oracle/health-check that shells out to nodetool on 3.11.11+/3.11.12 images.

trigger: In a Cassandra ring: shut down one node, then create a new keyspace/table on a surviving node (bumping the cluster schema version) while the dead node still carries the old/stale schema in gossip. Then replace the terminated node via -Dcassandra.replace_address_first_boot=<deadNodeIP>. On 3.11.10 the replacement node's startup blocks on schema agreement across ALL known endpoints (including the dead node it is replacing, whose stale schema can never reconcile) and dies on a timeout. Minimal faithful repro = 2-node ring (1 seed + 1 victim) + 1 replacement pod.
verbatim_signature:
java.lang.RuntimeException: Didn't receive schemas for all known versions within the timeout
	at org.apache.cassandra.service.StorageService.waitForSchema(StorageService.java:947) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.10.jar:3.11.10]
	at org.apache.cassandra.service.StorageService.joinTokenRing(StorageService.java:987) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.10.jar:3.11.10]
evidence: Built a 2-node ring on cassandra:3.11.10 in ns repro-16692 (seed 10.244.2.112, victim 10.244.1.133), both UN, single schema e84b6a60-24cf-30ca-9b58-452d92911703. Deleted (NOT decommissioned) the victim pod; seed marked it DN while its gossip entry persisted (nodetool gossipinfo on /10.244.1.133: STATUS shutdown,true; SCHEMA e84b6a60-... = stale V0). Created keyspace repro16692_ks on the seed -> seed schema bumped to V1 9720161e-bbcb-30a5-b369-d80903d72666 (confirmed divergence in gossipinfo: dead victim still e84b6a60-..., live seed 9720161e-...). Deployed a replacement pod (3.11.10, NOT a seed, fresh ephemeral data) with env JVM_EXTRA_OPTS=-Dcassandra.replace_address_first_boot=10.244.1.133; verified the flag took (it appears in the replacement's "JVM Arguments: [... -Dcassandra.replace_address_first_boot=10.244.1.133 ...]"). The replacement went JOINING: waiting for ring information, then at 2026-06-12 07:29:53 threw, verbatim: "ERROR [main] CassandraDaemon.java:803 - Exception encountered during startup / java.lang.RuntimeException: Didn't receive schemas for all known versions within the timeout / at org.apache.cassandra.service.StorageService.waitForSchema(StorageService.java:947) / at org.apache.cassandra.service.StorageService.joinTokenRing(StorageService.java:987) / at ...initServer(StorageService.java:753/687) / at ...CassandraDaemon.setup(395)/activate(633)/main(786)". Pod ended phase=Failed, container exitCode=3 (message appears 3x in the saved log). Matches the Jira body exactly (same message + waitForSchema/joinTokenRing/initServer frames); only line numbers differ because the report was a Netflix nf-cassandra-3.0.24.1 build vs stock apache-cassandra-3.11.10. Failure is pre-streaming (joinTokenRing -> waitForSchema), so an empty keyspace and a few rows suffice; no cassandra-stress, ephemeral storage, well under disk budget. Raw replacement log saved to /tmp/repro16692_replacement_3.11.10.log.
control: A/B control on the FIXED image cassandra:3.11.11 (buggy patch+1; 3.11 ceiling=19 so A/B possible). Repeated the IDENTICAL sequence (cluster repro16692c: seed 10.244.1.135 + victim 10.244.3.111 ring; deleted victim -> DN with retained stale gossip schema e84b6a60-...; created keyspace repro16692c_ks on seed -> schema cf57c621-...; deployed replacement 3.11.11 with -Dcassandra.replace_address_first_boot=10.244.3.111). Result: ZERO occurrences of "Didn't receive schemas for all known versions within the timeout" (vs 3 on 3.11.10). The fixed node recognized the down node and did NOT block on it (WARN MigrationCoordinator.java:506 - Can't send schema pull request: node ... is down), then progressed: "JOINING: schema complete, ready to bootstrap" -> "JOINING: Replacing a node with token(s): [...]" -> "JOINING: Starting to bootstrap..." -> "Bootstrap completed for tokens [...]" -> "Node /10.244.2.113 will complete replacement of /10.244.3.111 ...". Post-replacement nodetool status on the seed shows 2 UN with the new replacement 10.244.2.113 joined (50.5% ownership), confirming it took over the dead victim's range. So the bug is version-correlated: dies on 3.11.10, succeeds on 3.11.11 with the same topology/workload/flag. This matches the fix (CASSANDRA-16692 exempts the replaced node from the all-endpoints schema-agreement wait introduced by CASSANDRA-15158). Full fixed log saved to /tmp/repro16692_replacement_3.11.11_FIXED.log.
notes: Reproducer faithful to the Jira body's 3 steps. Used standalone pods (not the multi-node StatefulSet template) because node replacement needs per-pod control: capture victim IP before deletion, let the seed mark it DN (NOT decommission/removenode, which would purge gossip state and erase the stale schema), then bring up a separate replacement pod with the replace flag and fresh data. Mechanism: CASSANDRA-15158 made startup wait for schema agreement across all known endpoints including the down node being replaced; buggy 3.11.10 has 15158 but not the 16692 fix. The control's transient "node /10.244.1.135 (seed) is down" WARN at startup is normal pre-convergence gossip and resolved immediately (next line is a successful schema pull from the seed, then schema-complete/bootstrap/join) — it is not the bug and does not weaken the differential. Side observation (expected Cassandra behavior, not the bug and not a tooling issue): on BOTH versions, running CREATE KEYSPACE on the seed while the peer is down returns cqlsh NoHostAvailable plus "schema version mismatch detected" warnings, yet the DDL still applies locally and the schema version bumps (DDL waits for cross-node schema agreement that the dead node prevents).
tag_correction: none - the classifier hints were correct. topology=ring matched (the bug needs a multi-node ring + node replacement). The hint trigger ("shut down a node, change schema on others, then replace the stale-schema terminated node -> replacement startup fails on schema-agreement timeout") matched the Jira body and the observed failure exactly. confidence=H was justified.
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling issues observed. (Cosmetic only: the harness reset/removed my TaskCreate task list mid-run so the final TaskUpdate calls returned "Task not found"; no impact on the reproduction. The cqlsh NoHostAvailable on DDL-with-a-down-peer is expected Cassandra behavior, not tooling.)

topology: 2-node ring (StatefulSet, RF=2 SimpleStrategy) on kind, namespace repro-16671, keyspace ks16671. Matches classifier hint topology=ring.
trigger: On a 2-node ring with gossip-isolated nodes (clean bidirectional DN): cass-0 gets INSERT (USING TIMESTAMP 1000, sets PK liveness) -> flush -> UPDATE SET v (USING TIMESTAMP 2000, covers all non-PK columns, no PK liveness) -> flush, producing 2 sstables; cass-1 gets DELETE v (USING TIMESTAMP 3000) -> flush. Then SELECT * FROM ks16671.tbl WHERE pk=1 AND ck='1' at CONSISTENCY ALL coordinated from cass-1. The timestamp-ordered read path stops early on the all-columns UPDATE sstable, dropping PK liveness; merged with the other replica's column deletion the coordinator drops the whole row.
evidence: On buggy cassandra:3.11.10, `SELECT * FROM ks16671.tbl WHERE pk=1 AND ck='1'` at CONSISTENCY ALL (coordinator cass-1, first read) returned `(0 rows)` — the row was WRONGLY dropped. The same read should return row(1,'1',null) per CQL semantics (the row still has live PK columns pk=1, ck='1'). Smoking-gun contradiction: on the same buggy node, `SELECT v ...` at CL=ALL returned `null` (1 rows) — so the row data is present but `SELECT *` regresses. Physical divergence was proven via /opt/cassandra/tools/bin/sstabledump before the read: cass-0 sstable md-1 has liveness_info{tstamp .001Z}+v=1 (INSERT), md-2 has v=2@.002Z with NO liveness_info (all-columns UPDATE); cass-1 has ONLY the v deletion tombstone@.003Z (no live v cell). This exactly matches the in-JVM dtest in the Jira body whose first assertion assertRows(SELECT *, row(1,"1",null)) fails.
control: A/B control on FIXED image cassandra:3.11.11 (fixVersions include 3.11.11). Identical namespace/keyspace/schema/ring, identical gossip-isolation, identical divergent writes (physical state verified identical via sstabledump: cass-0 me-1 liveness_info{.001Z}+v=1, me-2 v=2@.002Z no-liveness, cass-1 v tombstone@.003Z), identical CL=ALL read from cass-1. Result: `SELECT *` returned `1 | 1 | null` (1 rows) — the row is correctly present. `SELECT v` returned `null` (1 rows). The fixed version returns the row; the buggy version drops it. Identical physical sstable state on both isolates the difference to the timestamp-ordered read-path fix (CASSANDRA-16671 reverting the CASSANDRA-16226 early-stop regression).
notes: Reproduced an in-JVM dtest (executeInternal + per-node flush) on a real kind ring using gossip isolation. Key gotcha discovered and worked around: `nodetool disablegossip` freezes a node's OWN failure-detector evaluation, so after disabling gossip on both nodes, cass-0 would never convict cass-1 (asymmetric: cass-1 saw cass-0 DN but cass-0 saw cass-1 UN). Fix: re-enable gossip on cass-0 only (cass-1 stays silent) so cass-0's FD convicts cass-1, achieving clean bidirectional DN before the isolated writes. Hinted handoff disabled throughout; each isolated write at CL=ONE landed local-only (sstabledump-confirmed no leakage). First read captured (CL=ALL triggers read-repair). Used minimal data (1 row), no stress, ephemeral storage — negligible disk. Both buggy and fixed images were already preloaded on kind worker nodes (no pull needed).
tag_correction: none — classifier hint topology=ring, confidence=H, and the trigger (UPDATE covering all columns on node1 + column DELETE on node2 + SELECT at ALL -> expected row not returned) all matched the Jira body and reproduced exactly.
tooling_findings: None specific to SREGym tooling. Operational note: the multi-node ring template's readinessProbe has initialDelaySeconds=90 with podManagementPolicy OrderedReady, so a 2-node ring takes ~3+ minutes to fully roll out (cass-0 ready then cass-1 starts); a functioning node can be detected sooner by exec'ing nodetool status + cqlsh directly rather than waiting on the k8s readiness gate. sstabledump is not on PATH in the cassandra:3.11.x image; full path is /opt/cassandra/tools/bin/sstabledump. The shared SREGym task list (TaskList/TaskUpdate) was concurrently mutated/owned by other co-running repro agents (tasks appeared/disappeared and referenced unrelated bugs), so it was unreliable for this session's tracking; did not modify it beyond my own attempts. RECORD-ONLY respected: no repo/tooling/Cassandra files edited.

topology: single (no deploy needed — source-based determination; underlying bug topology is a 2-node RF=1 ring, but it does not apply to 4.1.3)
trigger: 2-node RF=1: decommission node1 while looping `nodetool cleanup` on node2. Intended mechanism: cleanup computes ownership from LOCAL ranges only, ignoring the PENDING ranges node2 holds for streamed-in data during decommission, so just-streamed rows are deleted -> silent data loss.
verbatim_signature: throw new RuntimeException("Node is involved in cluster membership changes. Not safe to run cleanup.");
evidence: Source-based determination from primary source (no deploy needed; race-based dynamic repro on a fixed build would be ambiguous, source is not). CASSANDRA-18824 is a 3.x BACKPORT of CASSANDRA-16418; the body states "already fixed in 4.x with CASSANDRA-16418". The 16418 guard is COMPILED INTO the assigned buggy image cassandra:4.1.3. In src/java/org/apache/cassandra/service/StorageService.java @ tag cassandra-4.1.3, the nodetool-cleanup entry point forceKeyspaceCleanup(int,String,String...) contains: `if (tokenMetadata.getPendingRanges(keyspaceName, getBroadcastAddressAndPort()).size() > 0) throw new RuntimeException("Node is involved in cluster membership changes. Not safe to run cleanup.");`. This runs BEFORE CompactionManager.performCleanup. During node1's decommission, node2 (the only other node, RF=1) receives node1's ranges as PENDING ranges for the user keyspace while streaming; so getPendingRanges(userKeyspace, node2).size() > 0 is true precisely in the reproducer's window, and each cleanup-loop iteration THROWS instead of deleting rows — the data-loss mechanism is shadowed by 16418's validation. The guard is ABSENT in cassandra-4.1.0 (same method, only the system-keyspace check; grep "Not safe to run cleanup": 4.1.0=0, 4.1.3=1, 4.1.4=1). CHANGES.txt @ cassandra-4.1.4: line 146 "Add safeguard so cleanup fails when node has pending ranges (CASSANDRA-16418)" sits under the 4.1.1 section (4.1.1 header line 126, 4.1.0 header line 170) => guard shipped 4.1.1. CASSANDRA-18824 does NOT appear anywhere in 4.1.x CHANGES.txt (grep exit 1) => its 4.1.4 fixVersion is a forward-merge no-op. CompactionManager.performCleanup @ 4.1.3 still uses only `StorageService.instance.getLocalReplicas(...)` (no pending-range awareness), confirming 16418 fixed the bug at the entry point by refusing cleanup, not by changing performCleanup. Conclusion: on cassandra:4.1.3 the STR outcome ("cleanup removed some rows") cannot occur; a live data-loss repro requires <= 4.1.0 (or <= 4.0.7 on the 4.0 line), outside this candidate's assigned version.
control: Within-version reasoning (no dynamic A/B needed; determination is dispositive). The suggested fixed-control image cassandra:4.1.4 carries the IDENTICAL guard as 4.1.3 (grep "Not safe to run cleanup" = 1 in both), so a 4.1.3-vs-4.1.4 A/B would show NO divergence — both reject the cleanup — which itself confirms 4.1.3 is not buggy. The true buggy/fixed pair is cassandra:4.1.0 (no guard -> data loss) vs cassandra:4.1.1 (CASSANDRA-16418 added the guard).
notes: Load-bearing chain: the 16418 guard does not merely EXIST in 4.1.3 — it FIRES in this exact reproducer. With RF=1 on a 2-node ring, decommissioning node1 makes node2 the receiver; node2 holds node1's ranges as PENDING ranges for the user keyspace during streaming, so tokenMetadata.getPendingRanges(userKeyspace, node2).size() > 0 holds throughout the cleanup-loop window, and forceKeyspaceCleanup throws the RuntimeException on every iteration instead of deleting rows. Classic "mechanism shadowed by validation," not merely "a fix shipped somewhere." No pods were deployed and no namespace created — this was a pure primary-source code determination across tags cassandra-4.1.0 / 4.1.3 / 4.1.4. Budget used: orientation + 5 fetch/grep cycles, no deploy cycles.
tag_correction: Topology hint (ring, 2-node RF=1) is correct for the UNDERLYING bug, but the assigned buggy VERSION is wrong. The classifier mechanically computed buggy = 4.1.4 - 1 = 4.1.3 from CASSANDRA-18824's fixVersions, not recognizing that (a) 18824 is a 3.x backport whose 4.1.4 fixVersion is a forward-merge no-op (18824 is absent from 4.1.x CHANGES.txt), and (b) the 4.1 line was actually fixed by the PARENT ticket CASSANDRA-16418 at 4.1.1. Therefore 4.1.3 already contains the fix and is not a buggy image for this bug. A genuine repro target would be cassandra:4.1.0 (4.1 line) or cassandra:4.0.7 (4.0 line, fixed by 16418 at 4.0.8).
tooling_findings: Version-resolution gap in the candidate-generation pipeline: for a BACKPORT ticket, computing buggy = (min fixVersion in the target line) - 1 is incorrect when that line was already fixed by the parent ticket and the backport's fixVersion on that line is only a forward-merge stamp. Here it produced buggy=4.1.3 for a 4.1 line that was fixed at 4.1.1 by CASSANDRA-16418. A more robust resolver would cross-check CHANGES.txt for the ticket key on the target branch (18824 is absent from 4.1.x CHANGES.txt) and/or follow the "backport of <PARENT>" link to find the real first-fixed version per line. RECORD ONLY — not fixed.

topology: single-node Cassandra pod (cassandra:3.11.10) in kind ns repro-12949; no ring needed
trigger: On a table created WITH compression chunk_length_in_kb=64: (1) JMX-set the writable MBean attribute CompressionParameters (= the setCompressionParameters() setter) to chunk_length_in_kb=128 on the node (intended local-only); persisted schema correctly stays 64. (2) Run an UNRELATED ALTER TABLE ... WITH comment='...' that never mentions compression. The unrelated ALTER serializes the in-place-mutated CFMetaData, persisting compression as 128 (and on a cluster would announce/disseminate it).
verbatim_signature: {'chunk_length_in_kb': '128', 'class': 'org.apache.cassandra.io.compress.LZ4Compressor'}
evidence: Buggy 3.11.10: baseline system_schema.tables compression = {'chunk_length_in_kb': '64', 'class': '...LZ4Compressor'}. After JMX setAttribute(CompressionParameters) to 128, getAttribute reports in-memory 128 but persisted schema STILL 64 (JMX correctly local-only). After `ALTER TABLE repro12949_ks.t1 WITH comment='trigger-12949'` (comment only), SELECT compression returns {'chunk_length_in_kb': '128', 'class': '...LZ4Compressor'} and DESCRIBE TABLE shows both `comment = 'trigger-12949'` AND `compression = {... '128' ...}` — the comment-only ALTER silently persisted the JMX-set compression (64->128). All raw outputs in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-12949.md. JMX path: image is JRE-only and host has no Java, so I drove JMX from the JRE's bundled Nashorn `jjs`; setCompressionParameters is exposed as a writable attribute (invoke() gave NoSuchMethodException: setCompressionParameters(java.util.Map)), so setAttribute on org.apache.cassandra.db:type=ColumnFamilies,keyspace=repro12949_ks,columnfamily=t1 via 127.0.0.1:7199.
control: A/B fixed-image control on cassandra:3.11.11 (3.11.11 is in Jira fixVersions; 3.11.10+1=11 <= ceiling 19). Identical workload (same /tmp/jmxset.js, same CQL): baseline 64; after JMX setAttribute, in-memory getAttribute = 128 but persisted schema still 64; after the identical unrelated `ALTER TABLE ... WITH comment='trigger-12949'`, persisted compression STAYS 64 (no leak). Clean isolation: buggy 3.11.10 flips 64->128 on an unrelated ALTER, fixed 3.11.11 stays 64.
notes: Deterministic, reproduced on the first attempt with a matching fixed-version control. Pure schema bug — zero data rows required. The single most-telling artifact is the DESCRIBE pair coupling "I changed only the comment" (comment = 'trigger-12949') with "compression changed" ('128'); the verbatim_signature uses the post-ALTER compression row, which appears literally in the log. Key JMX-access discovery: the cassandra:3.11.x image is JRE-only (no javac) and the host has no Java, so I used the JRE's Nashorn `jjs` to script JMX (no compilation), and used setAttribute on the writable CompressionParameters attribute rather than invoke() (the method is not exposed as a JMX operation in 3.11).
tag_correction: Hint said topology=ring; actual reproduction used a single node. The defect is that an unrelated ALTER persists the JMX-mutated in-memory CFMetaData; the local system_schema write and the peer schema announcement are the same migration mutation, so the locally-persisted leaked value (observable via system_schema/DESCRIBE on one node) is exactly what would disseminate. "Global dissemination" is only the transport, not needed to observe the bug. Hint confidence=M and trigger description were otherwise accurate (JMX setCompressionParameters + later ALTER -> change persisted). Note the JMX surface is a writable attribute, not an invokable operation, in 3.11.
tooling_findings: None. Used only the provided single-node pod template, kubectl, kubectl cp, and the in-image JRE jjs/cqlsh/nodetool. No SREGym repo files or tooling were edited. (Observation, not a defect: the cassandra:3.11.x images are JRE-only, so any JMX-method bug reproduction in this harness must rely on the bundled Nashorn jjs or an external JDK pod + kubectl cp, since invoking JMX with a Map argument has no nodetool/cqlsh path.)

topology: 2-node Cassandra StatefulSet ring in kind (ns repro-20449, image cassandra:5.0.3), keyspace ks20449 RF=2 (NetworkTopologyStrategy dc1:2) so both nodes are replicas; read_repair='NONE'. Control: identical 2-node ring in ns repro-20449-ctrl on cassandra:5.0.4. Both rings showed 2x UN. Ephemeral storage.
trigger: A single UPDATE mutating multiple collection columns where one is a set REPLACEMENT (SET s2={2}, which emits a complex deletion / collection tombstone) and others are APPENDS (s1=s1+{2}, s3=s3+{2}). Coordinator (cass-0) applies it correctly from the in-memory object; the serialized copy sent to the peer drops the s2 complex deletion, so the peer keeps the older INSERT-time tombstone and merges the old element into the replacement -> peer's s2 = {1,2} instead of {2}.
evidence:
Workload (identical on 5.0.3 buggy and 5.0.4 control): CREATE TABLE ks20449.multi_collection (k int,c int,s1 set<int>,s2 set<int>,s3 set<int>, PRIMARY KEY(k,c)) WITH read_repair='NONE'; then via cqlsh on cass-0 at CONSISTENCY ALL: INSERT (0,0,{1},{1},{1}); UPDATE SET s2={2}, s1=s1+{2}, s3=s3+{2} WHERE k=0 AND c=0. Expected on every replica: s1={1,2}, s2={2}, s3={1,2}.

BUGGY 5.0.3: A per-replica CL ONE cqlsh read returned the WRONG merged row ` 0 | 0 | {1, 2} | {1, 2} | {1, 2}` (s2 should be {2}, got {1,2}); the only differing cell across replicas is s2 (the replacement) while s1/s3 (appends) are identically {1,2}. Authoritative per-node truth from sstabledump (after nodetool flush) on each node's local Data.db: cass-0 (coordinator) s2 has complex deletion marked_deleted .402797Z (=UPDATE ts) and only element path [2] -> correct s2={2}. cass-1 (peer) s2 has complex deletion marked_deleted .389227Z (=stale INSERT ts, NOT the UPDATE's .402797Z) and BOTH element paths [1] and [2] survive -> s2={1,2}. The surviving stale element cell on the buggy peer is verbatim: { "name" : "s2", "path" : [ "1" ], "value" : "" }. The s2 complex deletion that accompanied the replacement was lost during Mutation serialization to the peer.

CONTROL 5.0.4: both replicas read s2={2} at CL ONE; the previously-buggy peer (cass-1) sstabledump shows s2 complex deletion at .051552Z (=UPDATE ts) and only element [2] -> s2={2}. Divergence gone. Same workload, same topology, single version difference => bug present on 5.0.3, fixed on 5.0.4.
control: A/B with fixed image cassandra:5.0.4 (5.0.3-patch+1=4 <= 5.0 ceiling 8). Ran the byte-identical workload on a 5.0.4 2-node RF=2 ring in ns repro-20449-ctrl. Result: both nodes' CL ONE reads show s2={2}, and the peer (cass-1) sstabledump shows the s2 complex deletion stamped with the UPDATE timestamp (.051552Z) and only element [2] present. No replica divergence -> the fix is effective; the 5.0.3 divergence is not present on 5.0.4.
notes: Reproduced the exact bug. The Jira body's reproducer is an in-JVM 2-node dtest (testMultipleSetsComplexDeletion); I adapted it to a real kind ring using plain CQL (method change, not a tag change). Per-node identity (cass-0=correct coordinator-side apply, cass-1=buggy serialized-peer) is established SOLELY from sstabledump of each node's local Data.db. Important read-path caveat: the cqlsh-from-pod CL ONE reads were routed by the snitch to a non-local replica, so the cqlsh wrong row ({1,2,1,2,1,2}) only proves 'a CL ONE read can return the merged set' and does NOT deterministically map to a specific pod; the on-disk sstabledump is the authoritative per-node evidence and removes that ambiguity. read_repair='NONE' and the presence of MULTIPLE collections in the row are both load-bearing (the bug loses one complex deletion while iterating several collections). CONSISTENCY was set via the cqlsh session command, not inline. Full log at /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-20449.md.
tag_correction: none
tooling_findings: (1) `sstabledump` is not on PATH in the official cassandra:5.0.x image; it lives at /opt/cassandra/tools/bin/sstabledump. (2) `USING CONSISTENCY <level>` is not valid CQL3 inline syntax (SyntaxException 'no viable alternative at input CONSISTENCY'); consistency must be set as a cqlsh session command (`CONSISTENCY ALL;`) before the statement — relevant if SREGym tooling generates CL-qualified statements. (3) cqlsh exec-from-pod CL ONE reads are snitch-routed and not guaranteed local to the pod, so per-replica verification of divergence bugs should use sstabledump on each node's local Data.db rather than relying on per-pod cqlsh reads. The 2-node ring pod template worked as-is.

topology: 2-node Cassandra ring (StatefulSet cass-0/cass-1) in kind ns repro-20189, RF=2 (NetworkTopologyStrategy dc1:2), ephemeral storage, GossipingPropertyFileSnitch, hinted_handoff_enabled=false. Real-ring translation of the in-JVM dtest.
trigger: Table WITH read_repair='NONE', SAI index on column a. (1) INSERT (k=0,a=1) at CL ALL TS1 -> flush both -> incremental repair (marks a=1 sstable repairedAt>0 on BOTH nodes). (2) Split row via gossip isolation + CL ONE: b=2 written to cass-0 only, c=3 written to cass-1 only -> flush both. (3) Query SELECT * WHERE a=1 AND b=2 AND c=3 ALLOW FILTERING at CL ALL. FilterTree uses strict per-replica filtering because the index column returned only repaired matches while multiple non-indexed cols (b,c) need post-filtering; neither replica alone satisfies a AND b AND c, so the reconciled row is silently dropped.
verbatim_signature:
SAI intersection query "CONSISTENCY ALL; SELECT * FROM repro20189.partial_updates WHERE a = 1 AND b = 2 AND c = 3 ALLOW FILTERING" on buggy 5.0.3 returns:

 k | a | b | c
---+---+---+---


(0 rows)

— while the identical-CL PK read "SELECT * WHERE k = 0" returns "0 | 1 | 2 | 3" (1 rows). The (0 rows) is a dropped row, not an empty table.
evidence: REPRODUCED with verbatim buggy signature + cross-version A/B control. On buggy 5.0.3 at CL ALL: PK read WHERE k=0 returns (0,1,2,3) [1 row], but SAI intersection WHERE a=1 AND b=2 AND c=3 ALLOW FILTERING returns (0 rows) — a silent missing row = consistency violation. Preconditions verified: repairedAt non-zero on a=1 sstable on BOTH nodes (sstablemetadata: "Repaired at: 1781250656915"); physical split confirmed by sstabledump (NODE0: a=1,b=2 / NODE1: a=1,c=3). Predicate ladder rules out artifact and matches the strict-filtering mechanism exactly: a=1 -> 1 row; a=1 AND b=2 -> 1 row; a=1 AND c=3 -> 1 row; a=1 AND b=2 AND c=3 -> 0 rows (only MULTIPLE non-indexed split predicates trigger the drop, matching the JIRA title). node0 locally satisfies a&b, node1 locally satisfies a&c, neither satisfies a&b&c -> per-replica pre-reconciliation filtering drops it.
control: A/B control on cassandra:5.0.4 (fixed; 5.0.4 <= 5.0 ceiling 8). Identical 2-node ring/config/workload, identically verified (repairedAt non-zero: "Repaired at: 1781251120584"; split confirmed by sstabledump NODE0 a=1,b=2 / NODE1 a=1,c=3). On 5.0.4 the IDENTICAL filtered query at CL ALL returns "0 | 1 | 2 | 3" (1 rows) instead of (0 rows). Therefore the 5.0.3 (0 rows) is a dropped row, not an empty table: PK read returns (0,1,2,3) on both versions; only the buggy 5.0.3 SAI intersection path loses it.
notes: Bug fires only with the precise ordering: a=1 index match must be REPAIRED (repairedAt>0, requires incremental repair which is the 5.0.x nodetool repair default) while b/c stay UNREPAIRED, written AFTER the repair. hinted_handoff disabled to prevent the gossip-isolated peer from healing the split via hints. The in-JVM dtest testPartialUpdatesOnNonIndexedColumnsAfterRepair maps cleanly to a real ring: coordinator.execute(...CL.ALL) -> cqlsh CONSISTENCY ALL; executeInternal (node-local) -> disable peer gossip (poll until DN) + write at CL ONE through live node. Avoided a tag-anchored false 'blocked': the ring hint held and the dtest was reproducible on a real ring, not in-JVM-only.
tag_correction: none
tooling_findings: (1) cqlsh -e with multiple statements separated by newlines raised SyntaxException on a trailing empty statement but had ALREADY executed the preceding statements (CREATE TABLE/INDEX succeeded) — ran statements individually thereafter. (2) sstabledump/sstablemetadata are NOT on PATH in cassandra:5.0.x images; must use full path /opt/cassandra/tools/bin/. (3) nodetool repair defaults to incremental:true in this image (sets repairedAt), so no explicit -inc flag needed; verified via sstablemetadata. RECORD-ONLY: did not modify any tooling.

topology: single-node (one Cassandra pod in kind, namespace repro-14463). Tag hint said "ring" but the bug lives in the per-node StorageService.prepareForReplacement() startup guard and reproduces on a single pod, because the docker entrypoint makes a lone pod its own seed (CASSANDRA_SEEDS defaults to the pod's own broadcast IP).
trigger: Start a node with cassandra.replace_address set (JVM_EXTRA_OPTS=-Dcassandra.replace_address=10.255.255.254) while the node is in its OWN seed list (single-pod entrypoint default), with auto_bootstrap=true and initial_token unset. On 4.0.0 the seed+replace combination is NOT blocked at startup (it proceeds into replacement logic and, with a real dead-node IP, would skip streaming and generate a fresh random token set); on 4.0.1 the node refuses to start. A/B run with identical env, only the image tag differs.
verbatim_signature: INFO  [main] 2026-06-12 08:11:25,759 StorageService.java:528 - Gathering node replacement information for /10.255.255.254:7000
evidence: Discriminator is StorageService.prepareForReplacement(). BUGGY 4.0.0 log (lines 137-151) reaches "StorageService.java:528 - Gathering node replacement information for /10.255.255.254:7000" (passed the seed+replace guard), then dies downstream at prepareForReplacement(StorageService.java:533) only because the dummy target is not in gossip ("Cannot replace_address /10.255.255.254:7000 because it doesn't exist in gossip"); the consistency-guarantee guard string appears 0 times in the 4.0.0 log. CONTROL 4.0.1 log (lines 137-151), on byte-identical config, throws AT the guard prepareForReplacement(StorageService.java:522): "Exception (java.lang.RuntimeException) encountered during startup: Replacing a node without bootstrapping risks invalidating consistency guarantees as the expected data may not be present until repair is run. To perform this operation, please restart with -Dcassandra.allow_unsafe_replace=true" and "Gathering node replacement information" appears 0 times. Both pods' "Node configuration" line confirms the preconditions: auto_bootstrap=true; initial_token=null; seed_provider=SimpleSeedProvider{seeds=<own broadcast IP>}; JVM Arguments include -Dcassandra.replace_address=10.255.255.254. Source confirms the change: 4.0.0 guard is `if(!isAutoBootstrap() && !allow_unsafe_replace)` (not taken when auto_bootstrap=true) vs 4.0.1 guard `if(!shouldBootstrap() && !allow_unsafe_replace)` where shouldBootstrap()=isAutoBootstrap() && !bootstrapComplete() && !isSeed(), so a seed (isSeed()==true) is blocked. CHANGES.txt 4.0.1: "Don't allow seeds to replace without using unsafe (CASSANDRA-14463)".
control: A/B with fixed image cassandra:4.0.1 run with IDENTICAL env (same JVM_EXTRA_OPTS replace_address, same unset CASSANDRA_SEEDS so own-IP seed, same auto_bootstrap/initial_token defaults). 4.0.1 refuses to start at StorageService.prepareForReplacement(StorageService.java:522) with "Replacing a node without bootstrapping risks invalidating consistency guarantees as the expected data may not be present until repair is run. To perform this operation, please restart with -Dcassandra.allow_unsafe_replace=true" and never logs "Gathering node replacement information" (0 occurrences) — the exact inverse of 4.0.0. Only the image tag differed between runs.
notes: Bug = a missing/too-weak startup guard: a node that is its own seed + has replace_address set is not prevented from joining (and, per the Jira body, would generate brand-new random tokens instead of inheriting the dead node's tokens). Reproduced via the guard-bypass discriminator, which is precisely what the fix ("Don't allow seeds to replace without using unsafe") added. Used a dummy replace target IP (10.255.255.254, not in gossip) so the only variable under test is WHETHER the seed+replace guard fires; this is why 4.0.0 dies a few lines later than the guard rather than fully joining. Demonstrating the literal new-token ring join would need a 2-node ring with a dead node (target IP present in gossip), which is heavier and unnecessary since the guard-bypass IS the behavior the fix changes. Smallest possible footprint: two single-node pods, ephemeral storage, no keyspace/data written (the JVM fails during startup before serving CQL), so no unique keyspace was needed.
tag_correction: topology hint "ring" is wrong for reproduction purposes: the bug reproduces on a SINGLE node. The defect is in the per-node prepareForReplacement() startup guard gated only by (isSeed, auto_bootstrap, initial_token unset, replace_address set); a lone pod satisfies isSeed()==true because the docker entrypoint defaults CASSANDRA_SEEDS to the pod's own broadcast IP. confidence=M and trigger hint were otherwise accurate.
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling defect observed. Host-environment notes only (not fixed, per record-only): `gh` CLI and `java`/`javap` are not on PATH on this host; I worked around source inspection via raw.githubusercontent.com (WebFetch) and by reading CHANGES.txt out of the image with `docker cp`. The cassandra:* images are JREs (no javap), so bytecode decompile inside the image is not possible. None of this blocked the reproduction.

topology: 2-node ring inside kind (ns repro-14559): pod `seed` (cassandra:3.11.7, stays UP, does the conviction) + pod `target` (cassandra:3.11.7) whose container command is a `tail -f /dev/null` sleeper so cassandra is launched/killed as a process via the image entrypoint. The pod is never deleted, so its IP (10.244.1.141) is stable across in-pod process restarts — that IS the "replace with the same address" precondition. A/B control used pods seed8/target8 on cassandra:3.11.8 in the same namespace.
trigger: Follow the Jira body exactly on the target pod (stable IP). (1) target joins the ring normally -> UN. (2) pkill -9 cassandra; wait until seed shows DN. (3) wipe data+commitlog dir and relaunch with -Dcassandra.replace_address=<target's own IP> -> enters the same-address HIBERNATE replace path ("Writes will not be forwarded ... same address as the node to be replaced"). (4) pkill mid-bootstrap (before NORMAL) -> leaves a STATUS:hibernate,true gossip entry on the seed. (5) wipe data dir and relaunch WITHOUT the replace_address flag -> on 3.11.7 this is ALLOWED and begins a FRESH bootstrap with new tokens (no collision check). (6) pkill mid-bootstrap again. (7) ~30s later the seed convicts it as a FatClient and removes the endpoint+tokens from gossip. -Dcassandra.ring_delay_ms=60000 set only on target launches (runtime property, not a source edit) to widen the mid-bootstrap kill window; seed FatClient timer stays at default ~30s.
verbatim_signature: INFO  [GossipTasks:1] 2026-06-12 08:17:07,912 Gossiper.java:880 - FatClient /10.244.1.141 has been silent for 30000ms, removing from gossip
evidence: On buggy 3.11.7 the seed's /opt/cassandra/logs/system.log shows the full unsafe sequence for endpoint /10.244.1.141: "08:16:20 StorageService.java:2254 - Node /10.244.1.141 state jump to bootstrap" (the no-flag restart was permitted into a fresh bootstrap), then "08:16:56 ... is now DOWN", then the verbatim FatClient line at 08:17:07 removing it from gossip. After that, on the seed: nodetool status lists ONLY the seed (10.244.1.141 gone) and `nodetool gossipinfo | grep -c 10.244.1.141` returns 0 — i.e. the node AND its tokens were unsafely removed, exactly as the Jira describes. The fresh bootstrap was confirmed by "BootStrapper.java:228 - Generated random tokens" and the seed seeing a NEW Host ID (b5f462ce-... vs original 9ae376f2-...). The hibernate precondition was captured: seed gossipinfo for /10.244.1.141 showed "STATUS:2:hibernate,true". This identical FatClient line appears in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-14559.md.
control: A/B against the FIXED cassandra:3.11.8 (<= 3.11 ceiling 19). Ran the IDENTICAL dance (seed8/target8, target8 IP 10.244.1.143). Steps 1-4 behaved the same (UN join, DN, same-address hibernate replace, kill mid-bootstrap establishing the hibernate entry). At Step 5 (restart WITHOUT replace_address) the fixed version REFUSED to start at the new guard, literal from target8 /tmp/cS.log: "WARN [main] 2026-06-12 08:20:43,059 Gossiper.java:825 - A node with the same IP in hibernate status was detected. Was a replacement already attempted?" followed by "Exception (java.lang.RuntimeException) encountered during startup: A node with address /10.244.1.143 already exists, cancelling join. Use cassandra.replace_address if you want to replace this node. at org.apache.cassandra.service.StorageService.checkForEndpointCollision(StorageService.java:603) ~[apache-cassandra-3.11.8.jar:3.11.8]". Because the no-flag restart is rejected, the node never enters a fresh bootstrap; seed8's system.log shows NO "state jump to bootstrap" and NO "FatClient ... removing from gossip" line. The unsafe removal is impossible on 3.11.8 — clean contrast.
notes: Two non-obvious obstacles solved: (a) the replacing node must have an EMPTY data+commitlog dir or prepareForReplacement throws "Cannot replace address with a node that is already bootstrapped" (SystemKeyspace.bootstrapComplete()==true from the prior normal join) — this is NOT the bug, just a precondition; clearing /var/lib/cassandra/{data,commitlog,hints,saved_caches} fixed it. Note /opt/cassandra/data is a symlink to /var/lib/cassandra and cassandra.yaml dir lines are commented out (defaults used). (b) "mid-bootstrap" is easy to hit: the node sits in the RING_DELAY/pending-range sleep for ~60s (widened via ring_delay_ms), so killing ~10-18s after launch lands inside the window with an empty dataset. Data footprint was a few tens of KiB — no disk concern. Reproduced on first full pass once preconditions were right; well within budget (~3-4 deploy/test cycles total counting the control).
tag_correction: none — the classifier hint (topology=ring, trigger: replace_address same IP / stop mid-bootstrap / restart without flag / stop again -> FatClient removal after 30s) matched the Jira body exactly and reproduced as described.
tooling_findings: None specific to SREGym tooling. Minor environment quirks (recorded, not fixed): `bash -lc` over `kubectl exec` emits harmless "mesg: ttyname failed: Inappropriate ioctl for device" noise; `pkill -9 -f CassandraDaemon` returns rc=137 when it kills a live process (occasionally terminating the exec shell mid-command), so kill and subsequent fs operations were issued as separate exec calls. `pgrep -fc CassandraDaemon` self-matches the bash -lc argv and over-counts; verified liveness with `ps -eo pid,comm` instead (killed processes showed as <defunct> zombies reaped by their setsid parent, i.e. not actually running).

topology: 2-node ring in kind: cass-seed (3.11.9, reaches NORMAL) + cass-joiner (3.11.9, started with JVM_EXTRA_OPTS=-Dcassandra.write_survey=true and CASSANDRA_SEEDS=seed-IP). Two individual pods, NOT a uniform StatefulSet, so write_survey applies only to the joiner. Ephemeral storage, namespace repro-16146.
trigger: Park the joiner in the BOOT/JOINING precondition (write_survey=true completes streaming then halts: "Startup complete, but write survey mode is active, not becoming an active ring member"; tokens are saved so getLocalTokens() is non-empty). Then run `nodetool disablegossip` followed by `nodetool enablegossip` on the joiner. On 3.11.9 both succeed (no guard) and StorageService#setGossipTokens blindly flips the gossip STATUS from BOOT to NORMAL.
evidence: Buggy 3.11.9 joiner (10.244.2.123) BEFORE the dance: `nodetool netstats` -> `Mode: JOINING`; self gossipinfo -> `STATUS:24:BOOT,-2177934520239161152`; seed `nodetool status` shows the joiner as `UJ`. After `nodetool disablegossip` (exit 0) + `nodetool enablegossip` (exit 0): joiner self gossipinfo flips to `STATUS:87:NORMAL,-1077568207160367180` WHILE its own `nodetool netstats` still reports `Mode: JOINING`, and the seed's `nodetool status` flips the joiner from `UJ` to `UN`. The gossip-says-NORMAL vs node-says-JOINING juxtaposition, corroborated by the seed (so not a local display artifact), is the bug: a node that never finished joining now reads as a Normal ring member eligible for reads/writes. Joiner server log around the dance: "Stopping gossip by operator request" / "node hasn't joined, not announcing shutdown" / "Starting gossip by operator request".
control: A/B control on cassandra:3.11.10 (fix commit fee7a108, which adds an isNormal() guard to stopGossiping/startGossiping), identical 2-node write_survey ring. Joiner (10.244.2.126) reached the SAME precondition: `Mode: JOINING`, self `STATUS:32:BOOT,1572982465826127769`, seed shows `UJ`. Running `nodetool disablegossip` is REFUSED with exit code 1 and verbatim: "nodetool: Unable to stop gossip because the node is not in the normal state. Try to stop the node instead." After the refusal the joiner self STATUS is unchanged (`STATUS:32:BOOT,1572982465826127769`) and the seed still shows it as `UJ`. The fix prevents the operator from disabling gossip on a non-NORMAL node, so the BOOT->NORMAL flip can never occur. The sole A/B difference is the buggy 3.11.9 code path described in the Jira.
notes: The Jira describes a *crashed* bootstrap leaving the node in BOOT/JOINING; I instead parked the joiner in the identical state via write_survey=true. This is faithful (same operationMode=JOINING, gossip STATUS=BOOT) and more deterministic, and crucially keeps tokens saved — a fresh -Dcassandra.join_ring=false node would have NO saved tokens and would hit an AssertionError in getLocalTokens() instead of the NORMAL flip (wrong signature). Both buggy and control images were already cached locally (no build needed). One self-inflicted hiccup: a sed mishap once injected a malformed CASSANDRA_SEEDS value ("SEED_IP=10.244.2.124") causing the control joiner to crash with "The seed provider lists no seeds" — fixed by regenerating the YAML with the literal seed IP; not a Cassandra behavior.
tag_correction: none — classifier hint (topology=ring, trigger: bootstrap fails + disablegossip+enablegossip -> setGossipTokens blindly flips state to NORMAL) is accurate. Only refinement: used write_survey=true to reach the BOOT/JOINING precondition rather than a crashed bootstrap (cleaner, deterministic, tokens stay saved). Mechanism and observable outcome match the Jira body exactly.
tooling_findings: None. The single-pod template, multi-node guidance, and cached images all worked. Note for future runs: the StatefulSet multi-node template in the skill is not suitable for this bug because write_survey must apply only to the joiner (a uniform StatefulSet would put node-0 in write_survey too and it would never reach NORMAL); using two individual pods is the right call here.

trigger: Bring up a 2-node ring on cassandra:3.11.8 (cass-0 seed/survivor, cass-1 victim). nodetool decommission cass-1 so it transitions to LEFT and leaves the ring; then scale the StatefulSet to 1 replica so cass-1's pod and its port 7000 disappear (becomes unreachable). On the survivor enable DEBUG for org.apache.cassandra.net.OutboundTcpConnection and org.apache.cassandra.gms.Gossiper at runtime (nodetool setlogginglevel, no restart). The survivor keeps SELECTING the departed LEFT node for gossip via the unreachable-member path and repeatedly tries to connect to it, logging connection failures on the MessagingService-Outgoing-/<ip>-Gossip thread.
verbatim_signature:
DEBUG [MessagingService-Outgoing-/10.244.2.125-Gossip] 2026-06-12 08:46:00,222 OutboundTcpConnection.java:546 - Unable to connect to /10.244.2.125
java.net.ConnectException: Connection timed out
evidence: Buggy 3.11.8: victim cass-1 (10.244.2.125) decommissioned -> 'Removing tokens ... for /10.244.2.125' at 08:43:01, 'Convicting /10.244.2.125 with status LEFT - alive true' + 'InetAddress /10.244.2.125 is now DOWN' at 08:43:49, 'gossip quarantine over' at 08:44:01. nodetool status on survivor shows ONLY 10.244.1.146 (UN) - the victim has LEFT the ring; yet nodetool gossipinfo still lists '/10.244.2.125 ... STATUS:173:LEFT'. The survivor then repeatedly attempts to gossip-connect to the LEFT node AFTER conviction: 'Attempting to connect to /10.244.2.125' on the -Gossip thread at 08:43:50, 08:46:00, 08:48:11, each producing a connection failure: two distinct 'Unable to connect to /10.244.2.125' (java.net.ConnectException: Connection timed out) at 08:46:00,222 and 08:48:11,292 with full stack OutboundTcpConnection.connect(434) -> OutboundTcpConnectionPool.newSocket. Sustained ~2-min cadence (gated by the kind blackhole connect-timeout). Control 3.11.9 (identical decommission+scale-down sequence, victim 10.244.2.127, observed ~7 min after the 08:56:21 LEFT conviction): ZERO 'Attempting to connect' and ZERO 'Unable to connect' to the LEFT node after conviction (all 3 connect attempts at 08:53:08/08:53:11/08:55:34 are pre-conviction live-gossip and succeed with 'Done connecting'). The fix stops selecting the LEFT node for gossip.
control: A/B with released fix image cassandra:3.11.9 (fixVersions include 3.11.9; 9 <= 3.11 ceiling 19). Buggy ring deleted first to free disk, then an identical 2-node ring on 3.11.9 in the same namespace ran the identical decommission(cass-1)+enable-DEBUG+scale-to-1 sequence. Discriminator = the LEFT state: 3.11.8 keeps issuing gossip connect attempts to the LEFT node after conviction (08:43:50, 08:46:00, 08:48:11 -> repeated 'Unable to connect' timeouts), whereas 3.11.9 issues NONE after the node is convicted LEFT (observed ~7 min, 0 attempts, 0 failures). Both versions retain the LEFT node in gossipinfo with normal expiry; only 3.11.8 selects it for gossip. Confirms the CASSANDRA-16156 fix.
notes: Used a 2-node ephemeral-storage ring (no PVC), no user data written (decommission of an empty ring streams instantly) - minimal disk footprint on the shared host (stayed at ~80% / 13G free). The bug's wasted gossip-connect attempts to the LEFT node happen regardless of log level, but in 3.11.8 they are logged at DEBUG (the 4.0 NoSpamLogger INFO line in the Jira does not exist in the 3.11 messaging stack), so an operator at the default INFO level sees nothing in system.log - the spam is visible in debug.log / after nodetool setlogginglevel. This does not weaken 'reproduced': the defective behavior (survivor repeatedly connecting to a node that has LEFT) is directly observed with timestamps and a verbatim ConnectException, plus the gossipinfo STATUS:LEFT proof and the 3.11.9 control. Only my own namespace repro-16156 was touched; other concurrent namespaces (repro-15899/15902/15924, cass-*, k8ssandra-operator, cert-manager) were left untouched.
tag_correction: Mechanism/trigger CONFIRMED (decommission -> peer still gossips to the LEFT node via the unreachable-member path; topology=ring confirmed). Three literal corrections, none changing the bug: (1) The Jira's verbatim log is from a 4.0 in-JVM dtest using the NEW netty messaging stack (NoSpamLogger 'failed to connect', io.netty AnnotatedConnectException, URGENT_MESSAGES, loopback :7012). On the actual buggy image 3.11.8 the same bug surfaces through the OLD OutboundTcpConnection stack: thread 'MessagingService-Outgoing-/<ip>-Gossip', message 'Unable to connect to /<ip>', port 7000, real pod IPs. (2) In the kind overlay the departed pod IP is blackholed, so the OS error is 'java.net.ConnectException: Connection timed out' rather than the hint's 'Connection refused' - same code path (OutboundTcpConnection.connect -> OutboundTcpConnectionPool.newSocket), downstream socket error differs only. (3) Spam is at DEBUG in 3.11 (not INFO); surfaced at runtime via nodetool setlogginglevel without restart.
tooling_findings: None. The provided multi-node ring template (StatefulSet + headless Service, OrderedReady, ephemeral storage) worked as-is for both the buggy and control deployments. The readinessProbe-based rollout, nodetool decommission, and nodetool setlogginglevel runtime DEBUG toggling all behaved as documented. No SREGym tooling issues observed.

topology: 2-node ring (StatefulSet, kind), RF=1 SimpleStrategy keyspace. cass-0 owns 51.2%, cass-1 owns 48.8%. Buggy image cassandra:4.1.0; A/B control on cassandra:4.1.1. Stream throttled to 1 Mb/s; data fattened to ~9 MB/node with incompressible random payloads to widen the destructive window.
trigger: Run `nodetool cleanup` on the SURVIVING receiver node while a peer is decommissioning (its ranges are streaming in and are pending on the survivor). Decommission needed `--force` (system_distributed RF=3 with N=2). Throttle the stream + loop cleanup on cass-0 to land cleanup while netstats shows the node Receiving streamed files. On 4.1.0 cleanup proceeds (no pending-range guard) and silently deletes the streamed data.
verbatim_signature: BUGGY 4.1.0: cqlsh "CONSISTENCY ONE; SELECT COUNT(*) FROM repro16418.t;" returned `count 3375` (durable, single-node ring after scale-down) where baseline was `count 7000` — i.e. ~3625 rows silently deleted by `nodetool cleanup` (EXIT=0) while `nodetool netstats` on the same node showed `Receiving 28 files, 9122082 bytes total. Already received 2 files (7.14%), 28055 bytes total (0.31%)`.
evidence: /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-16418.md
control: FIXED 4.1.1, identical 2-node RF=1 ring + identical decommission/cleanup workload: at the moment cass-1 was UL (Leaving) with pending ranges live on cass-0, `nodetool cleanup repro16418` was REJECTED with `error: Node is involved in cluster membership changes. Not safe to run cleanup.` / `java.lang.RuntimeException: Node is involved in cluster membership changes. Not safe to run cleanup. at org.apache.cassandra.service.StorageService.forceKeyspaceCleanup(StorageService.java:3810)` (exit code 2). No data deleted. This is exactly the guard added by fix commit 8bb9c72; on 4.1.0 the guard is absent so the same call succeeds and deletes streamed data. Clean A/B contrast.
notes: Mechanism = cleanup ignores pending ranges. Reproduced the VERIFIED scenario from the Jira body (decommission), running cleanup on the surviving receiver (which is isJoined()==true and so bypasses 4.1.0's old isJoined() guard in CompactionManager.performCleanup — exactly the hole the fix closes). Bootstrap symmetry was explicitly unverified by the reporter and likely no-op'd by that old guard on a JOINING node, so I did not pursue it. Enablers that mattered: (1) RF=1 so deleted data has no replica to mask the loss; (2) incompressible random payloads — repeated-char payloads compressed to ~0 under LZ4 giving only ~2s stream window (too short); ~9MB/node random gave ~70s at 1 Mb/s; (3) loop cleanup on cass-0 during the background decommission and confirm via netstats the node is Receiving before counting; (4) scale StatefulSet to replicas=1 after the destructive cleanup so the recreated pod can't re-bootstrap and re-stream the data back. Total data footprint ~18MB, well within disk budget.
tag_correction: Classifier hint (topology=ring, trigger "cleanup concurrent with bootstrap/decommission -> streamed data silently deleted") is essentially correct. Refinement: only the DECOMMISSION half is verified/reproducible on the buggy image; the BOOTSTRAP half was explicitly unverified by the reporter and is likely shadowed on 4.1.0 by the pre-existing isJoined() guard for a JOINING node. Reproduced via decommission with cleanup on the surviving receiver. Confidence M -> reproduced.
tooling_findings: No SREGym tooling was modified. Two reproduction caveats relevant to any automated oracle for this bug: (1) A StatefulSet auto-recreates a decommissioned pod (wants N replicas); the recreated pod re-bootstraps and can stream the deleted data back, masking the loss — you must pin replicas down (kubectl scale --replicas=1) right after the destructive cleanup to observe durable loss. (2) A normal decommission at N=2 is blocked by system_distributed RF=3 ("Not enough live nodes to maintain replication factor in keyspace system_distributed (RF = 3, N = 2)"); `nodetool decommission --force` is required. Both are expected Cassandra/k8s behaviors, not bugs.

topology: 3-node single-token ring (num_tokens=1), RF=2 SimpleStrategy keyspace, ephemeral storage, in kind (StatefulSet cass-0/1/2, terminationGracePeriodSeconds=90)
trigger: On a single-token ring, run `nodetool move <token>` on cass-2 (target token lands in another node's range so peers compute pending ranges), poll until cass-2 = UM (Up/Moving), then gracefully shut down cass-2 mid-move via `kubectl scale --replicas=2`. The graceful shutdown announces gossip STATUS shutdown,true but Gossiper.markAsShutdown() in 4.0.0 omits subscriber.onChange(), so TokenMetadata never clears cass-2's MOVING status -> peers keep cass-2 as DM (Down+Moving) with phantom pending ranges -> bogus UnavailableException to clients.
verbatim_signature: <stdin>:1:NoHostAvailable: ('Unable to complete the operation against any hosts', {<Host: 127.0.0.1:9042 dc1>: Unavailable('Error from server: code=1000 [Unavailable exception] message="Cannot achieve consistency level QUORUM" info={'consistency': 'QUORUM', 'required_replicas': 3, 'alive_replicas': 2}')})
evidence:
Two independent buggy signatures, both in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-16796.md.

(1) PERSISTENT mechanism: cass-2 announced graceful SHUTDOWN in gossip but its MOVING status never cleared from peers' TokenMetadata.
 - cass-0 gossipinfo for cass-2: `STATUS_WITH_PORT:410:shutdown,true` (graceful shutdown, not a hard crash).
 - cass-0 `nodetool status repro16796` shows cass-2 as `DM  10.244.2.132 ... ` = Down + still Moving. In a fixed build (>=4.0.1) markAsShutdown() fires onChange and the node would read DN with pending ranges cleared. DM-after-shutdown,true is the buggy state.
 - cass-0 system.log: only `InetAddress /10.244.2.132:7000 is now DOWN` — cass-2 was never "removed"/"state normal", so the moving status sticks.

(2) CLIENT-VISIBLE symptom (Jira's named symptom): QUORUM write sweep over 30 keys (RF=2 so QUORUM=2), coordinator cass-0, both peers UP. id=21 failed with `required_replicas': 3, 'alive_replicas': 2` — impossible for RF=2. `nodetool getendpoints repro16796 t 21` = {10.244.1.156 cass-0 UP, 10.244.2.132 cass-2 DOWN}; required:3 means the coordinator added a PHANTOM PENDING replica (cass-1) due to cass-2's stale MOVING state, inflating blockForWrite 2->3 = bogus UnavailableException while 2 nodes are up. Contrast: id=1/id=28 failed with required:2/alive:1 which is LEGITIMATE (cass-2 is a natural replica there and is down), confirming required:3 is the discriminating impossible-for-RF=2 signature.
control: Within-version + fix-commit reasoning (no fixed-image A/B run, to stay within budget — the required:3/alive:2 signature is self-proving). Fix commit fbb20b9162b73c4de8a82cf4ffdde3304e904603 (fixVersions 3.0.25/3.11.11/4.0.1) changes only Gossiper.markAsShutdown() to additionally call subscriber.onChange(endpoint, ApplicationState.STATUS, shutdown). In 4.0.0 that notification is absent, so TokenMetadata is never told the moving node shut down and its MOVING status + pending ranges persist (-> DM, -> phantom pending replica). On cassandra:4.0.1 the identical move+graceful-shutdown sequence would deliver the STATUS change, clear cass-2's MOVING status, and cass-2 would read DN (not DM) with the inflated replica set gone. cassandra:4.0.1 image exists and 4.0.1 <= 4.0 ceiling (20), so a deterministic DN-vs-DM A/B is feasible but was not run to conserve the deploy budget (two deploys already spent: vnode->single-token redeploy).
notes: Mechanism cleanly reproduced on a REAL ring (not in-JVM-only). The PERSISTENT/deterministic evidence is the DM (Down+Moving) state after gossip shutdown,true — it never cleared on its own (matches Jira "in practice until the peer is next bounced"). The bogus client UnavailableException (required:3/alive:2) is RACE-Y/TRANSIENT, exactly as the Jira states ("peers can SOMETIMES maintain pending ranges"): it was emitted verbatim by the server on the first QUORUM sweep, but later sweeps succeeded after a disablegossip/enablegossip I ran on cass-1 triggered a PendingRangeCalculator rerun that recomputed that particular range mapping away (the DM movingEndpoints entry persisted). I did not re-trigger further to avoid thrash. Move window was widened with `nodetool setstreamthroughput 1` on all nodes; cass-2 caught at UM, then graceful shutdown via scale-down. No bulk data (30 small rows). NoHostAvailable {} (empty dict) seen on some retries is an unrelated cqlsh local control-connection hiccup, not the bug.
tag_correction: Classifier hints were essentially correct: topology=ring is right, and the trigger (MOVE + graceful shutdown of moving node -> stale pending ranges -> bogus unavailable) matches the Jira body and reproduced. One important refinement: the ring MUST be single-token (num_tokens=1). With the Docker image's default vnodes (16 tokens) `nodetool move` is rejected outright ("This node has more than one token and cannot be moved thusly." / UnsupportedOperationException at StorageService.move). Confidence was M; reproduced with a verbatim self-proving signature.
tooling_findings: None specific to SREGym tooling. Operational notes for this host: (1) cassandra:4.0.0 Docker image ships a JRE only (no javac; no jconsole/jmxterm), so reading the StorageServiceMBean getPendingRangeToEndpointMap directly was not possible in-pod — I relied on nodetool status (DM state) and the client-visible UnavailableException instead. The host itself also has no JDK. (2) The default image uses vnodes; reproducing MOVE bugs requires setting CASSANDRA_NUM_TOKENS=1. (3) JMX is LOCAL_JMX=yes (127.0.0.1:7199), so any JMX reader must run inside the pod.

topology: 2 plain Cassandra pods (cassandra:4.1.1) pinned to kind-worker3, each fronted by its own headless-equivalent ClusterIP Service (svc-0=seed, svc-1) so broadcast_address is a STABLE ClusterIP while listen_address rides the changing pod IP. prefer_local=true in cassandra-rackdc.properties, GossipingPropertyFileSnitch. No PVC (ephemeral). Namespace repro-16718. A/B control = identical topology on cassandra:4.1.2.
trigger: On a UN/UN ring with prefer_local=true and internal(listen)!=broadcast: the seed caches the peer's pod IP as preferred_ip in system.peers. Delete cass-1 and recreate it -> it returns with a NEW pod IP (changed listen_address) but the SAME broadcast ClusterIP (same gossip endpoint key), so the seed retains the STALE preferred_ip (old, dead pod IP). cass-1's startup shadow round then fails because the seed mis-routes its gossip reply to that stale internal address.
verbatim_signature:
java.lang.RuntimeException: Unable to gossip with any peers
	at org.apache.cassandra.gms.Gossiper.doShadowRound(Gossiper.java:1916)
	at org.apache.cassandra.service.StorageService.checkForEndpointCollision(StorageService.java:694)
evidence: BUGGY (4.1.1): ring UN/UN with cass-1 broadcast=10.96.227.137(ClusterIP) listen=10.244.1.158(pod IP); seed system.peers preferred_ip=10.244.1.158 (non-null = the corruptible pointer). Deleted cass-1 -> recreated at NEW pod IP 10.244.1.159, same broadcast ClusterIP; seed still showed preferred_ip=10.244.1.158 (stale/dead). cass-1 went CrashLoopBackOff (restartCount 4+), seed nodetool status showed it DN. --previous log: 'Exception (java.lang.RuntimeException) encountered during startup: Unable to gossip with any peers' at org.apache.cassandra.gms.Gossiper.doShadowRound(Gossiper.java:1916) <- dies INSIDE the shadow round. Corroborating Jira 'response never received': OutboundConnection log shows cass-1's new pod IP successfully CONNECTED to the seed (/10.96.227.137:7000(/10.244.1.159:45234)->/10.96.195.147:7000 successfully connected) yet the shadow round still timed out -> reply was mis-routed to the dead old internal IP. All commands+raw output in /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-16718.md sections 3-4.
control: A/B on fixed image cassandra:4.1.2 under BYTE-IDENTICAL topology/config/trigger (same prefer_local, same per-pod ClusterIP broadcast, same stale preferred_ip after delete: peer 10.96.222.151 / preferred_ip 10.244.1.161 while cass-1 moved to new pod IP 10.244.1.162). 4.1.2 did NOT rejoin cleanly either -- it also crash-looped -- BUT with a DIFFERENT exception at a LATER stage: 'A node with address /10.96.222.151:7000 already exists, cancelling join. Use cassandra.replace_address...' at StorageService.checkForEndpointCollision(StorageService.java:784). This is the discriminator: checkForEndpointCollision calls doShadowRound FIRST; the line-784 collision throw is only reachable if doShadowRound RETURNED the seed's ring state. So 4.1.2's gossip shadow round SUCCEEDED (bug absent) and it then stopped at the standard, expected replace_address guard (an artifact of my fresh-join-reusing-a-dead-endpoint vehicle, not a regression). 4.1.1 dies INSIDE doShadowRound (Gossiper.java:1916); 4.1.2 passes it and dies after (StorageService.java:784). Same plumbing both versions => the plumbing confound is ruled out; only the version differs and only the fixed version's gossip works. Matches fix commit b791644 (ReconnectableSnitchHelper.onDead purges stale INTERNAL_ADDRESS_AND_PORT; OutboundConnectionSettings resolves via Gossiper.getInternalAddressAndPort).
notes: Reproduction vehicle differs slightly from the reporter's: the reporter restarted an EXISTING node with a changed listen_address; I used a FRESH join reusing a dead node's stable broadcast ClusterIP (new host_id), which is why the FIXED version additionally trips the replace_address endpoint-collision guard at line 784. The ROOT defect exercised is identical -- under prefer_local the seed mis-routes the gossip shadow-round reply to the stale INTERNAL_ADDRESS_AND_PORT for that broadcast endpoint -- and the fix is the same (b791644). A first attempt with a flat StatefulSet+PVC (listen==broadcast, system.peers preferred_ip=null) rejoined cleanly and did NOT reproduce: that topology has no distinct internal address to corrupt. The per-pod ClusterIP staging (stable broadcast, changing listen) makes preferred_ip non-null and is what exercises the bug. Buggy binary's literal message is 'Unable to gossip with any peers' (the Jira reporter paraphrased it as 'any seeds'); identical code path Gossiper.doShadowRound.
tag_correction: Hint trigger (enable prefer_local + change listen_address + restart -> 'Unable to gossip with any seeds') is accurate to the Jira body and DID reproduce on the buggy image. Refinements: (1) it only fires when the node has a STABLE broadcast identity DISTINCT from its changing listen address (preferred_ip non-null) -- a naive flat ring where listen==broadcast does NOT reproduce; this is a staging requirement, not a topology-tag error (hint topology=ring is correct). (2) the buggy binary's exact message is 'Unable to gossip with any peers', not 'seeds'. Confidence M was appropriate -- it needed the right networking model to surface.
tooling_findings: `kind load docker-image cassandra:4.1.1` and `cassandra:4.1.2` both failed with `ctr: content digest sha256:... not found` (multi-arch manifest import incompatibility in this kind/containerd combination). Worked around by pulling directly into the node's containerd: `docker exec kind-worker3 crictl pull cassandra:4.1.x` (exit 0). Pre-existing repro-15899/cass-0 was sitting in ErrImageNeverPull (a different session's namespace) -- not touched. RECORD-ONLY: nothing fixed.

topology: single-node (single pod cassandra:4.0.2 in kind ns repro-17342; a ring was not needed to establish the blocking facts)
trigger: Many vnodes (16) x heavy range-splitting (100 ranges/vnode) x 22 keyspaces x RF3 produce ~8100 incremental-repair-session rows in system.repairs; on restart, RepairState.add() re-sorts the full range list on every range added (super-linear), so loading them takes 30+ minutes. Trigger volume = total ranges across sessions, not row count.
verbatim_signature: NONE — this bug has no client/server error signature. It is pure startup wall-time latency: the repairs table is processed silently at boot (log: "Initializing system.repairs" ... "1 ops" ... "Truncate of system.repairs is complete") with no exception/ERROR/WARN. A verbatim buggy signature does not exist, so 'reproduced' is structurally unclaimable.
evidence: On cassandra:4.0.2 (num_tokens=16): (1) Startup log shows system.repairs is loaded/truncated SILENTLY with zero rows and no exception/ERROR — confirms the effect is latency, not an error path. grep -iE "exception|error|WARN.*repair" over the full startup log returned no repair-related error. (2) Schema confirms `ranges set<blob>` is the per-session range list (serialized Range<Token>) that RepairState.add() sorts. (3) Clean re-test (no truncate between): SELECT count -> 0; INSERT INTO system.repairs(parent_id,state) VALUES(now(),1) -> accepted and PERSISTS, count -> 1, but ranges=null. A row with empty ranges never exercises the O(n^2) sort, so hand-seeding rows does not reach the buggy path. Reaching the trigger needs ~1600 valid range blobs/session across thousands of sessions — only producible via hundreds of real nodetool repair -inc runs on a live ring (un-stageable in budget) or via the JVM unit test the reporter attached. Full log: /tmp/repro-CASSANDRA-17342.md
control: A/B is moot. Fix shipped in 4.0.3 (4.0.2 patch+1 = 4.0.3 <= 4.0 ceiling 20), so cassandra:4.0.3 exists, but with no error signature and no budget-feasible way to reach the trigger range-volume there is nothing to diff at any stageable scale. The fix (collect rows in LocalSessions and process as a group, avoiding the per-add resort in RepairState) is an internal startup-latency optimization observable only as wall-clock boot time at production scale.
notes: Disposition needs-fix-test: the reporter explicitly states the bug is "demonstrated in the attached unit test" exercising RepairState.add(); that JVM unit test is the only budget-feasible reproducer and needs a from-source 4.0.2 build (the prebuilt image ships no test harness), out of budget. NOTE the bug IS genuinely operator-visible at production scale (a real operator hit 30-min restarts), so the unit test is the only FEASIBLE reproducer here, not literally the only one — confirmed-blocked (production-scale repair-session generation un-stageable + no error signature) would be equally defensible. Self-correction: an initial reading that CQL writes to system.repairs were "silently dropped" was WRONG (artifact of running TRUNCATE before the count); a clean re-test showed the INSERT persists. No conclusion in the log or this result rests on that retracted claim. This is CPU/time-to-generate bound, NOT disk-bound (rows are sub-KiB), so it is not blocked-disk-constrained.
tag_correction: Hint topology=ring/confidence=M/trigger=restart-30min: the MECHANISM is accurate, but the reproducer is a JVM unit test (per reporter) or production-scale generation of thousands of incremental-repair sessions — not a live-ring symptom stageable in budget — and it yields NO error/exception/wrong-result, only startup latency. A single pod was sufficient to establish the blocking facts; a ring was not needed. Effective disposition is needs-fix-test (a JVM-unit-test reproducer), not a ring repro.
tooling_findings: None. Existing kind cluster and cassandra:4.0.2 image worked as expected; cqlsh and logs accessible. No SREGym tooling defects observed during this attempt.
